items.title: How long-term dietary cholesterol can slow down its own clearance by liver cells
items.summary: The paper investigated how prolonged exposure to dietary cholesterol affects the liver's ability to clear LDL cholesterol from the bloodstream. The researchers focused on the low-density lipoprotein receptor (LDLR), which liver cells use to remove LDL cholesterol, and examined how sustained high cholesterol conditions alter its regulation. They discovered a cell-signalling mechanism whereby elevated cholesterol triggers enzyme-mediated degradation of LDLR, causing the receptor to be internalized and broken down, thereby reducing the liver's cholesterol-clearing capacity. Critically, blocking the relevant enzyme was found to restore LDLR levels in liver cells, pointing to a potential therapeutic strategy for managing high cholesterol.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it uncovers a cell-signalling mechanism by which chronically elevated dietary cholesterol accelerates its own accumulation—by triggering enzymatic degradation of the very liver receptor responsible for clearing it from the blood. The finding is clinically significant because blocking this enzyme was shown to restore receptor levels, pointing toward a potential new therapeutic approach for high cholesterol and heart disease.
items.citation: How long-term dietary cholesterol can slow down its own clearance by liver cells. Nature. 2026

items.title: The mutational landscape of STING-induced immunity
items.summary: This paper investigated how the amino acid sequence of STING, a key immune signalling protein involved in host defence, cancer, senescence, and inflammation, determines its functional activity. The researchers developed a massively parallel mutagenesis assay to systematically profile thousands of single amino acid variants of STING, mapping how individual substitutions affect its immunostimulatory capacity and its ability to produce distinct signalling outputs such as type I interferon induction versus non-canonical autophagy. Cryo-electron microscopy structures of selected hyperactive variants revealed new regulatory principles governing the conformational transition from inactive to signalling-competent states. The study found that mutational effects are widespread across the functional landscape, with certain substitutions sensitising STING to its natural ligand or decoupling different downstream signalling pathways, demonstrating that diverse functional responses can be achieved through single point changes. The findings also illuminate the clinical and evolutionary significance of naturally occurring STING variants and collectively define the molecular principles that tune STING activity across immune contexts.
items.why_notable: This work draws attention because a large-scale systematic mapping of how mutations affect the STING signalling protein reveals the molecular rules governing its activity, offering a comprehensive resource for understanding and potentially tuning immune responses.
items.citation: Bing Zhang, Pengbiao Xu, Yu Meng et al.. The mutational landscape of STING-induced immunity. Nature. 2026

The review frames this integrated approach as essential for tackling major societal challenges around food security and hidden hunger.
items.citation: Dominique Van Der Straeten, Mustafa Bulut, Da Cao et al.. Genetic technologies to enhance crop nutritional value under climate change. Nature. 2026

items.citation: Xue Feng, Shuo Zhang, Yuqi Wang et al.. Dietary cholesterol activates a Ral-dependent pathway driving LDLR turnover. Nature. 2026

items.title: Disparate privacy risks from medical AI
items.summary: This paper investigated privacy risks in medical artificial intelligence models used for diagnostic applications, focusing on whether individual patient data could be identified as part of a model's training set. The researchers conducted patient-level privacy audits using membership inference attacks across a diverse range of medical datasets, examining attack success at the level of individual patients rather than in aggregate across all records. They found that these attacks could achieve near-perfect success rates against specific individuals even when aggregate measures suggested performance barely exceeding random chance, demonstrating that population-level privacy metrics can dramatically underestimate the risk faced by particular patients. The study also revealed that privacy risk scales with model capacity and falls disproportionately on underrepresented groups defined by disease status, race, insurance type, sex, or imaging protocol. The authors conclude that current aggregate privacy evaluation frameworks are insufficient and call for the development of risk assessment and mitigation approaches that account for the heterogeneous privacy risks experienced by all patients contributing data to these systems.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals that AI models used for medical diagnostics carry privacy risks, specifically that they can be exploited through membership inference attacks to expose sensitive information about individuals whose data was used in training—and that these risks are not evenly distributed across populations.
items.citation: Moritz Knolle, Martin J. Menten, Friederike Jungmann et al.. Disparate privacy risks from medical AI. Nature. 2026

items.title: Small-molecule modulation of β-arrestins
items.summary: This study addressed a longstanding gap in GPCR pharmacology by developing direct chemical tools to modulate β-arrestins, which are key signal transducers that had previously lacked selective small-molecule inhibitors. The researchers identified small-molecule inhibitors of β-arrestins and characterized their mechanism using pharmacological, biochemical, biophysical, and structural approaches including cryo-electron microscopy, molecular dynamics simulations, and mutagenesis. The inhibitors were found to disrupt β-arrestin engagement with activated GPCRs, impairing receptor desensitization, internalization, and β-arrestin-dependent signaling while leaving G protein–receptor coupling intact. Structural analysis revealed that one compound stabilizes a conformation of β-arrestin1 by binding a pocket within its central crest, formed by the middle, C, and lariat loops, which constitutes a critical receptor-binding interface and represents a novel allosteric site. These findings provide a mechanistic framework for β-arrestin modulation and offer a new strategy for designing pathway-selective GPCR therapeutics that target transducers rather than the receptor itself.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it reveals the molecular mechanism by which small molecules can block β-arrestin engagement with activated GPCRs, while also uncovering a previously unrecognized allosteric regulatory site on β-arrestins that could be exploited for drug development.
items.citation: Alem W. Kahsai, Natalia Pakharukova, Henry Y. Kwon et al.. Small-molecule modulation of β-arrestins. Nature. 2026

items.title: Immunological mechanisms of mRNA vaccines for infectious diseases
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it synthesizes how the individual components of mRNA–lipid-nanoparticle vaccines can be deliberately adjusted to shape immune responses, offering a roadmap for tackling pathogens that have proven difficult to target with existing approaches.
items.citation: Michela Locci, Norbert Pardi. Immunological mechanisms of mRNA vaccines for infectious diseases. Nature. 2026

The advance suggests a practical path to cryogen-free thermal management in nanoscale systems.
items.citation: Jiamin Lin, Baixu Xiang, Renguang Liu et al.. Optical cooling by interfacial charge transfer in 2D heterostructures. Nature. 2026

items.title: Detection of anisotropic cosmic structures on a gigaparsec scale
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it presents evidence for enormous coherent structures in the Universe that stretch across gigaparsec scales, directly challenging the long-held cosmological assumption that matter distribution becomes statistically uniform and isotropic at sufficiently large scales.
items.citation: Francesco Sylos Labini, Marco Galoppo. Detection of anisotropic cosmic structures on a gigaparsec scale. Nature. 2026

items.title: An ECG biomarker for sudden cardiac death discovered with deep learning
items.summary: This paper investigated whether deep learning applied to electrocardiogram data could identify patients at high risk of sudden cardiac death more effectively than the currently dominant clinical biomarker, left ventricular ejection fraction. The researchers trained a deep learning model on a large dataset linking all ECGs from a Swedish region to death certificates, then externally validated it in both a United States health system and a Taiwanese hospital registry. The model successfully identified a high-risk group with a substantially higher annual rate of sudden cardiac death than patients flagged by reduced ejection fraction, and the vast majority of the model's high-risk patients were not captured by the existing biomarker. To interpret what the model had learned, the authors paired it with a generative ECG model, revealing a previously undescribed waveform morphology that robustly predicts sudden cardiac death and is visually apparent. Using electrophysiological first principles, the authors then propose and preliminarily test a new mechanistic hypothesis about the origins of sudden cardiac death based on this discovered biomarker.
items.why_notable: A deep-learning approach applied to ECG waveforms has uncovered a biomarker that can predict sudden cardiac death more accurately than existing clinical methods. The finding is notable because the biomarker is described as easily visible, suggesting it could be practically useful in clinical settings.
items.citation: Ziad Obermeyer, Alexander Schubert, James Ross et al.. An ECG biomarker for sudden cardiac death discovered with deep learning. Nature. 2026

items.title: Epiblast diversification and blood formation in a human pregastrula
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it maps, at high resolution, the spatial gene-expression landscape of a human embryo just before gastrulation, revealing three distinct cell fate trajectories from the epiblast and demonstrating that blood cell formation begins even earlier than previously understood—and from an unexpected tissue of origin.
items.citation: Zhenyu Xiao, Yandong Gong, Xiaomin Yang et al.. Epiblast diversification and blood formation in a human pregastrula. Nature. 2026

items.title: Fourier pixels for bidirectional light control
items.summary: This paper addresses the fundamental limitation that conventional optical pixels can only detect or emit light intensity, lacking the ability to fully control and sense the amplitude, phase, and polarization of optical wavefronts simultaneously. The researchers developed miniaturized diffractive elements based on Fourier optics, exploiting plasmonic surface waves that propagate coherently across metallic surfaces and interact with specially designed wavy microstructures to generate or characterize arbitrary optical wavefronts. By directing plasmons toward microstructures shaped through Fourier analysis, the platform can both generate background-free optical wavefronts and fully characterize incoming light fields. Combining multiple such components yields multifunctional "Fourier pixels" capable of bidirectional, vectorial control over the complete optical field, an approach the authors extend to photonic waveguide modes. The resulting architecture is claimed to be scalable and universally applicable to fields such as adaptive optics, holographic displays, optical communication, and quantum information processing.
items.why_notable: This work draws attention because it introduces a miniaturized platform capable of both controlling and sensing the full set of optical wavefront properties—amplitude, phase, and polarization—within individual pixels, enabling a broad range of advanced photonic functions in a single versatile device.
items.citation: Yannik M. Glauser, Sander J. W. Vonk, David B. Seda et al.. Fourier pixels for bidirectional light control. Nature. 2026

items.title: Genetic diversity of late Neanderthals in northwestern Europe
items.summary: This study investigated the population structure and genetic diversity of late Neanderthals in northwestern Europe by generating genetic data from a large number of individuals recovered from multiple archaeological sites in Belgium and France, including a high-coverage genome from a well-preserved specimen. The researchers found that most of these individuals were more closely related to each other than to other contemporaneous Neanderthals elsewhere in Europe, indicating a degree of regional population cohesion, while some also carried ancestry from an older Neanderthal lineage predating the divergence of late Neanderthals. Unlike Neanderthals from Altai, these northwestern European individuals showed no evidence of close-relative mating, suggesting they lived in larger or better-connected social groups. Despite temporal overlap with early modern humans in the region, no detectable gene flow from modern humans into this Neanderthal population was found. Finally, the absence of increasing genetic load over time argues against the hypothesis that progressive genetic deterioration contributed to Neanderthal extinction.
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it offers a new window into the genetic diversity of late Neanderthals in northwestern Europe at a critical period near their extinction, revealing insights that were previously inaccessible. It also demonstrates that even fragmentary genomic data can meaningfully refine understanding of variation within Neanderthal populations.
items.citation: Alba Bossoms Mesa, Elena Essel, Stéphane Peyrégne et al.. Genetic diversity of late Neanderthals in northwestern Europe. Nature. 2026

items.title: Crude oil fractionation by means of mesoporous polyacrylonitrile membranes
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it demonstrates that a common membrane material, typically used only as a non-selective support, can instead achieve effective molecular-level separation of crude oil, offering a potentially transformative alternative to energy-intensive distillation with substantially lower emissions and resource consumption.
items.citation: Jihoon Choi, Hyeokjun Seo, Minyong Lee et al.. Crude oil fractionation by means of mesoporous polyacrylonitrile membranes. Nature. 2026

items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it presents the first observational detection of a gravitational wave signal directly tied to the remnant black hole's horizon following a merger, opening a new way to empirically measure exotic strong-gravity phenomena such as frame-dragging in the ergosphere and horizon surface gravity.
items.citation: N. Lu, Sizheng Ma, O. J. Piccinni et al.. GW250114 reveals signatures of post-merger black-hole horizon. Nature. 2026

items.title: Chiral laser gyroscopes breaking the lock-in limit
items.summary: The abstract provided is too thin to summarize, as it contains only a fragment of the conclusions and lacks information about the study's background, methods, and full findings.
items.why_notable: The work draws attention because it solves a longstanding fundamental limitation of ring laser gyroscopes—the lock-in effect that prevents accurate sensing of very slow rotations—by exploiting spontaneous symmetry breaking rather than added external components. This enables precise near-zero rotation measurement while also opening a path toward smaller, simpler devices.
items.citation: Yuanhao Mao, Jipeng Xu, Hong-Teng Ji et al.. Chiral laser gyroscopes breaking the lock-in limit. Nature. 2026

items.title: Zero-shot design of drug-binding proteins via neural iterative selection−expansion
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of designing proteins from scratch that bind specific small-molecule drugs, a problem where existing deep-learning approaches have previously fallen short of zero-shot success. The researchers developed a graph neural network called LASErMPNN that designs protein sequences compatible with a given backbone and docked ligand, and paired it with a structure prediction network that models protein-ligand complexes from sequence information alone. By iterating these two networks in a closed loop—a strategy termed neural iterative selection-expansion (NISE)—they optimized the compatibility between protein sequence, structure, and ligand, outperforming physics-based design approaches. Applying NISE to two chemically distinct drug molecules, the method achieved high experimental success rates and produced binders with nanomolar-to-picomolar affinities that dramatically outperformed competing methods. The network also suggested amino acid substitutions that further improved binding affinity without experimental input, and the resulting binders were capable of protecting a chemically labile drug from degradation, illustrating potential applications in drug delivery, sensing, and catalysis.
items.why_notable: The work draws attention because it demonstrates that pairing two neural networks in an iterative loop can generate drug-binding proteins entirely from scratch—without prior examples—achieving high accuracy, affinity, and success rates. This capability opens promising avenues for applications such as targeted drug delivery and sequestration.
items.citation: Benjamin Fry, Kaia Slaw, Nicholas F. Polizzi. Zero-shot design of drug-binding proteins via neural iterative selection−expansion. Nature. 2026

items.title: Ductile alloys offering 100 MPa tensile strength at 2,400 °C
items.summary: This paper investigated the development of refractory metal alloys capable of simultaneously achieving extreme high-temperature strength and room-temperature ductility, a combination previously unrealized in any single material. The researchers designed a tantalum-based casting alloy strengthened by hafnium oxide nanoparticles, produced through a novel boron-intervened in situ oxidation reaction that generated densely and uniformly dispersed oxide particles of approximately fifty nanometers in diameter within grain interiors. The boron also segregated around the oxide nanoparticles, providing exceptional thermal stability against particle coarsening at temperatures reaching up to the maximum tested condition. The resulting alloy demonstrated tensile yield strengths far exceeding those of all prior refractory alloys, including multi-principal-element compositions, at temperatures above two thousand degrees Celsius, while also exhibiting high ultimate tensile strength and substantial elongation at room temperature. This work extends traditional oxide-dispersion strengthening strategies into a regime where ductile metallic alloys can bear mechanical loads at ultrahigh temperatures previously accessible only to brittle ceramics or intermetallics.
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because the alloy achieves an unusual combination of room-temperature ductility and record-breaking tensile strength at extraordinarily high temperatures, a pairing previously considered difficult to obtain. This capability opens the door to structural, load-bearing applications in extreme thermal environments well beyond what existing materials can withstand.
items.citation: Mintao Xue, Suzhi Li, Yi-Xiang Wang et al.. Ductile alloys offering 100 MPa tensile strength at 2,400 °C. Nature. 2026

items.title: Alternate RNA decoding results in stable and abundant proteins in mammals
items.why_notable: This work draws attention by revealing that an understudied form of RNA decoding produces peptide sequence modifications with major consequences for protein stability, tissue-specific proteomes, and disease, suggesting the process plays a far broader functional role in mammals than previously appreciated.

items.citation: Steven P. Hepplestone, R. J. Hicken. Laser light switches on heat flow in ultra-thin structures. Nature. 2026

items.title: Genomic insights into the population dynamics and demise of Neanderthals
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because a large influx of Neanderthal genomic data challenges previously held assumptions about these ancient hominins while opening up new questions about their population dynamics and ultimate disappearance.
items.citation: Carles Lalueza‐Fox. Genomic insights into the population dynamics and demise of Neanderthals. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: Sperm whales have different dialects
items.why_notable: The research is drawing attention because it reveals that sperm whales in different parts of the Mediterranean communicate using distinct regional variations in their click patterns, suggesting the existence of whale dialects. This finding, alongside other highlighted advances, points to a richer complexity in animal communication than previously appreciated.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: Sperm whales have different dialects. Nature. 2026

items.title: ‘Us’ not ‘them’: scientists must use their skills to help stop polarization and division
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it calls on researchers who study societal polarization to actively apply their expertise toward finding solutions that bridge divisions, rather than merely documenting the problem.
items.citation: ‘Us’ not ‘them’: scientists must use their skills to help stop polarization and division. Nature. 2026

items.title: A hidden predictor of sudden cardiac death uncovered by deep learning
items.why_notable: Deep learning analysis of electrocardiogram recordings has revealed a previously unrecognized group of people at elevated risk for sudden cardiac death, a finding that standard clinical assessment had missed. The work suggests that hidden patterns in routine heart recordings may hold untapped predictive power for one of medicine's most difficult-to-anticipate outcomes.
items.citation: Changxin Lai. A hidden predictor of sudden cardiac death uncovered by deep learning. Nature. 2026

items.title: ‘Megacluster’ of genes enables bacteria to make potent antibiotic mixture
items.why_notable: The work is attracting attention because researchers identified a large gene cluster in soil bacteria responsible for producing synergistic mixtures of antibiotic molecules, offering a potential new strategy for combating antibiotic-resistant infections.
items.citation: Steven T. Rutherford. ‘Megacluster’ of genes enables bacteria to make potent antibiotic mixture. Nature. 2026

items.title: Antibiotic cocktail made by soil bacteria can kill superbugs
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because soil bacteria have been found to produce a combination of antibiotic compounds capable of killing drug-resistant superbugs. The cocktail's potency is attributed to its ability to simultaneously attack multiple targets within an essential metabolic pathway, a strategy that could help overcome existing antibiotic resistance.
items.citation: Mohana Basu. Antibiotic cocktail made by soil bacteria can kill superbugs. Nature. 2026

items.title: Crude oil turns cheap porous membrane into a sieve to refine itself without heat
items.why_notable: The work is attracting attention because a cheap, unmodified porous membrane can refine crude oil without heat by exploiting the oil's own heavy hydrocarbons to spontaneously form ultra-narrow separation channels. This self-assembling process reportedly achieves naphtha-range separation with dramatically lower energy use and emissions than conventional distillation.
items.citation: Crude oil turns cheap porous membrane into a sieve to refine itself without heat. Nature. 2026

items.title: Long-sought chemical inhibitors of β-arrestin proteins
items.summary: The paper concerns β-arrestin proteins, which regulate signaling through G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), a major class of cell-surface signaling proteins. Researchers identified small molecules that bind directly to β-arrestins and inhibit their function, representing the first chemical tools of this kind. These three small molecules were found to disrupt the interaction between β-arrestins and GPCRs. The work provides new molecular probes for studying β-arrestin biology and opens potential avenues for developing therapeutics that target specific GPCR signaling pathways by acting on the transducer rather than the receptor itself.
items.why_notable: The work is attracting attention because it delivers the first small molecules that directly bind and inhibit β-arrestin proteins, which have previously lacked such chemical tools. This breakthrough enables precise probing of β-arrestin biology and opens new possibilities for developing pathway-specific therapeutics targeting GPCR signalling.
items.citation: Long-sought chemical inhibitors of β-arrestin proteins. Nature. 2026

items.title: Why science needs the humanities more than ever
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it argues that modern scientific advances in areas like AI and biotechnology generate complex ethical and social questions that go beyond what scientific or technical knowledge alone can address. The piece makes the case that humanistic perspectives are not merely complementary but essential to navigating these challenges responsibly.
items.citation: Xin Fan. Why science needs the humanities more than ever. Nature. 2026

items.title: A global map of seagrass ecosystems
items.why_notable: By merging satellite imagery with machine learning, researchers have produced the first complete global map of seagrass meadows, a breakthrough that could significantly advance conservation efforts for these ecologically vital ecosystems.
items.citation: Carlos M. Duarte, Dorte Krause-Jensen. A global map of seagrass ecosystems. Nature. 2026

items.title: Retraction Note: Sub-second periodicity in a fast radio burst
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because a previously published finding about periodic structure within a fast radio burst has been officially retracted, casting doubt on those reported results.
items.citation: Bridget C. Andersen, Kevin Bandura, Mohit Bhardwaj et al.. Retraction Note: Sub-second periodicity in a fast radio burst. Nature. 2026

items.title: Nepal’s new science ministry must strengthen scientific capacity
items.why_notable: The establishment of a new science ministry in Nepal is drawing attention as an opportunity — and an imperative — to build the country's scientific capacity. The framing suggests this institutional development carries significant expectations for advancing research and innovation in the country.
items.citation: Achyut Aryal. Nepal’s new science ministry must strengthen scientific capacity. Nature. 2026

items.title: Academic success still assumes uninterrupted careers
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it challenges the prevailing assumption in academia that career success is built on continuous, uninterrupted progression, suggesting that this standard disadvantages those whose careers have been disrupted.
items.citation: Dharani Yerrakalva. Academic success still assumes uninterrupted careers. Nature. 2026

items.title: The halo effect: how academic hierarchy undermines peer review and enables fraud
items.why_notable: The work draws attention by examining how institutional prestige and academic status bias the peer-review process, potentially allowing fraudulent research to pass scrutiny. It raises concerns that deference to hierarchy within academia creates systemic vulnerabilities in the mechanisms meant to ensure scientific integrity.
items.citation: Wenfeng Wang. The halo effect: how academic hierarchy undermines peer review and enables fraud. Nature. 2026

items.title: Do not leave fungi out of impact assessments
items.why_notable: The work draws attention to the systematic exclusion of fungi from environmental and biodiversity impact assessments, arguing that this omission represents a significant blind spot in how we evaluate human impacts on ecosystems.
items.citation: Ricardo da Silva Ribeiro, Flávia G. Chaves, Carlos Augusto Vidigal Fraga et al.. Do not leave fungi out of impact assessments. Nature. 2026

items.title: A 1970s patent that changed the course of commercial biotechnology
items.why_notable: The work is drawing attention because it revisits a landmark patent for recombinant DNA technology that fundamentally redirected the trajectory of commercial biotechnology, highlighting its historical significance through the journal's archival record.
items.citation: A 1970s patent that changed the course of commercial biotechnology. Nature. 2026

items.title: Silicon Valley’s vision for global AI is flawed: each country needs its own blueprint
items.why_notable: This work is drawing attention because it challenges the dominant Silicon Valley model of AI development, arguing that a one-size-fits-all global strategy fails to account for the distinct infrastructure, energy, and linguistic realities faced by emerging economies. The framing suggests that these countries' experiences are actively revealing the shortcomings of current AI expansion efforts and that tailored, country-specific approaches are needed instead.
items.citation: Pelonomi Moiloa. Silicon Valley’s vision for global AI is flawed: each country needs its own blueprint. Nature. 2026

items.title: Europe as science superpower: what it will take to rival the US and China
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because ongoing upheaval in US science and broader geopolitical instability have opened a window for Europe to assert itself as a global research leader, raising urgent questions about whether the continent can secure the funding and innovation capacity needed to compete with established superpowers.
items.citation: Elizabeth Gibney. Europe as science superpower: what it will take to rival the US and China. Nature. 2026

items.title: How should I respond to race-based exclusion in my lab?
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it addresses the serious and underreported problem of race-based discrimination within academic research environments, specifically how affected researchers can navigate exclusion and unequal standards while protecting their careers.
items.citation: Christine Ro. How should I respond to race-based exclusion in my lab?. Nature. 2026

items.title: Daily briefing: NASA to launch satellite-rescue mission
items.why_notable: The mission is drawing interest because it involves actively rescuing a satellite by significantly boosting its orbit, a notable demonstration of in-space intervention capability. The briefing also highlights broader geopolitical and workplace tensions, including Europe's ambitions to compete scientifically with major powers and debate over remote working policies driven by leadership personality traits.
items.citation: Flora Graham. Daily briefing: NASA to launch satellite-rescue mission. Nature. 2026

items.title: Europe must seize the moment to lead on free and open science
items.why_notable: This work is attracting attention because it calls on Europe to take a leadership role in advancing open, democratic science at a moment when such principles are seen as under threat. The framing positions Europe as an underrecognized force in global research with both the capacity and responsibility to ensure science remains freely accessible to researchers worldwide.
items.citation: Europe must seize the moment to lead on free and open science. Nature. 2026

items.title: Making samples one billion times bigger lets simple microscopes pinpoint amino acids
items.why_notable: By physically expanding protein samples to an enormous degree, this approach overcomes the resolution limits of conventional light microscopy, enabling individual amino acids to be identified without the need for more complex imaging techniques.
items.citation: Ewen Callaway. Making samples one billion times bigger lets simple microscopes pinpoint amino acids. Nature. 2026

items.title: Early Detection and Surveillance of the SARS-CoV-2 Variant BA.3.2 — Worldwide, November 2024–February 2026
items.summary: The paper examined the emergence and early spread of the SARS-CoV-2 variant BA.3.2, first identified in South Africa in late 2024, with a focus on its detection within the United States and globally. Researchers employed a multimodal genomic surveillance strategy incorporating traveler-based sampling, clinical specimens, airplane wastewater, and community wastewater monitoring across multiple states. BA.3.2 carries a substantial number of substitutions and deletions in the spike protein relative to the antigens used in current COVID-19 vaccines, raising concern that existing immune protection may be reduced. Surveillance detected the variant in travelers, clinical patients, and wastewater systems spanning dozens of states, with the variant also reported in more than twenty countries worldwide. The authors conclude that continued genomic surveillance is essential given BA.3.2's potential to evade vaccine- and infection-derived immunity and its ongoing public health implications.
items.venue: MMWR Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report
items.citation: Mila Shakya, Kevin C. Ma, Laura J. Hughes et al.. Early Detection and Surveillance of the SARS-CoV-2 Variant BA.3.2 — Worldwide, November 2024–February 2026. MMWR Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report. 2026

items.title: Validation of molecular detection of <i>Candida auris</i> from wastewater.
items.summary: The study addressed the challenge of monitoring *Candida auris*, a multidrug-resistant fungal pathogen that spreads efficiently in healthcare settings through skin colonization and shedding, by exploring wastewater-based surveillance as an alternative to direct patient screening. The researchers validated a quantitative PCR assay, originally developed for clinical colonization screening, for use with wastewater samples. Testing in a wastewater matrix demonstrated high amplification efficiency, strong reproducibility, and a low limit of detection, indicating the assay performs reliably outside its original clinical context. The findings support adapting this qPCR protocol for integration into existing wastewater surveillance workflows, offering a more cost-effective approach to monitoring *C. auris* prevalence across entire healthcare systems, including in settings where direct patient screening is limited or unavailable.
items.citation: Rodríguez Stewart RM, Litvintseva AP, Chavez J et al.. Validation of molecular detection of <i>Candida auris</i> from wastewater.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: A Systematic Review of Gastrointestinal and Respiratory Pathogen Detection in Wastewater in Africa, with Focus on Rwanda: Implications for Early Warning and Public Health Surveillance.
items.summary: This systematic review examined the use of wastewater-based epidemiology (WBE) as a surveillance tool for gastrointestinal and respiratory pathogens, with a particular focus on the African context and Rwanda specifically. The authors conducted a narrative systematic review drawing on peer-reviewed literature, global health reports, and surveillance studies published over more than two decades, ultimately synthesizing findings from a substantial body of eligible studies identified through major academic databases. The review found that WBE offers a non-invasive and cost-effective means of detecting both symptomatic and asymptomatic infections at the population level, providing real-time epidemiological intelligence that can complement and fill gaps left by traditional clinical surveillance systems. Key challenges identified include variability in sampling methods, environmental factors that accelerate pathogen decay, and inconsistencies in laboratory workflows across settings. The authors conclude that integrating WBE into public health surveillance infrastructure, particularly in low- and middle-income countries such as Rwanda, could meaningfully strengthen epidemic preparedness and outbreak response, provided that investment in laboratory capacity, standardized protocols, and multisectoral collaboration is sustained.
items.citation: Bambara S, Isingizwe MC, Adegboyega TT et al.. A Systematic Review of Gastrointestinal and Respiratory Pathogen Detection in Wastewater in Africa, with Focus on Rwanda: Implications for Early Warning and Public Health Surveillance.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Multi-Pathogen Wastewater Surveillance enables Real-Time Targeted Public Health Interventions During the 2025 African Nations Championship Football Tournament
items.summary: This study examined whether multi-pathogen wastewater-based surveillance could serve as a practical public health tool during a major mass gathering in a low- and middle-income country. Researchers deployed Moore swabs across dozens of manholes at a national stadium in Uganda during multiple match days of the African Nations Championship football tournament, concentrating and analyzing samples for a broad panel of enteric and respiratory pathogens using quantitative PCR. The vast majority of collected wastewater samples tested positive for at least one pathogen, with enteric pathogens such as Shigella, Rotavirus A, and Enterovirus detected most frequently, while mpox virus was found in a notable share of samples and respiratory pathogens including SARS-CoV-2 and Influenza B appeared at lower rates. Surveillance results directly informed real-time public health responses including enhanced sanitation, hygiene promotion, environmental disinfection, and strengthened syndromic surveillance at the venue. The authors conclude that integrating multi-pathogen wastewater surveillance into mass-gathering preparedness frameworks is operationally feasible in low-resource settings and can provide actionable, population-level intelligence to support outbreak detection and response.
items.citation: Nsawotebba A, Morunyanga I, Nakintu V et al.. Multi-Pathogen Wastewater Surveillance enables Real-Time Targeted Public Health Interventions During the 2025 African Nations Championship Football Tournament. 2026

items.title: Revealing the hidden burden: wastewater-based epidemiology for underreported and emerging infectious diseases in communities.
items.summary: Wastewater-based epidemiology has emerged as a powerful population-level surveillance tool that captures infectious disease burden beyond what traditional clinical reporting systems detect. This review examines how wastewater analysis can identify underreported, novel, and emerging pathogens, including enteric, respiratory, and neglected infectious agents that clinical systems routinely miss. Sequencing-based studies applied to wastewater have revealed extensive diversity within the human virome, including numerous novel and divergent viruses, while frequent co-detection of multiple viral taxa suggests that co-infections may shape viral evolution and disease dynamics. The approach has demonstrated the ability to detect both known and novel pathogens before they are clinically recognized, despite ongoing methodological challenges in quantification and biological validation. The review concludes that advances in long-read sequencing, bioinformatics, and global data integration will strengthen wastewater genomics as a scalable, equitable component of early-warning and One Health surveillance frameworks.
items.citation: Greaves JC, Rodriguez RA. Revealing the hidden burden: wastewater-based epidemiology for underreported and emerging infectious diseases in communities.. Environmental monitoring and assessment. 2026

items.title: Environmental surveillance of pathogens in Africa.
items.summary: This review examined the state of environmental surveillance (ES) for infectious pathogens across Africa, where resource constraints limit traditional clinical surveillance. Using a rapid review methodology drawing on multiple major databases, the authors systematically identified and synthesized published studies that applied ES—particularly wastewater surveillance—to detect pathogens of public health concern on the continent. The review found a diverse range of microbial species detected, spanning bacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites, with SARS-CoV-2, rotaviruses, and polioviruses among the most commonly identified viral pathogens and Vibrio cholerae the most frequently reported bacterium. Grab sampling predominated as the collection method, while concentration techniques varied depending on pathogen type. The authors conclude that ES of infectious pathogens in Africa remains limited in scope and call for its expansion to improve pathogen monitoring, deepen understanding of transmission dynamics, and strengthen preparedness for emerging variants.
items.citation: Darko E, Akortia D, Nkrumah G et al.. Environmental surveillance of pathogens in Africa.. Applied and environmental microbiology. 2026

items.title: Hospital wastewater-based surveillance as an early-warning system for toxigenic <i>Vibrio cholerae</i>: first detection, isolation, characterization, and traceback investigation in Beijing, China.
items.summary: This study reports the first detection of toxigenic *Vibrio cholerae* in hospital wastewater in Beijing, China, demonstrating the application of wastewater-based surveillance as an early-warning tool. Upon detecting a positive signal, researchers isolated viable *V. cholerae* from the wastewater sample, identified it as the O1 Ogawa serotype, and performed whole-genome sequencing and comparative genomic analysis. The genomic data showed the isolate was closely related to locally circulating strains but distinct from the seventh pandemic lineage, suggesting a link to indigenous transmission chains rather than international spread. A multi-source traceback investigation integrating clinical, environmental, and epidemiological data was immediately launched following detection. The authors conclude that hospital wastewater surveillance offers significant early-warning and situational-awareness capabilities for emerging pathogens and should be considered an essential component of infectious disease prevention and control systems.
items.citation: Gao Q, Huang Y, Lv B et al.. Hospital wastewater-based surveillance as an early-warning system for toxigenic <i>Vibrio cholerae</i>: first detection, isolation, characterization, and traceback investigation in Beijing, China.. Emerging microbes & infections. 2026

items.title: Ecological and Microbial Processes in Green Waste Co-Composting for Pathogen Control and Evaluation of Compost Quality Index (CQI) Toward Agricultural Biosafety
items.summary: This study examined whether co-composting green garden waste with sheep manure under semi-arid Moroccan conditions could effectively inactivate pathogens and produce agriculturally safe compost. Using an open windrow system over a period of several months, the researchers monitored thermophilic temperatures and tracked reductions in key indicator organisms including Escherichia coli, total coliforms, Staphylococcus aureus, and sulfite-reducing Clostridia, while Salmonella was absent throughout. Pathogen reductions surpassed the threshold considered indicative of effective sanitization, and the authors noted that inactivation mechanisms beyond heat alone appeared to contribute to pathogen elimination despite moderate temperature variability. Compost quality was assessed using a Compost Quality Index, which distinguished between the two heap compositions, with fallen leaves combined with sheep manure rated as good quality and green waste combined with sheep manure rated as moderate quality, reflecting differences in microbial dynamics and compost stability. The study concludes that open windrow co-composting is a practical and low-cost approach for safe organic waste management that can support sustainable agriculture and help meet regulatory biosafety standards.
items.citation: Majda Oueld Lhaj, Rachid Moussadek, Hatim Sanad et al.. Ecological and Microbial Processes in Green Waste Co-Composting for Pathogen Control and Evaluation of Compost Quality Index (CQI) Toward Agricultural Biosafety. Environments. 2026

items.title: Next-Generation Strategies for Controlling Foodborne Pathogens: Precision Antimicrobials, Biofilm Disruption, and Emerging Molecular Interventions
items.summary: This review examines the growing inadequacy of conventional chemical preservatives and sanitizers against foodborne pathogens, which increasingly persist through biofilm formation and antimicrobial resistance in complex food-processing environments. The authors survey a broad range of emerging biological, nanotechnological, and plant-derived strategies—including bacteriophages, phage-derived enzymes, bacteriocins, quorum-sensing inhibitors, nanoparticle delivery systems, and bioactive natural compounds—designed to selectively target pathogens while preserving beneficial microbial communities. The review also covers advances in anti-biofilm surface engineering and the integration of these individual tools into coordinated multi-hurdle systems supported by real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. Particular attention is given to digital frameworks such as digital twins of food-processing lines, which are proposed as tools to connect pathogen detection, risk prediction, and targeted intervention in a unified operational system. The authors conclude by identifying persistent knowledge gaps, regulatory hurdles, and the need for standardized validation and long-term safety evaluation before these precision food-safety technologies can be reliably deployed in real-world settings.
items.citation: Ayman Elbehiry, Ahmed I. Alajaji. Next-Generation Strategies for Controlling Foodborne Pathogens: Precision Antimicrobials, Biofilm Disruption, and Emerging Molecular Interventions. Foods. 2026

items.title: Understanding Pseudomonas aeruginosa Biofilms: Quorum Sensing, c-di-GMP Signaling, and Emerging Antibiofilm Approaches
items.summary: This review examines how *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* forms highly resistant biofilms and why conventional antibiotic susceptibility testing fails to predict treatment outcomes in biofilm-associated infections. The authors describe the molecular regulatory systems governing biofilm development, particularly quorum sensing and cyclic di-GMP signaling, which coordinate matrix production, efflux pump activity, and the formation of dormant persister and viable-but-nonculturable cell populations. Key matrix components such as Psl, Pel, alginate, and extracellular DNA impede antimicrobial penetration, while regulatory proteins drive upregulation of efflux systems that further reduce intracellular drug concentrations, collectively explaining the large gap between standard minimum inhibitory concentrations and biofilm-specific eradication thresholds. The review synthesizes emerging antibiofilm strategies including quorum sensing inhibitors, compounds that reduce c-di-GMP signaling, bacteriophages, nanoparticles, and host-directed therapies, alongside advanced imaging and diagnostic tools to improve detection and treatment planning. A staged therapeutic framework is proposed that sequentially targets matrix dispersal, antibiotic killing, and immune clearance, situated within a One Health perspective that connects clinical infections to environmental biofilm reservoirs.
items.citation: Ayman Elbehiry, Eman Marzouk, Husam M. Edrees et al.. Understanding Pseudomonas aeruginosa Biofilms: Quorum Sensing, c-di-GMP Signaling, and Emerging Antibiofilm Approaches. Microorganisms. 2026

items.title: Microplastics in the Rural Environment: Sources, Transport, and Impacts
items.summary: This review examined the presence, sources, movement, and ecological and health impacts of microplastics in rural environments, a setting that has received far less scientific attention than urban or marine contexts. The authors synthesized existing literature on contamination pathways, identifying both local sources such as agricultural plastics, domestic waste, and road runoff, as well as broader regional processes including atmospheric deposition and hydrological transport. Rural lakes, streams, soils, and groundwater were found to function as both sinks and secondary sources of microplastics, with polyethylene, polypropylene, and polyethylene terephthalate being the most commonly detected polymer types. Documented ecological consequences include bioaccumulation in freshwater organisms, soil degradation, and food chain transfer, while human health risks arise from contaminated groundwater, air, and locally produced food. The review concludes that rural systems are underrepresented in monitoring and policy efforts and calls for context-specific mitigation strategies, improved wastewater treatment, and integrated surveillance frameworks.
items.citation: Awnon Bhowmik, Goutam Kumar Saha. Microplastics in the Rural Environment: Sources, Transport, and Impacts. Pollutants. 2026

items.title: Bacterial defense mechanisms against bacteriophages: an evolutionary arms race
items.summary: This review paper examined the co-evolutionary competition between bacteria and bacteriophages, focusing on the diverse mechanisms bacteria use to resist phage infection and the counter-adaptations phages employ in response. The authors systematically surveyed known bacterial defense strategies operating at multiple stages of the phage infection cycle, including surface-level defenses such as receptor modification, capsule production, and biofilm formation, as well as intracellular mechanisms including restriction-modification systems, abortive infection, and CRISPR-Cas adaptive immunity. Phages were found to circumvent these defenses through strategies such as anti-CRISPR proteins, receptor mimicry, and enzymatic degradation of capsules and biofilms. The review concludes that these dynamic interactions shape broader microbial ecosystems and that understanding them is essential for developing phage-based therapies against multidrug-resistant bacteria. Emerging approaches such as engineered phages and combination therapies are highlighted as promising avenues informed by this evolutionary framework.
items.citation: Rafwana Ibrahim, Jesil Mathew Aranjani. Bacterial defense mechanisms against bacteriophages: an evolutionary arms race. Archives of Microbiology. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper investigates the theoretical foundations of anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes, which are widely used tools for sequential hypothesis testing and detection. The authors reformulate the standard recipe of mixing exponential test statistics over a prior as a two-player zero-sum game in which a Learner chooses a prior and Nature adaptively constructs a difficult score process, with a pathwise Gibbs-variational identity governing the Learner's wealth along every realized path. From this game-theoretic framework, they derive that the law of the iterated logarithm emerges as the minimax detection boundary rather than as an artifact of combinatorial slack, and that the optimal prior is not a free design choice but is uniquely determined as the equalizer strategy that renders every boundary-crossing time equally costly for Nature. This equalizer strategy turns out to coincide with the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales in log-log coordinates, and it yields a sharp closed-form correction to the iterated logarithm boundary with an explicitly characterized coefficient. The paper unifies a range of previously disparate constructions—including Howard-Ramdas stitching, betting confidence sequences, and the Erdős-Kolmogorov integral test—as special cases of this single equalizer principle, with an empirical companion evaluation confirming the central identities.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigates whether the neutral hydrogen 21-cm absorption line, long predicted to appear in Fast Radio Burst spectra, can realistically be detected given the complicating effects of scintillation. The authors developed an efficient simulation model of diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen to characterize how frequency-dependent flux modulations interact with the absorption profile across different signal regimes. They found that detection is feasible when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs substantially in scale from the absorption line width, and that stacking repeated bursts can improve detectability provided the bursts are separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale to avoid correlated frequency modulations. Cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds suggests one particular repeating source may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud. Even with current and planned sensitive telescopes, however, the combined effects of scintillation and detector noise require stacking a very large number of bursts to achieve a statistically significant detection, with improved detector sensitivity being key to eventually probing hydrogen clouds in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Kinematic detection of dusty outflows from AGN: PAH kinematics of type 2 quasars with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy
items.summary: This paper investigated the kinematics of dust in the outflows of type 2 quasars by analyzing Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon features observed with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy. The researchers applied Principal Component Analysis tomography to produce velocity maps of PAH emission in five type 2 quasars drawn from the Quasar Feedback sample, comparing the dust kinematics with those of ionized and molecular gas tracers. Velocity maps of the large, neutral PAH feature at 11.3 microns were successfully produced for three of the five objects, all of which showed clear evidence of dusty outflows after subtracting disk kinematics, with molecular hydrogen rotational transitions providing consistent supporting evidence. The ionized PAH feature at 6.2 microns could not be mapped, possibly due to intrinsic profile differences or AGN-driven suppression, consistent with prior findings that AGN outflows preferentially contain neutral rather than ionized PAHs. The results suggest that dusty outflows become more prevalent at higher Eddington ratios and may play an important role in AGN evolution, though the authors caution that the small sample size limits strong conclusions.
items.citation: Fergus R. Donnan, Cristina Ramos Almeida, Omaira González Martín et al.. Kinematic detection of dusty outflows from AGN: PAH kinematics of type 2 quasars with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy. arXiv. 2026

items.title: First results of sub-arcsecond scale objects identified with ASKAP using interplanetary scintillation
items.summary: This paper presents a catalogue of compact radio sources detected through interplanetary scintillation using the ASKAP telescope, targeting objects smaller than approximately one-tenth of an arcsecond. The authors developed a data capture approach using a fast-sampling system to overcome the telescope's standard integration time limitations, enabling sub-second visibility measurements across a wide field of view. The observations cleanly distinguish two populations of compact sources: hot spots embedded within extended radio lobes, and unresolved sources associated with active galactic nuclei or compact symmetric objects in galactic nuclei. Combining these results with lower-frequency observations from the Murchison Widefield Array revealed that peaked-spectrum spectral energy distributions dominate the compact-source population at frequencies below one gigahertz. The work is presented as a pilot study and pathfinder for a broader upcoming survey using the same interplanetary scintillation technique with ASKAP.
items.citation: R. Chhetri, J. S. Morgan, R. D. Ekers et al.. First results of sub-arcsecond scale objects identified with ASKAP using interplanetary scintillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: CacheMPC: Certified Cached Model Predictive Control for Quadruped Locomotion
items.summary: This paper addresses the computational bottleneck in Model Predictive Control for quadruped locomotion, where solving quadratic programs each control cycle limits update rates on embedded hardware. The proposed Certified CacheMPC system stores and reuses previously computed contact-force trajectory solutions using a locality-sensitive hashing scheme, retrieving cached solutions only when a posterior certificate confirms feasibility and bounds cost suboptimality, with a fallback hierarchy that combines cached retrieval, deadline-bounded fresh solves, and shifted prior solutions. The framework was evaluated extensively in MuJoCo simulation and deployed on physical hardware, achieving large median speedups in solve time in both simulation and on-robot settings. Stability testing across thousands of trials found no statistically significant difference in closed-loop stable rates between cache variants and the baseline, though the specific safety contribution of the certificate mechanism could not be resolved at the tested sample sizes.
items.citation: Nimesh Khandelwal, Mehul Anand, Shakti S. Gupta et al.. CacheMPC: Certified Cached Model Predictive Control for Quadruped Locomotion. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Agent-Native Immune System: Architecture, Taxonomy, and Engineering
items.summary: This paper addresses the growing security vulnerabilities of autonomous AI agents that arise from their persistent memory, tool-use capabilities, and multi-agent coordination, arguing that existing defenses such as perimeter security and training-time alignment are insufficient because they operate outside the agent's active reasoning loop. The authors propose the Agent-Native Immune System, a biologically inspired defense architecture embedded directly within the agent's cognitive processes. The framework introduces a six-layer Immune Tower spanning physical isolation to high-level cognitive defenses, a taxonomy distinguishing superficial non-parametric defenses from more robust parametric vaccines, and a self-monitoring metacognitive structure called the Harness Triad that enables continual adaptation to novel threats. A key conceptual contribution is the theoretical distinction between model alignment, characterized as a static value foundation established during training, and agent immunity, which functions as a dynamic runtime enforcement mechanism against attacks such as memory poisoning, tool-chain manipulation, and multi-agent protocol exploitation. The paper concludes by identifying open challenges including immune protocol standardization, new evaluation metrics for measuring false-positive interventions, and the co-evolutionary dynamics between threats and defenses in collective agent ecosystems.
items.citation: Bo Shen, Lifeng Chang, Tianyuan Wei et al.. Agent-Native Immune System: Architecture, Taxonomy, and Engineering. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.
items.summary: The study addressed the need for improved detection of *Schistosoma mansoni* infection in its intermediate snail hosts, which is critical for schistosomiasis control but hampered by the poor sensitivity of conventional parasitological methods. Researchers developed a loop-mediated isothermal amplification assay targeting the mitochondrial NADH dehydrogenase subunit 1 gene of *S. mansoni*, optimized it under simple isothermal conditions, and evaluated its performance against microscopy, conventional PCR, and real-time PCR using both laboratory-infected snails and field-collected samples from two African countries. In laboratory testing, the assay achieved complete specificity and high sensitivity, outperforming microscopy and matching the performance of real-time PCR. In field surveys, the LAMP assay detected a higher proportion of infected snails than conventional PCR, while microscopy failed to identify any positive individuals. The authors conclude that this assay offers a sensitive, specific, and practically deployable tool for routine snail surveillance and early detection of shifts in schistosomiasis transmission.
items.citation: Li HM, Qin ZQ, Lv S et al.. Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.. Tropical medicine and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.
items.summary: The paper investigated whether a machine learning approach could automate the interpretation of loop-mediated isothermal amplification diagnostic results, which are typically subject to human error when read visually. The researchers applied the YOLOv8 object detection algorithm to digital images of LAMP assay tubes, training it to locate individual tubes and classify them as positive or negative for the target DNA. The trained model achieved high overall accuracy, precision, and recall for both positive and negative classifications on a test set. The authors conclude that this approach is suitable for real-time LAMP diagnostics, offering a faster, more objective, and more reliable alternative to manual visual interpretation.
items.citation: Mezgebo B, Chaffee R, Castellanos LR et al.. You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.
items.summary: This review examines isothermal amplification technologies as diagnostic tools for detecting pathogens in both livestock and companion animals. The authors surveyed recent advances in these nucleic acid amplification methods, which operate at a constant temperature and therefore eliminate the need for thermocycling equipment required by conventional polymerase chain reaction approaches. The review covers innovations in assay design, improvements in analytical performance, and the integration of these methods into portable, field-deployable platforms suited to resource-limited settings. The authors also address the limitations of traditional and molecular diagnostic methods, particularly their dependence on expensive instrumentation, specialized infrastructure, and trained personnel, which restrict their use outside centralized laboratories. The review concludes by identifying remaining challenges and future directions for incorporating isothermal amplification into routine veterinary diagnostics and broader animal disease surveillance programs.
items.citation: Tooryan F, Enyertornye B, Choudhury FJ et al.. Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.. Frontiers in veterinary science. 2026

items.title: Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.
items.summary: This study developed and validated a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification workflow by adapting a commercially available digital polymerase chain reaction platform to perform absolute quantification without standard curves. The researchers optimized the reaction chemistry using a diluted fluorescent dye master mix and targeted a mitochondrial gene region from an aquatic invasive species as a model system. They compared the new digital approach against conventional turbidimetric bulk isothermal amplification across a range of template concentrations. The digital method demonstrated stable and reliable detection down to very low copy numbers, whereas conventional bulk amplification showed greater variability and occasional amplification delays at low concentrations. The high partition density of the platform was identified as a key advantage for reducing molecular dropout, making this approach applicable to diagnostics, food safety testing, and environmental DNA monitoring.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Fukuta S, Suzuki R. Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the need for decentralized, rapid testing accessible outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluated approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, emphasizing their suitability for simplified and equipment-free deployment. Amplification strategies reviewed span both thermal cycling methods such as polymerase chain reaction and isothermal alternatives including loop-mediated isothermal amplification and recombinase polymerase amplification, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review covers a spectrum from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, which together offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition at the point of care.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: Numerical study of the ITER divertor plasma with the B2-EIRENE code package
items.summary: The abstract is too thin to summarize, as it contains only a report citation reference and a note about corrections, with no scientific content describing the study's methods, objectives, or findings.
items.citation: V. Kotov. Numerical study of the ITER divertor plasma with the B2-EIRENE code package. Dokumentenrepositorium der RUB (Ruhr University Bochum). 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology and their applications in laboratory medicine, with particular focus on how innovations in design, fabrication, and artificial intelligence integration are reshaping clinical diagnostics. The authors surveyed developments in chip fabrication approaches, including three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, alongside improvements in biosensor integration that enable real-time detection of biomarkers such as circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. These capabilities have expanded the utility of lab-on-a-chip systems across cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, and personalized medicine. A central finding is that merging microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning produces smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support, substantially enhancing device reliability and analytical power. The review also identifies persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be widely adopted in routine clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities
items.summary: This review examines wastewater-based genomic surveillance as a tool for monitoring SARS-CoV-2 and other viruses at the community level, focusing on its capacity to detect known and emerging mutations and characterize circulating viral lineages. The authors survey the landscape of sample preparation methods, sequencing technologies, and bioinformatics approaches currently used in this field. They identify key challenges, including difficulties in distinguishing closely related lineages and identifying novel ones, as well as limitations in computational analysis pipelines that remain largely untested under conditions of reduced clinical surveillance and continued viral evolution. Despite these obstacles, the review concludes that wastewater genomic surveillance holds considerable promise for reinforcing public health monitoring systems, particularly as traditional clinical surveillance declines.
items.citation: Viorel Munteanu, Michael A. Saldana, David Dreifuss et al.. SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities. Genome biology. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of biosensing technologies designed to detect priority foodborne pathogens, with relevance to both food safety and clinical healthcare settings. The authors survey electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic sensing platforms, emphasizing how advances in functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering have improved sensor sensitivity, specificity, and performance in complex food matrices. A central focus is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into these platforms, enabling real-time analytics, intelligent pattern recognition, and high-throughput multiplexed detection that transform conventional biosensors into smart diagnostic devices. The review critically assesses practical challenges including matrix interference, bioreceptor instability, manufacturing scalability, and the absence of regulatory standardization, while proposing mitigation strategies such as ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, explainable AI, and printable electronics. Looking ahead, the authors outline emerging directions including self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen detection panels, blockchain-based traceability, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward regulation-ready next-generation food safety monitoring.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Early diagnosis and treatment of leptospirosis: Optimizing clinical outcomes
items.summary: Leptospirosis is a widespread zoonotic disease responsible for enormous global morbidity and mortality, yet it is frequently diagnosed only after serious organ complications have already developed. This review examines why delayed diagnosis is harmful, emphasizing that the leptospiremic phase represents a critical therapeutic window during which antibiotic treatment is most effective and complications such as renal failure, jaundice, and pulmonary hemorrhage can be prevented. The authors evaluate the shortcomings of existing clinical and laboratory diagnostic approaches alongside emerging molecular and biomarker-based methods, as well as integrated scoring systems intended to aid frontline triage. Evidence for early antibiotic therapy and supportive care strategies is synthesized, and severity prediction tools are assessed. The authors advocate for a paradigm shift toward point-of-care, field-adaptable diagnostics and integrated care pathways as means of reducing treatment delays and lowering the overall burden of the disease.
items.citation: Umaporn Limothai, Nattachai Srisawat, David A. Haake. Early diagnosis and treatment of leptospirosis: Optimizing clinical outcomes. Journal of Infection. 2026

items.title: Advanced Numerical Modeling of Powder Bed Fusion: From Physics-Based Simulations to AI-Augmented Digital Twins
items.summary: This review paper examines the current state and future directions of numerical modeling for powder bed fusion additive manufacturing processes. The authors synthesize advances across physics-based simulations, machine learning methods, and digital twin frameworks, analyzing modeling approaches at multiple scales ranging from melt pool dynamics at the micro-scale to residual stress predictions at the part scale. The review highlights hybrid physics-data-driven approaches for capturing process-structure-property relationships, with particular attention to the integration of real-time sensing and artificial intelligence-enhanced optimization as foundations for emerging digital twins. Key challenges identified include computational cost, data scarcity, and model interoperability, while the authors also outline opportunities for developing scalable and interpretable industry-ready platforms. The paper concludes by positioning digital twins as a transformative paradigm capable of advancing powder bed fusion toward reliable and industrially scalable manufacturing.
items.citation: Łukasz Łach, Dmytro Svyetlichnyy. Advanced Numerical Modeling of Powder Bed Fusion: From Physics-Based Simulations to AI-Augmented Digital Twins. Materials. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
items.citation: Cheng L, Zhang B, Hu X et al.. Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Frontiers in medicine. 2026

items.title: Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.
items.summary: This narrative review examined the diagnostic and prognostic value of pancreatic stone protein as a biomarker for sepsis in paediatric patients, particularly in the context of nosocomial and postoperative infections where distinguishing infectious from sterile inflammation is clinically difficult. The authors conducted a structured literature search and synthesized findings from studies meeting their inclusion criteria, comparing pancreatic stone protein performance against conventional markers such as C-reactive protein and procalcitonin. Across the analysed studies, pancreatic stone protein levels were significantly elevated in children with sepsis compared to those with non-infectious systemic inflammation, and elevations correlated with bacteraemia, organ dysfunction, and mortality. The biomarker demonstrated diagnostic performance comparable to or exceeding that of traditional markers, including in neonatal and other high-risk paediatric populations. The authors conclude that pancreatic stone protein is a promising tool for early sepsis identification and risk stratification in children, but emphasize that study variability necessitates larger multicentre prospective trials and integration into multimarker diagnostic approaches before broad clinical adoption.
items.citation: Ramírez Quintana A, Gaman EL, Badea M et al.. Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis investigated whether the immature platelet fraction, a measure of bone marrow thrombopoietic activity, could serve as an early diagnostic biomarker for sepsis. The authors searched major databases and pooled data from studies meeting their inclusion criteria, calculating summary diagnostic accuracy statistics including sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic odds ratio, with a subgroup analysis focused on adults. The meta-analysis found that the immature platelet fraction demonstrated only moderate diagnostic accuracy for sepsis, with pooled sensitivity and specificity both in the low-to-mid seventy percent range and moderate heterogeneity across the included studies. The authors conclude that the immature platelet fraction may serve as a complementary rather than standalone biomarker for sepsis diagnosis, but emphasize that the findings are based on a very small number of studies and should be interpreted with caution. They call for further standardized prospective research to clarify its clinical utility.
items.citation: Wiranata S, Manuaba IBAP, Supadmanaba IGP et al.. Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Hematology, transfusion and cell therapy. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein as biomarkers for neonatal sepsis, addressing inconsistencies in prior clinical studies. Following PRISMA guidelines, the authors searched three major databases and pooled data from eligible controlled studies involving nearly five hundred neonates, calculating sensitivity, specificity, and area under the summary receiver operating characteristic curve for each biomarker. The results showed that interleukin-27 achieved higher pooled sensitivity and specificity than C-reactive protein, and its area under the curve was also notably greater. The authors conclude that interleukin-27 demonstrates superior overall diagnostic accuracy compared with C-reactive protein for identifying neonatal sepsis. They propose that interleukin-27 may serve as a more reliable biomarker to support early and accurate diagnosis of neonatal sepsis in clinical settings.
items.citation: Liu W, Zhang Y, Liu W et al.. Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in pediatrics. 2026

items.title: Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.
items.summary: This paper examined the landscape of diagnostic tools for neonatal sepsis in low- and middle-income countries, where the condition remains a major driver of infant death despite limited diagnostic progress.
