The authors synthesised evidence across a range of current and emerging diagnostic strategies, including clinical algorithms, host-response biomarkers, haematological parameters, continuous monitoring, and artificial intelligence-based digital platforms, evaluating each against the WHO Target Product Profile for neonatal sepsis triage. The analysis assessed not only diagnostic performance but also operational feasibility and affordability within resource-constrained settings. The authors identified several promising approaches, including host-response biomarkers with greater accuracy than those currently in use and affordable technologies capable of enhancing existing predictive algorithms. Their findings support moving toward integrated diagnostic strategies that combine point-of-care testing, routine clinical data, and digital decision support to enable earlier detection, more rational antibiotic use, and reduced neonatal mortality.
items.citation: Gleeson B, Blumel B, Ibarz Pavon AB et al.. Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.. BMJ global health. 2026

items.title: Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.
items.summary: The study investigated whether calprotectin, a neutrophil activation marker measured in plasma, could serve as a useful diagnostic biomarker for sepsis and for identifying the source of infection in emergency department patients presenting with suspected sepsis. Researchers prospectively enrolled hundreds of patients at a university hospital emergency department and measured calprotectin levels at admission, analyzing its ability to discriminate between sepsis and non-infectious inflammatory conditions as well as between different infection sources. Calprotectin showed only modest ability to distinguish sepsis from non-infectious systemic inflammation and was not an independent predictor when C-reactive protein was included in the analysis. However, calprotectin demonstrated value in differentiating lower respiratory tract infections from upper respiratory tract infections and urinary tract infections, and adding calprotectin to C-reactive protein significantly improved discriminatory performance for these comparisons. The authors conclude that calprotectin may help identify the source of infection in sepsis presentations but that further research is needed to determine its role in routine clinical assessment.
items.citation: Wollmer M, Wessman T, Melander O et al.. Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.. Open forum infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists and next-generation incretin-based medications: metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal benefits
items.summary: This paper reviews the clinical profile and expanding therapeutic applications of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists and related incretin-based medications. Originally developed for type 2 diabetes, these agents provide effective glycaemic control with weight reduction and low hypoglycaemia risk, and have since demonstrated reductions in major adverse cardiovascular events, heart failure hospitalisations, and kidney disease progression including slowing of declining filtration rate and reduction of albuminuria. Approved agents and the dual GIP/GLP-1 co-agonist tirzepatide have also shown benefits in obesity management, encompassing prevention of type 2 diabetes, improvements in steatotic liver disease, obstructive sleep apnoea, and knee osteoarthritis. Emerging areas of investigation include neurodegenerative diseases and substance use disorders, alongside development of oral small-molecule formulations and next-generation dual and triple receptor agonists designed to achieve greater efficacy, particularly for weight loss. The review notes that gastrointestinal adverse events remain a significant challenge, and that dose-escalation strategies require further optimisation to balance efficacy with tolerability.
items.citation: Michael A Nauck, KATHERINE R. TUTTLE, Matthias H. Tschöp et al.. GLP-1 receptor agonists and next-generation incretin-based medications: metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal benefits. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history and key milestones in the field while focusing particularly on how lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways regulate the behavior of immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. The review explores how lipid metabolites influence immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation across both innate and adaptive immunity, and discusses multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels. The authors also address the involvement of lipid metabolism in conditions such as autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review surveys therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical trial progress, and emerging directions including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review examines the role of neutrophils in chronic inflammation, challenging the traditional view that these innate immune cells function only in acute, short-term responses. The authors synthesize current knowledge on how neutrophils, through mechanisms such as neutrophil extracellular trap formation, reactive oxygen species production, degranulation, and phagocytosis, can adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype that leads to dysregulated activation, collateral tissue damage, and a sustained inflammatory environment. The review also explores how neutrophils actively communicate with other immune and non-immune cell types—including endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes—to orchestrate broader inflammatory and tissue remodeling processes. These principles are then applied to specific immune-driven chronic diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders. The authors conclude that a deeper understanding of neutrophil behavior in chronic inflammatory contexts may reveal novel insights into disease pathology and identify new therapeutic targets.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, encompassing carcinogenesis, tumor progression, prognosis, and therapeutic response. The authors trace the historical development of the field from early observations of microbial presence in tumors through to landmark mechanistic discoveries, and they synthesize current understanding of how microorganisms modulate oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a promoter and inhibitor of tumor growth, and it explores how microbial communities influence responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by advocating for personalized, microbiota-targeted oncological approaches and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical application in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases
items.summary: This review examines organ cross-talk, the communication networks between organs that maintain physiological homeostasis and mediate responses to environmental stimuli. The authors comprehensively synthesized current research on inter-organ interactions, focusing particularly on the brain, heart, and gut as central nodes, and organized findings on axes including brain-gut, brain-liver, brain-heart, heart-kidney, heart-lung, heart-liver, gut-liver, gut-kidney, and gut-lung connections. The molecular signaling pathways underlying these interactions in both healthy and disease states were systematically elucidated, and the review visualizes the overall landscape of organ interaction research using a Sankey diagram. Based on these mechanistic insights, the authors propose multiple therapeutic strategies and highlight the technological advances enabling progress in this field. The review ultimately advocates for network-driven, systems biology approaches alongside personalized diagnostic and treatment strategies to better address complex multiorgan diseases.
items.citation: Huiting Che, Yidan Gao, Yonghu Xu et al.. Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species (ROS)
items.summary: This review paper examines the pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species, synthesizing a decade of advances to reframe ROS not as uniformly toxic molecules but as context-dependent mediators whose effects depend on the specific species produced, the subcellular compartment in which they arise, and the kinetics of their generation. The authors survey the major enzymatic sources of ROS and explain how compartmentalized hydrogen peroxide microgradients enable reversible signaling through cysteine modification, while iron-rich environments shift chemistry toward more destructive radical species that drive regulated cell death pathways including ferroptosis and parthanatos. These mechanisms are connected to disease processes spanning cardiovascular, metabolic, neurodegenerative, oncologic, pulmonary, renal, and critical illness contexts, with particular attention to crosstalk between ROS, reactive nitrogen and sulfur species, and iron metabolism. The paper critically evaluates measurement methodology, advocating for species-resolved and compartment-aware detection tools rather than generic oxidative stress assays, arguing that accurate attribution of damage to specific sources is essential for guiding therapeutic trials. On the therapeutic side, the authors argue against broad antioxidant supplementation in favor of targeted, time-staged interventions aimed at specific ROS-generating nodes and calibrated to biomarkers of flux and damage.
authors: José Manuel Pérez de la Lastra
items.citation: José Manuel Pérez de la Lastra, Celia María Curieses Andrés, Elena Bustamante Munguira et al.. Pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Archives of Toxicology. 2026

items.title: When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.
items.citation: Mezzadri L, Bonfanti P. When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.. Le infezioni in medicina. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor platform designed for rapid, point-of-care detection of antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogens. The researchers constructed disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, using non-covalent π-π stacking to attach the probes without disrupting the electrode's electronic properties or surface area. A key innovation was a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduced preparation time dramatically while maintaining sensor performance, and the platform achieved amplification-free and label-free detection of a colistin resistance gene with strong discrimination between target and non-target sequences. The biosensor was also readily retargeted to detect a different resistance gene simply by swapping the PNA probe, demonstrating broad adaptability. The platform showed consistent performance across independently fabricated sensors, supporting its potential for scalable manufacturing and decentralized diagnostic applications relevant to antimicrobial resistance and pandemic preparedness.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Rapid Molecular Diagnostics for Bloodstream Infection in Patients with Chronic Kidney Disease.
items.summary: This review examined the role of rapid molecular diagnostic technologies in detecting bloodstream infection pathogens and antimicrobial resistance markers, with a particular focus on patients with chronic kidney disease, including those on hemodialysis. The authors evaluated how these platforms, which enable faster identification of causative organisms and resistance determinants compared to conventional methods, can support earlier and more appropriate antimicrobial therapy. Evidence from clinical studies suggests that when combined with antimicrobial stewardship programs and applied following blood culture positivity, rapid molecular diagnostics facilitate timely therapy adjustments and may help minimize exposure to nephrotoxic agents in patients with impaired renal function. However, the review highlights important limitations, including variability in diagnostic accuracy across platforms, the inconsistent relationship between detected resistance genes and phenotypic susceptibility, and the predominance of evidence from general hospital populations rather than CKD-specific cohorts. The authors conclude that while these technologies can improve antimicrobial decision-making, their effectiveness requires integration with conventional microbiology and structured care pathways, and that further research in nephrology populations is needed to clarify clinical impact.
items.citation: Elbehiry A, Marzouk E, Abalkhail A et al.. Rapid Molecular Diagnostics for Bloodstream Infection in Patients with Chronic Kidney Disease.. Diagnostics (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Clinical and Economic Value of Rapid Microbiological Diagnostics in Bloodstream Infections: A State-of-the-Art Evidence Review with Emphasis on PCR and MALDI-TOF.
items.summary: This review examined the clinical and economic value of rapid microbiological diagnostic technologies, specifically polymerase chain reaction platforms and matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight mass spectrometry, in the management of bloodstream infections. A structured synthesis of evidence was conducted across multiple academic databases, incorporating economic evaluations and clinical studies reporting resource-use outcomes published over a roughly decade-long period. Rapid diagnostics consistently reduced time to pathogen identification and appropriate targeted therapy compared to conventional culture-based methods, with molecular platforms returning results within hours and the mass spectrometry approach achieving identification within an hour of culture positivity. Clinical benefits included earlier therapy optimization, reduced mortality, and shorter hospital stays, especially when these technologies were paired with antimicrobial stewardship programs. Economic analyses demonstrated improved cost-effectiveness through reductions in hospitalization duration, intensive care utilization, and antimicrobial expenditure, though the magnitude of benefit varied according to healthcare setting, available infrastructure, and how the diagnostics were integrated into clinical workflows.
items.citation: Raycheva R, Lengerova G, Petrov M et al.. Clinical and Economic Value of Rapid Microbiological Diagnostics in Bloodstream Infections: A State-of-the-Art Evidence Review with Emphasis on PCR and MALDI-TOF.. Microorganisms. 2026

items.title: Rapid Microbiological Diagnostics for Sepsis: Narrative Review of Current and Prospective Approaches.
items.summary: This narrative review examines current and emerging rapid microbiological diagnostic tools relevant to the diagnosis of sepsis, with the goal of providing critical care clinicians a practical introduction to these technologies. The authors surveyed peer-reviewed literature and organized diagnostic approaches into three broad categories: methods applied to incubated positive blood cultures, culture-independent nucleic acid detection methods, and antigen-based detection methods applied directly to clinical specimens. Blood culture-based approaches accelerate pathogen identification and antimicrobial susceptibility testing by measuring biological amplification signals more directly than traditional plate-culture workflows, while nucleic acid and antigen detection methods bypass culture entirely but involve tradeoffs in sensitivity, specificity, and clinical interpretation. The review concludes that refinements in blood culture-based diagnostics have substantially reduced time to actionable results, and that culture-independent approaches can deliver results within a few hours. However, the authors note considerable heterogeneity in the availability and applicability of these technologies, and that the aggregate clinical benefit of these interventions remains variable across settings.
items.citation: Harrison LB, Sohani ZN, Lasry D et al.. Rapid Microbiological Diagnostics for Sepsis: Narrative Review of Current and Prospective Approaches.. Critical care explorations. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic performance and cost-effectiveness of a combined rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing (RAST) and lateral flow immunochromatographic assay-based workflow for bloodstream infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated a diagnostic workflow combining EUCAST rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing with lateral flow immunochromatographic assays applied directly to positive blood cultures for the management of bloodstream infections. Using a retrospective single-center design with monomicrobial bacteremia episodes, researchers compared this combined approach against broth microdilution as a reference standard and assessed its clinical and economic value relative to alternative strategies including MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry alone and a molecular syndromic panel. The combined workflow demonstrated very high categorical agreement with definitive susceptibility testing and perfect concordance for detecting extended-spectrum β-lactamases and carbapenemases, with antimicrobial therapy appropriately modified in the majority of patients. Economic modeling indicated that adding lateral flow assays to MALDI-TOF identification and rapid susceptibility testing offered a favorable balance of cost and time gained compared to both simpler and more expensive molecular alternatives. The authors conclude that this combined approach provides rapid, accurate, and cost-effective microbiological guidance, representing a practical alternative to syndromic molecular panels for bloodstream infection management.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Hernández S, Albert E, Cano S et al.. Diagnostic performance and cost-effectiveness of a combined rapid antimicrobial susceptibility testing (RAST) and lateral flow immunochromatographic assay-based workflow for bloodstream infections.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
items.summary: The 2026 Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines represent a comprehensive international update to clinical guidance for the identification and management of sepsis and septic shock in adult patients, incorporating evidence published through mid-2025 and developed using GRADE methodology. The guideline panel evaluated existing and new evidence across domains including screening, infection management, hemodynamic support, respiratory care, adjunctive therapies, and long-term outcomes, producing a total of statements spanning strong recommendations, conditional recommendations, good practice statements, and acknowledgments of insufficient evidence. Notable new recommendations include prehospital sepsis screening and antibiotic administration for patients with prolonged transport times, age-stratified mean arterial pressure targets favoring permissive hypotension in older patients, targeted use of anaerobic antibiotic coverage based on infection risk factors, selective digestive decontamination in low-resistance settings, and active fluid removal after the acute resuscitation phase. Key changes from prior guidance include a shift away from routine empiric antifungal therapy, support for either invasive or noninvasive blood pressure monitoring, and a preference for crystalloids alone over albumin supplementation for fluid resuscitation, while recommendations for early antimicrobial administration and initial fluid resuscitation volumes were largely preserved from previous iterations.
items.citation: Hallie C. Prescott, Massimo Antonelli, Waleed Alhazzani et al.. Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026. Critical Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives
items.summary: Silver nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest as antibacterial agents capable of acting against a broad spectrum of pathogens, including antibiotic-resistant strains, making them potential alternatives to conventional antimicrobial therapies. This review examines the current literature on their antibacterial mechanisms, which include silver ion release causing membrane disruption, generation of reactive oxygen species, inhibition of protein synthesis, and interference with DNA replication. The physicochemical properties of these nanoparticles — particularly their shape, size, and surface characteristics — are identified as critical determinants of their efficacy and specificity. The paper also surveys their practical applications across agricultural, biomedical, and environmental domains, while acknowledging that inappropriate dosing or suboptimal formulation can result in significant toxicity, representing a major challenge for safe deployment. Future perspectives for optimizing their use and minimizing adverse effects are discussed.
items.citation: Hasan Karataş, Furkan Eker, Emir Akdaşçi et al.. Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bowel diseases: pathological mechanisms and therapeutic perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the pathological mechanisms underlying inflammatory bowel disease, which encompasses Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, and evaluates current and emerging therapeutic strategies for these conditions. The authors synthesize existing knowledge on the disease's pathogenesis, highlighting intestinal mucosal immune dysregulation, intestinal barrier damage, gut microbiota dysbiosis, reactive oxygen species homeostasis imbalance, and the interplay between genetic and environmental factors. A central concern addressed is that a substantial proportion of patients fail to respond adequately to existing biologics and small-molecule drugs, and that prolonged use of these agents frequently causes significant side effects. The review then outlines emerging treatment directions, including reactive oxygen species modulators, novel immune-targeted therapies, intestinal barrier repair agents, microbiota-directed interventions, multi-omics-based precision medicine, and artificial intelligence-assisted approaches. The authors argue that these strategies collectively offer a path toward transitioning from generalized treatment regimens to individualized precision medicine, thereby bridging the gap between basic research and clinical application.
items.citation: Xiaona Yang, Hong Guo, Min Zou. Inflammatory bowel diseases: pathological mechanisms and therapeutic perspectives. Molecular Biomedicine. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies
items.summary: This review examines the molecular and cellular mechanisms driving inflammatory bone loss in periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease initiated by dysbiotic oral biofilms that progressively destroy the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone. The authors focus particularly on osteoclastogenesis and the key transcriptional regulators and signaling pathways that govern osteoclast differentiation and activation, including the MAPK, JAK/STAT, NF-κB, and PI3K/Akt pathways alongside transcription factors such as NFATc1, c-Fos, and c-Src. The review synthesizes current understanding of how these pathways coordinate the inflammatory response and tissue destruction characteristic of periodontitis, drawing parallels with other osteolytic conditions such as osteoporosis and rheumatoid arthritis. Emerging therapeutic strategies are discussed, encompassing small molecules, biologics, and natural compounds designed to modulate these signaling axes. The authors conclude that while mechanistic insights have revealed promising therapeutic targets, translating this knowledge into effective clinical interventions remains a significant challenge requiring continued investigation.
authors: Jeroen J. J. P. van den Beucken
items.citation: Rafael Scaf de Molon, Rolando Vernal, Gabriela Ezequiel Oliveira et al.. Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies. Bone Research. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and its dysregulation across a range of diseases, with particular emphasis on immune cell biology. The authors survey the research history of lipid metabolism and outline how immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils, depend on lipid metabolites for proliferation, differentiation, and activation in both innate and adaptive immunity. The review also details the transport, signaling, and multilevel regulatory mechanisms governing lipid metabolism, spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational processes. These insights are applied to understanding pathological contexts such as autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. The authors conclude by surveying therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, the current state of clinical trials, and emerging directions including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel interventional approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review paper examines the emerging role of neutrophils as central orchestrators of chronic inflammation, moving beyond their classical characterization as short-lived innate immune cells that merely eliminate pathogens. The authors synthesize current knowledge on how neutrophils, through mechanisms including neutrophil extracellular trap formation, reactive oxygen species production, degranulation, and phagocytosis, can adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype under chronic conditions that drives sustained tissue damage rather than resolution. The review highlights how dysregulated neutrophil activation leads to aberrant cell death, uncontrolled oxidative bursts, and continuous release of proteases and inflammatory cytokines that perpetuate the inflammatory environment. Importantly, the paper describes how neutrophils actively engage in crosstalk with endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes, thereby bridging innate and adaptive immune responses and challenging the traditional dichotomy between these systems. The authors apply this framework to the pathophysiology of several immune-driven diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders, arguing that deeper understanding of neutrophil behavior in these contexts may reveal novel therapeutic targets.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Diameter truncated operator evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of simulating operator dynamics in out-of-equilibrium quantum systems, where the rapid growth of operator complexity typically makes exact simulation computationally intractable. Building on existing approaches that truncate operators based on their Pauli weight (the number of non-trivial terms in a Pauli string expansion), the authors introduce an alternative truncation scheme based on operator diameter, meaning the spatial extent of the region on the lattice over which an operator has non-trivial support. They provide theoretical justification for this approach using existing analysis of generic quantum circuits and validate it through extensive numerical simulations of the kicked Ising model and the Heisenberg XXZ model. The diameter-based truncation is shown to efficiently and accurately reproduce local correlation functions and transport properties, suggesting it captures the physically relevant dynamics while discarding less important contributions. The method offers a leaner computational protocol compared to weight-based truncation, broadening the toolkit available for studying quantum many-body dynamics.
items.citation: Tom Holden-Dye, Max Marvell, Joel Mills et al.. Diameter truncated operator evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Excitation of Collective Modes in a Chiral Superfluid by Thermal Quench
items.summary: This paper investigates what happens when a chiral superfluid, specifically helium-3 A phase, is rapidly cooled through a second-order phase transition using time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau field theory. The authors performed large-scale simulations of thermal quenches incorporating Gaussian space-time white noise to model the nonequilibrium dynamics of the condensate. They found that rapid cooling not only produces topological defects such as vortices and domain walls through the Kibble-Zurek mechanism, but also excites collective bosonic modes within newly formed chiral domains. For thermal states, the power spectral density of these bosonic excitations shows a sharp onset at the frequency corresponding to the bosonic mode mass, followed by a decay inversely proportional to frequency, with sensitivity to the Langevin damping rate. The dynamical scaling exponent governing the Kibble-Zurek freeze-out transitions smoothly between values corresponding to underdamped and overdamped regimes as damping increases, while the correlation length exponent remains approximately one-half regardless of damping.
items.citation: Noble Gluscevich, J. A. Sauls. Excitation of Collective Modes in a Chiral Superfluid by Thermal Quench. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe
items.summary: This white paper examines how next-generation radio interferometry can advance understanding of jetted active galactic nuclei in the early Universe, particularly at redshifts beyond six. The authors argue that relativistic jets from these systems play a crucial role in regulating star formation and driving the rapid growth of supermassive black holes in their host galaxies. They propose that combining the Square Kilometre Array with very long baseline interferometry will deliver sub-microjansky sensitivity alongside milliarcsecond angular resolution, enabling detailed imaging and characterization of these extreme sources. Such observations are expected to directly test models of supermassive black hole formation and evolution, including jet-assisted super-Eddington accretion scenarios, while also providing precise geometric constraints on the cosmological framework. The paper further emphasizes that synergies with current and future multiwavelength facilities will be essential for a comprehensive panchromatic understanding of the earliest jetted AGN and their environments.
items.citation: C. Spingola, M. Mezcua, Y. Liu et al.. SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool
items.summary: This paper addresses the growing mismatch between the accelerating pace of AI-assisted scientific output and the limited capacity of traditional human peer review. The authors propose a taxonomy of four progressive levels of AI-human collaboration in scientific evaluation to frame how review processes might evolve. They introduce the Paper Assistant Tool, an agentic AI framework that ingests full manuscripts and produces comprehensive evaluations by checking theoretical results, validating experiments, suggesting improvements, and identifying flaws through inference scaling techniques. The system demonstrated meaningful improvement over zero-shot recall for detecting mathematical errors on a dedicated benchmark, and pilot deployments at two major computer science conferences showed it could catch critical errors and suggest substantive improvements before submission. The authors argue that such tools can reduce the cognitive burden on human referees while preserving their ultimate control over review outcomes.
items.citation: Rajesh Jayaram, Drew Tyler, David Woodruff et al.. Towards Automating Scientific Review with Google's Paper Assistant Tool. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Early Optical Follow-up of Gamma-Ray Bursts: The Critical Role of Robotic Telescopes
items.summary: This review paper examines the early optical emission of gamma-ray bursts, focusing on the critical window spanning seconds to minutes after a burst when key physical information about the explosion is encoded in the light. The authors synthesize the observational phenomenology captured by ground-based robotic telescopes, which are uniquely suited to respond rapidly and autonomously to real-time alerts, enabling high-cadence photometry of transient features that would otherwise be missed. The review covers distinct early optical signatures including prompt emission coincident with the gamma-ray phase, reverse shock optical flashes, the onset of forward shock afterglows, and superimposed flares and plateaus, discussing the physical mechanisms underlying each. Early optical observations have constrained the initial bulk Lorentz factor of gamma-ray burst jets, the degree of ejecta magnetization, the circumburst density profile, and magnetic field geometry through polarimetry. The paper also surveys the technical capabilities of major robotic facilities and outlines future prospects in the context of upcoming missions and observatories operating across the electromagnetic and multi-messenger spectrum.
items.citation: Rahul Gupta, Judith Racusin, Gor Oganesyan et al.. Early Optical Follow-up of Gamma-Ray Bursts: The Critical Role of Robotic Telescopes. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Classical versus quantum Anderson localization in disordered systems
items.summary: This paper investigates Anderson localization in three-dimensional disordered systems by comparing classical scalar waves with electronic tight-binding models across different disorder types. The central finding is that the widely used mapping between classical-wave localization and the electronic Anderson model with diagonal disorder is mathematically unjustified, because classical-wave systems belong to a distinct disorder class governed by the acoustic sum rule, which correlates diagonal and off-diagonal matrix elements. Using a unified eigenvalue framework with complementary spectral, eigenvector, and level-statistics diagnostics, the authors construct localization phase diagrams for all four disorder classes considered. They find that classical-wave systems share qualitative features with electronic off-diagonal disorder—namely, that localized states appear only near band edges while extended states persist at strong disorder in the central spectrum—yet the acoustic sum rule produces localization topologies fundamentally different from both diagonal and off-diagonal electronic cases, including a previously unreported extended localized regime near the upper band edge for mass disorder. These results provide a revised and unified understanding of Anderson localization in quantum and classical wave systems, with implications for photonic and acoustic media.
items.citation: Stefano Mossa, Giancarlo Ruocco, Walter Schirmacher. Classical versus quantum Anderson localization in disordered systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research
items.summary: This review paper examines the development and applications of three-dimensional organoids and organ-on-chip technologies as advanced alternatives to traditional two-dimensional cell cultures and animal models in biomedical research. The authors survey the underlying manufacturing principles of these systems, including the use of stem cells and patient-derived samples to generate organoids that recapitulate complex human organ architecture, as well as micro-engineered chip devices that simulate tissue interactions, fluid dynamics, and mechanical forces. The paper highlights how these platforms are being applied to model a range of diseases, including neurological, liver, gut, cardiac, cancer, and infectious diseases, and how they support high-throughput drug screening and nanotoxicology studies. The authors further discuss the integration of complementary technologies such as CRISPR gene editing, multi-omics profiling, artificial intelligence, and biosensors as accelerants toward personalized medicine. Despite ongoing challenges related to standardization, scalability, and ethical considerations, the authors conclude that organoids and organ-on-chip systems represent a transformative shift in biomedical research with the potential to reduce reliance on animal models and improve the safety and efficiency of drug discovery.
items.venue: Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A
items.citation: Vignesh Sakthivel, Santhanam Sanjai Dharshan, S. Karthick Raja Namasivayam et al.. Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research. Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A. 2026

items.title: Next‐Generation Wearable Optical Sensors for Personalized Health and Point‐of‐Care Diagnostics—A Systematic Review
items.summary: This systematic review examines the current landscape of wearable optical sensors, which combine photonic detection with flexible, skin-compatible materials for real-time physiological and environmental monitoring. The authors analyze the full technology stack, from traditional and advanced optical materials such as metallic nanoparticles, quantum dots, two-dimensional materials, and metal-organic frameworks, through to sensing mechanisms including colorimetry, luminescence, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, photoacoustic sensing, and optical fiber-based approaches. The review also addresses device-level integration trends, including smartphone-compatible platforms, microfluidics, and machine learning for signal processing and pattern recognition. Application domains covered span non-invasive health diagnostics, fitness monitoring, environmental sensing, and food safety. Drawing across materials science, photonics, bioengineering, and data science, the review synthesizes design principles, performance metrics, and translational challenges to provide a guiding framework for the development of next-generation wearable photonic technologies.
items.citation: Rituparna Duarah, Helena Torné‐Morató, Guolong Zhang et al.. Next‐Generation Wearable Optical Sensors for Personalized Health and Point‐of‐Care Diagnostics—A Systematic Review. Advanced Healthcare Materials. 2026

items.title: Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system
items.summary: The paper investigated the fabrication of carbon microelectrodes derived from the photoresist SU-8 through pyrolysis and their application in a microfluidic system for measuring hematocrit. Conventional two-step pyrolysis approaches suffer from adhesion problems, geometric distortion, and insufficient surface area in low-aspect-ratio planar designs, so the researchers developed a three-step pyrolysis protocol to address these limitations. The resulting electrodes were integrated into a PDMS-based microfluidic device, where hematocrit was determined by measuring current responses of red blood cells suspended in phosphate-buffered saline, revealing a linear relationship between current and cell concentration. Surface characterization showed that the pyrolytic carbon electrodes had substantially greater roughness and thickness compared to platinum electrodes, yielding increased electroactive surface area that reduced charge-transfer and concentration overpotentials. The device achieved precision and accuracy for hematocrit determination comparable to platinum-based systems, demonstrating that this scalable, noble-metal-free fabrication approach holds strong translational potential for low-cost diagnostic microdevices.
items.citation: Lee HY, Rogers J, Kendrick C et al.. Fabrication of photoresist SU-8 pyrolyzed carbon electrodes and their integration in a microfluidic hematocrit detection system. 2026

items.title: Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.
items.summary: This review examines the application of microfluidic and microelectromechanical systems technologies to the diagnosis of invasive fungal respiratory infections in immunocompromised patients, a population for whom conventional diagnostic methods remain inadequate in terms of sensitivity, specificity, and accessibility. The paper surveys a range of emerging biosensing approaches, including on-chip nucleic acid amplification and antigen immunodetection, CRISPR-enhanced analytical systems, and host response profiling through transcriptomic, proteomic, and cytokine signatures integrated into lab-on-a-chip formats. Additionally, it covers MEMS-based strategies for real-time detection of exhaled volatile organic compounds, mechanical biosensing of fungal targets, and monitoring of device-associated biofilms. The review identifies persistent translational barriers including insufficient multicenter validation, biofouling from complex biological matrices, and a lack of standardized biomarker panels. The authors argue that combining artificial intelligence-driven multianalyte interpretation, multiplexed host-pathogen marker integration, and cartridge-based near-patient systems could collectively advance fungal diagnostics toward earlier and more individualized clinical intervention.
items.citation: Georgakopoulou VE, Pitiriga VC. Microfluidic and MEMS-Based Biosensing Platforms for Fungal Respiratory Infections in Immunocompromised Patients: Toward Rapid, Specific, and Minimally Invasive Diagnosis.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Microfluidic point-of-care testing for antimicrobial resistance: advances, strategies, and One Health perspectives.
items.summary: This review paper examined the role of microfluidic technologies in addressing antimicrobial resistance through point-of-care testing, framed within the One Health concept that recognizes the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health. The authors surveyed current microfluidic approaches encompassing sample processing methods, manufacturing techniques, and detection strategies relevant to rapid diagnostics. The review highlighted recent advances in microfluidic platforms designed specifically for detecting antibiotic resistance genes and performing antimicrobial susceptibility testing in a rapid and cost-effective manner. The paper argues that microfluidic point-of-care testing holds strong potential to improve early detection of antimicrobial resistance and guide treatment decisions. The authors also discussed outstanding challenges and future directions for translating these microfluidic technologies into practical diagnostic tools within a One Health framework.
items.citation: Wang C, Feng J, Zhou C et al.. Microfluidic point-of-care testing for antimicrobial resistance: advances, strategies, and One Health perspectives.. Science in One Health. 2026

items.title: Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases
items.summary: This review paper examines organ cross-talk, the communication networks between organs that maintain physiological homeostasis and coordinate responses to environmental stimuli. The authors synthesize current research on interactions among core organ pairs, with particular emphasis on the brain, heart, and gut, covering axes such as brain-gut, brain-liver, brain-heart, heart-kidney, heart-lung, heart-liver, gut-liver, gut-kidney, and gut-lung connections. The molecular mechanisms and signaling pathways underlying these interactions are systematically described in both healthy and disease states, with the landscape of organ interaction research visualized through a Sankey diagram. On the basis of these mechanistic insights, the review proposes multiple therapeutic strategies that exploit organ cross-talk as potential intervention points for complex diseases. The authors conclude by advocating for network-driven models, innovative diagnostics, and personalized treatment approaches grounded in systems biology to advance understanding and management of multiorgan diseases.
items.citation: Huiting Che, Yidan Gao, Yonghu Xu et al.. Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Raman Spectral Classification with Machine Learning
items.summary: This review paper examines the growing application of machine learning and deep learning methods to the classification of Raman spectral data. The authors conducted a structured narrative review to synthesize research progress, current trends, and future directions in this field, covering both traditional chemometric approaches and advanced deep learning architectures. They found that data-driven methods offer substantial advantages over traditional techniques by enabling automatic feature extraction from raw spectral data, overcoming limitations such as weak scattering signals, high dimensionality, and the need for expert-guided feature engineering. Key application domains highlighted include biomedical diagnostics, food safety and authentication, mineralogical classification, and plastic and microplastic identification. The review also identifies persistent challenges including limited training data availability, poor cross-dataset generalization, reproducibility issues, and the limited interpretability of deep learning models, and it outlines directions for future research to address these gaps.
items.citation: Yonghao LIU, Yizhan Wu, Junjie Wang et al.. Recent Advances in Raman Spectral Classification with Machine Learning. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Quantum spin resonance in engineered proteins for multimodal sensing
items.summary: This paper investigated quantum sensing capabilities of engineered magneto-sensitive fluorescent proteins, specifically focusing on a protein called MagLOV and its variants created through directed evolution. The researchers demonstrated that these proteins exhibit optically detected magnetic resonance in living bacterial cells at room temperature, with signal quality sufficient for single-cell detection, and that directed evolution can be used to tune their responses to magnetic fields and radio frequencies. The underlying mechanism was identified as the radical-pair mechanism involving the protein backbone and a bound flavin cofactor. By combining optically detected magnetic resonance with fluorescence magnetic-field effects, the authors showed multiple practical sensing applications, including spatially localized imaging analogous to magnetic resonance imaging using a genetically encoded probe, molecular microenvironment sensing, multiplexed bio-imaging, and lock-in detection strategies that help overcome common biological imaging problems such as light scattering and autofluorescence. Collectively, the findings establish a suite of quantum-based multimodal sensing modalities built around genetically encodable, room-temperature-compatible fluorescent proteins engineered for defined quantum-mechanical properties.
items.citation: Gabriel Abrahams, Ana Štuhec, Vincent Spreng et al.. Quantum spin resonance in engineered proteins for multimodal sensing. Nature. 2026

items.title: Commercial Translation of Electrochemical Biosensors: Supply Chain Strategy, Scale-Up Manufacturing, and Regulatory–Quality Considerations
items.summary: This review examines why so few electrochemical biosensors successfully transition from laboratory demonstrations to commercial products, despite their analytical maturity. The authors analyzed supply chain design, scale-up manufacturing strategies, and regulatory and quality considerations by drawing on a broad range of academic and industrial literature. The review compares two dominant manufacturing paradigms—roll-to-roll printing and semiconductor-derived microfabrication—evaluating how each aligns with different product targets, cost structures, and risk classifications, while arguing that manufacturing and quality should be treated as upstream optimization problems rather than afterthoughts. Regulatory pathways across major jurisdictions are compared to show how differences in device classification, required evidence, and quality management systems influence time-to-market and post-market flexibility. Case studies spanning disposable glucose strips, cartridge-based blood analyzers, and continuous monitoring systems illustrate how identical electrochemical principles can generate vastly different commercialization challenges depending on clinical context and system integration requirements.
items.citation: Gao Zhou, Haibin Liu. Commercial Translation of Electrochemical Biosensors: Supply Chain Strategy, Scale-Up Manufacturing, and Regulatory–Quality Considerations. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Early Optical Follow-up of Gamma-Ray Bursts: The Critical Role of Robotic Telescopes
items.summary: This paper presents a comprehensive review of early optical observations of gamma-ray bursts, focusing on the emission that occurs within seconds to minutes after a burst triggers detection. The authors examine how ground-based robotic telescopes, through rapid automated responses to real-time alerts and high-cadence photometry, are essential for capturing fleeting phenomena including prompt optical emission, reverse shock flashes, forward shock afterglow onset, and optical flares and plateaus. Each of these observational features carries diagnostic information about the physical properties of the GRB jet, central engine, and surrounding environment. Early optical observations have been used to constrain the initial bulk Lorentz factor of the jet, the degree of ejecta magnetization, the circumburst density profile, and magnetic field geometry through polarimetry. The review also surveys the technical capabilities of major robotic facilities and looks ahead to future prospects enabled by upcoming missions and observatories, as well as multi-messenger observing strategies.
items.citation: Rahul Gupta, Judith Racusin, Gor Oganesyan et al.. Early Optical Follow-up of Gamma-Ray Bursts: The Critical Role of Robotic Telescopes. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Classical versus quantum Anderson localization in disordered systems
items.summary: This paper investigates Anderson localization in three-dimensional disordered systems by systematically comparing classical scalar waves with electronic tight-binding quantum models across different disorder types. The authors demonstrate that the standard mapping between classical-wave localization and the electronic Anderson model with diagonal disorder is mathematically unjustified, because classical-wave systems obey an acoustic sum rule that correlates diagonal and off-diagonal matrix elements, placing them in a distinct constrained disorder class. Using a unified eigenvalue framework with complementary spectral, eigenvector, and level-statistics diagnostics, they computed localization phase diagrams for four disorder classes. They find that classical-wave systems qualitatively resemble electronic off-diagonal disorder models in that localized states appear only near band edges while extended states persist at strong disorder in the central spectrum, yet the acoustic sum rule produces localization topologies fundamentally different from both standard disorder classes. For mass disorder in particular, the correct formulation reveals an extended localized regime near the upper band edge that was absent in previous analyses, providing new insight into Anderson localization in photonic and acoustic media.
items.citation: Stefano Mossa, Giancarlo Ruocco, Walter Schirmacher. Classical versus quantum Anderson localization in disordered systems. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Parameter-Efficient Continuous-Variable Photonic Quantum Neural Networks for Edge Quantum AI: Demonstration in Oral Cancer Detection
items.summary: This paper investigated whether continuous-variable photonic quantum neural networks could enable parameter-efficient oral cancer detection from smartphone images in low-resource clinical settings. The researchers built a hybrid classical-quantum pipeline combining a lightweight classical feature extractor with dimensionality reduction and a parameterized photonic quantum classifier operating at room temperature, and they proposed a simplified quantum layer architecture that substantially reduces the number of trainable parameters compared to a standard reference design. They also identified strategies involving dimensionality reduction and encoding restrictions that dramatically alleviate the barren plateau problem, which typically impedes training of quantum neural networks. The simplified architecture's advantage over the full layer proved dependent on the number of quantum modes used, with the simplified version outperforming at higher qumode counts while using far fewer parameters. The best-performing model achieved the highest validation classification performance of all tested models and reached perfect calibrated test accuracy using only a small fraction of the parameters required by a classical baseline, supporting the viability of room-temperature photonic quantum machine learning for edge medical imaging applications.
items.citation: Akshay Bhagwan Sonawane, Sophie Choe, Lakshman Tamil. Parameter-Efficient Continuous-Variable Photonic Quantum Neural Networks for Edge Quantum AI: Demonstration in Oral Cancer Detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Noise-Directed Adaptive Remapping for Integer Optimization: from qubits to (encoded) qudits
items.summary: This paper extends a heuristic meta-algorithm called Noise-Directed Adaptive Remapping, which treats device noise as a useful computational resource rather than a nuisance, from binary optimization to general discrete integer optimization problems. The authors identify the theoretical requirements for this extension, including the feasibility of noise attractors, the existence of compatible gauge transformations, and systematic rules for selecting transformations at each iteration across different encodings. They analyze these criteria for several encoding strategies—qudit-native, binary, one-hot, and domain-wall—using the Max-k-colorable subgraph problem as a concrete test case. The findings reveal that different encodings exhibit distinct advantages and tradeoffs within the framework, particularly in how noise-driven dynamics interact with the optimization landscape. The work also proposes noise behavior as a new criterion for comparing encoding choices for quantum optimization hardware, and outlines paths toward experimental implementation in superconducting qudit devices.
items.citation: Stuart Hadfield, Filip B. Maciejewski, Davide Venturelli. Noise-Directed Adaptive Remapping for Integer Optimization: from qubits to (encoded) qudits. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Hybrid quantum-classical neural network for sample-efficient recognition of topological phases
items.summary: This paper addresses the growing challenge of characterizing complex quantum states produced by quantum computers, where direct measurement approaches become prohibitively costly at scale. The authors introduce a hybrid quantum-classical neural network combining a shallow parameterized quantum circuit with a classical neural network, where the quantum component performs a nonlocal transformation of the measurement basis that is jointly optimized with the classical network to maximize statistical distinguishability between quantum states. Using supervised learning, they demonstrate that this architecture can successfully distinguish topological phases, specifically differentiating the topological phase of the surface code from a symmetry-enriched topological phase and random product states. The hybrid approach substantially reduces the number of measurements needed for both training and inference compared to a purely classical neural network operating on randomized Pauli measurements. A key advantage highlighted is that the method relies on shallow quantum circuits compatible with existing quantum hardware, making it a practical route toward efficient quantum state characterization.
items.citation: Markus K. Hoffmann, Leon C. Sander, Colin Scarato et al.. Hybrid quantum-classical neural network for sample-efficient recognition of topological phases. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Direct Observation of X-ray Double-Slit Interference in Momentum Space
items.summary: This paper challenges the conventional understanding of Young's double-slit experiment as a phenomenon that requires spatial propagation to manifest interference fringes. The researchers used perfect-crystal diffraction to project the reciprocal-space profile of a hard X-ray beam immediately after it passed through a double-slit aperture, accessing the interference pattern directly in momentum space rather than in real space. By capturing the field's invariant momentum marginal, they resolved the complete double-slit fringe structure without any propagation arm, focusing optics, or imaging detector. The work demonstrates that wave interference is fundamentally a momentum-space phenomenon that can be observed independently of real-space propagation, and it establishes a compact, lensless method for coherence diagnostics in X-ray optics.
items.citation: Fugui Yang, Xiaoxiao Liang, Tianchong Zhang et al.. Direct Observation of X-ray Double-Slit Interference in Momentum Space. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review paper examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a versatile and transformative material for addressing challenges in energy, environment, and healthcare. The authors survey the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies that give graphene oxide its distinctive characteristics, including tunable band gap, high surface area, structural flexibility, and the ability to be functionalized across dimensional configurations ranging from zero-dimensional to three-dimensional nanostructures. The review covers a broad range of applications, including environmental remediation through adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of pollutants and microplastics, energy storage and conversion technologies such as photocatalytic hydrogen production and batteries, and sensing platforms including surface-enhanced Raman scattering for ultrasensitive molecular detection. The authors also highlight graphene oxide's promise in healthcare contexts and emphasize its potential for real-world and industrial deployment. Despite its considerable potential, the review acknowledges persistent challenges related to scalability, long-term stability, and reproducibility, and proposes possible solutions to enable sustainable future applications.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid, accurate detection of *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, a significant foodborne pathogen, by developing a one-pot CRISPR/Cas13-based biosensor capable of simultaneously targeting two key genetic markers that define its serotype. The system exploited the differing collateral cleavage preferences of two distinct Cas13 proteins to establish dual fluorescence channels, allowing serotype-specific identification while reducing false positives, with all steps—amplification, transcription, and CRISPR sensing—integrated into a single reaction tube. The biosensor achieved high sensitivity in detecting the pathogen and was validated using fecal samples from a mouse infection model, with results consistent with standard PCR methods. Compared to conventional CRISPR-based diagnostics, this dual-channel approach improves specificity and serotype discrimination while remaining simple, rapid, and suitable for field deployment in food safety, clinical, and surveillance contexts.
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.
items.summary: The study addressed the need for rapid, sensitive detection of *Escherichia coli* in neonatal sepsis, where conventional blood culture is slow and insufficiently sensitive. The researchers developed a multiple cross-displacement amplification assay coupled with a lateral flow biosensor targeting the *uidA* gene, using a ten-primer design optimized at a fixed temperature and completing the full workflow within approximately one hour. Analytical testing demonstrated femtogram-level sensitivity with no cross-reactivity against non-*E. coli* organisms. Clinical validation using blood and cerebrospinal fluid specimens from neonates with suspected *E. coli* infection showed complete concordance with quantitative PCR, and the new assay identified a substantially higher proportion of positive cases than conventional culture. The authors conclude that this simple, low-equipment method offers a rapid and cost-effective tool for point-of-care *E. coli* diagnosis in resource-limited neonatal settings.
items.citation: Xiao R, Zou P, Zhou J et al.. Multiple cross displacement amplification coupled with a lateral flow biosensor for ultra-rapid and highly sensitive detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> in neonatal sepsis.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.
items.summary: The study addressed the need for rapid and sensitive detection of *Salmonella typhimurium*, a foodborne pathogen responsible for illness outbreaks linked to contaminated food. Researchers developed a biosensor that integrates DNAzyme-catalysed amplification with a hybridization chain reaction cascade and CRISPR/Cas12a activity, converting pathogen-specific targets into branched DNA nanowire structures bearing PAM sequences that trigger Cas12a-mediated cleavage and restoration of a fluorescent signal. This layered signal enrichment strategy achieved an exceptionally low detection limit and a broad linear range spanning several orders of magnitude in bacterial concentration. The biosensor demonstrated straightforward operation, rapid response, and high specificity, and its modular design—allowing substitution of the recognition and primer sequences—suggests it could be readily adapted for sensitive detection of other foodborne pathogens.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Liu Y, Yue X, Li B et al.. Target-to-signal conversion and spatial enrichment cascade boost CRISPR/Cas12a biosensing for trace-level pathogen detection.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor platform designed for rapid, point-of-care detection of antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogen threats. The approach used disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, which were immobilized via non-covalent π-π stacking to preserve the electrode's electronic properties and high surface area. A key innovation was a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduced preparation time from many hours to just minutes without sacrificing sensor performance, enabling amplification-free and label-free detection of a colistin resistance gene with low picomolar sensitivity when using bacterial plasmids. The platform successfully discriminated the target sequence from a non-complementary sequence and was readily retargeted to detect a different resistance gene simply by swapping the probe, demonstrating its generic adaptability. The biosensors showed reproducible performance across independently fabricated devices, supporting the potential for scalable manufacturing of decentralized diagnostic tools for antimicrobial resistance surveillance and pandemic preparedness.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.
items.summary: This review examines advances in biosensor technologies specifically developed for detecting two high-priority foodborne pathogens, *Salmonella* spp. and *Listeria monocytogenes*, which are among the leading causes of foodborne illness and mortality globally. The authors systematically mapped trends in bioreceptor development and transducer selection as they apply uniquely to each pathogen, rather than broadly surveying biosensing across many organisms. A distinctive contribution is the analysis of how artificial intelligence tools can accelerate biosensor design, optimization, and decision-making for detecting these two pathogens specifically. The review critically evaluates which biosensing strategies hold genuine potential for scalability, portability, and practical deployment in food industry settings, addressing the persistent gap between laboratory-stage concepts and real-world implementation. The work positions itself as a practical roadmap intended to guide future research toward field-ready, industry-relevant detection solutions.
items.citation: Gumush SE, Al-Attar R, Sandbecker E et al.. Advanced biosensing strategies for high-risk foodborne pathogens: a comprehensive review of <i>Salmonella</i> and <i>Listeria monocytogenes</i>.. Food chemistry. Molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Theoretical Investigation of Early Cancer Biomarker Sensing Using a PMMA–Gold Hybrid Quasi-D-Shaped Photonic-Crystal-Fiber-Based Surface Plasmon Resonance Biosensor
items.summary: This paper proposes and numerically investigates a quasi-D-shaped photonic crystal fiber biosensor that combines surface plasmon resonance with a gold plasmonic layer and a polymethyl methacrylate polymer coating for the detection of cancer cells. Using finite element method simulations, the researchers systematically analyzed how plasmonic material thickness and air hole geometry influence key sensing parameters, including confinement loss, wavelength sensitivity, and amplitude sensitivity. They found that exceeding an optimal gold layer thickness increases confinement loss and reduces sensitivity by limiting evanescent field penetration depth, while geometric variations in air holes also meaningfully alter sensor performance, underscoring the need for precise structural optimization. The optimized design achieved very high wavelength sensitivity and amplitude sensitivity values for cancerous cell types such as MDA-MB-231, with refractive index resolutions in the range of millionths of a refractive index unit. The authors conclude that their sensor outperforms several recently reported photonic crystal fiber surface plasmon resonance designs and shows strong potential for early cancer detection through its sensitivity to refractive index changes associated with cancerous cells.
items.citation: Ayushman Ramola, Amit Kumar Shakya, Nezah Balal et al.. Theoretical Investigation of Early Cancer Biomarker Sensing Using a PMMA–Gold Hybrid Quasi-D-Shaped Photonic-Crystal-Fiber-Based Surface Plasmon Resonance Biosensor. Micromachines. 2025

items.title: Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future trajectory of three-dimensional organoids and organ-on-chip technologies as alternatives to traditional two-dimensional cell cultures and animal models in biomedical research. The paper surveys the underlying manufacturing principles of these platforms, describing how stem cell- or patient-derived organoids recapitulate complex human organ architecture and how micro-engineered organ-on-chip devices replicate tissue-tissue interactions, fluid dynamics, and mechanical forces. The authors highlight applications across a range of disease areas, including neurological, hepatic, gastrointestinal, cardiac, oncological, and infectious diseases, as well as high-throughput drug screening and nanotoxicology. The integration of complementary technologies such as CRISPR gene editing, multi-omics profiling, artificial intelligence, and biosensors is identified as accelerating progress toward personalized medicine. Despite ongoing challenges related to standardization, ethical considerations, and scalability, the authors conclude that these platforms represent a fundamental shift in biomedical research with the potential to reduce reliance on animal models and more faithfully simulate human physiology for drug discovery.
items.venue: Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A
items.citation: Vignesh Sakthivel, Santhanam Sanjai Dharshan, S. Karthick Raja Namasivayam et al.. Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research. Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines how the gut microbiome contributes causally to colorectal cancer development, progression, and treatment response through multiple interconnected biological axes. The authors synthesized molecular, ecological, and translational evidence describing how dysbiotic microbial communities reprogram immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine networks within the tumor microenvironment. Enrichment of specific pathobionts alongside depletion of short-chain-fatty-acid-producing commensals drives carcinogenesis through genotoxin-induced DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, chronic inflammation, and immune suppression, with bacterial metabolites such as colibactin and cytolethal distending toxin leaving identifiable mutational signatures in human cancer genomes. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia coordinate barrier breakdown and metabolic cooperation to sustain tumor growth. Building on this framework, the authors highlight microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—repositioning the microbiome as an actionable determinant of both cancer pathogenesis and treatment outcome.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions
items.citation: Lorenzo Galluzzi, Oliver Kepp, Laurence Zitvogel et al.. Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions. Cancer Cell. 2025

items.title: Recent Advances in Raman Spectral Classification with Machine Learning
items.summary: This review examined the use of machine learning and deep learning methods for classifying Raman spectral data, addressing longstanding limitations of traditional chemometric approaches in handling complex, high-dimensional, and nonlinear spectral information. Using a structured narrative review methodology, the authors surveyed progress in applying both classical machine learning models and advanced deep learning architectures to Raman spectral analysis. The review covers applications across biomedical diagnostics, food safety and authentication, mineralogical classification, and plastic and microplastic identification, illustrating the breadth of domains where these methods have been deployed. Data-driven approaches were found to enable automatic feature extraction from raw spectra, circumventing the need for expert-guided feature engineering and achieving high classification accuracy. The authors nonetheless identify persistent challenges including limited training data availability, poor cross-dataset generalization, reproducibility issues, and insufficient interpretability of deep models, and they outline directions for future research to address these gaps.
items.citation: Yonghao LIU, Yizhan Wu, Junjie Wang et al.. Recent Advances in Raman Spectral Classification with Machine Learning. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).
items.summary: This study investigated the causes of severe acute respiratory infections among hospitalized adults in northern Thailand during the post-COVID-19 pandemic period. Researchers retrospectively analyzed respiratory samples from adult patients admitted to a regional hospital in Chiang Mai with SARI symptoms, testing specimens using multiplex real-time PCR capable of detecting a broad panel of respiratory pathogens. Nearly half of patients tested positive for at least one respiratory pathogen, with adenovirus and rhinovirus emerging as the most commonly detected viruses, and co-infections present in a notable proportion of cases. Seasonal patterns were observed, with influenza A and rhinovirus peaking in January while adenovirus and enterovirus circulated more consistently throughout the surveillance period. The authors conclude that enhanced molecular diagnostics and pathogen-specific surveillance are needed to guide clinical management, particularly given the high rate of empiric antibiotic use and the likelihood that undetected bacterial or fungal infections account for many of the pathogen-negative cases.
items.citation: Kham-Kjing N, Kawila R, Tariyo P et al.. Etiology of severe acute respiratory infections among adults in northern Thailand using multiplex PCR: A post-COVID-19 surveillance study (2023-2024).. PloS one. 2026

items.title: VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.
items.summary: This paper addressed the bioinformatics bottleneck in real-time pathogen detection using Oxford Nanopore sequencing combined with multiplex PCR syndromic panels. The authors developed a visualisation tool adapted from existing RAMPART software that processes raw sequencing data and performs taxonomic assignment within minutes, providing dynamic tracking of sequencing runs and amplicon coverage through an interactive interface. Validation experiments using complex clinical and environmental samples demonstrated that the system reliably and accurately identified all pathogens present. The tool is designed to be user-friendly and accessible for deployment in both field and clinical settings, supporting rapid syndromic surveillance and outbreak response.
items.citation: Lechat P, Kwasiborski A, Vincent R et al.. VisPan: real-time visualisation of multiplex amplicon-based sequencing panels for rapid syndromic surveillance and pathogen detection.. Bioinformatics (Oxford, England). 2026

items.title: Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.
