items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the BioFire FilmArray RP2.1 plus multiplex PCR panel for detecting respiratory pathogens and explored whether its cycle threshold values, made accessible through the FIREWORKS software platform, could serve as a semi-quantitative measure of pathogen load. Using retrospectively collected specimens that were also tested by comparison methods including Cepheid and in-house real-time PCR assays, the researchers assessed qualitative agreement and performed exploratory quantitative correlation analyses across hundreds of PCR results. The panel demonstrated excellent qualitative agreement with comparator methods, with most discordant results occurring in specimens containing low pathogen loads. Exploratory analysis revealed strong correlations between BioFire-derived cycle threshold values and those from reference assays, suggesting a reproducible quantitative relationship across platforms. The authors caution that the quantitative findings are preliminary given the exploratory study design and inherent challenges of cross-platform comparisons.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Vitale A, Mahler Z, Loup V et al.. Performance evaluation of the BioFire respiratory panel 2.1 plus and exploratory analysis of Ct values for respiratory pathogen quantification.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.
items.summary: This study examined pediatric pneumococcal respiratory infections and their bacterial co-detections in the two years immediately following the COVID-19 pandemic, during which non-pharmaceutical interventions had disrupted normal respiratory pathogen circulation. Researchers retrospectively analyzed respiratory specimens from children aged up to eighteen years using a multiplex real-time PCR panel capable of detecting seven bacterial pathogens at a tertiary pediatric hospital in Romania across 2022 and 2023. They found a marked rise in pneumococcal positivity rates between the two years, alongside a significant increase in co-detection of Streptococcus pneumoniae with Haemophilus influenzae, while single pneumococcal detections declined proportionally. Community-acquired pneumonia diagnoses also rose substantially over the same period, and affected children were predominantly boys from urban areas in early childhood. The authors conclude that these findings reflect a post-pandemic bacterial rebound and support the use of syndromic multiplex PCR testing to guide rapid pediatric diagnosis and antimicrobial stewardship.
items.citation: Stavăr-Matei L, Țocu L, Nechita A et al.. Pneumococcal Detection and Bacterial Co-Detection in Children After COVID-19: A Two-Year Multiplex PCR Study.. Biomedicines. 2026

items.title: Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.
items.summary: This study investigated the distribution, co-detection patterns, and seasonal dynamics of respiratory pathogens in the post-pandemic period using syndromic multiplex PCR testing at a tertiary-care hospital in Türkiye. Researchers retrospectively analysed several hundred respiratory samples from patients with suspected respiratory tract infections using the BioFire FilmArray Respiratory Panel, evaluating pathogen frequencies, co-infection rates, demographic associations, and correlations between circulating pathogens. At least one pathogen was detected in just over half of samples, with Rhinovirus/Enterovirus being the most prevalent pathogen overall and the dominant partner in co-detections, which occurred in roughly one-fifth of positive samples. Marked seasonal variation was observed, with influenza A, RSV, and several other pathogens clustering in the cold season, while a strong positive correlation emerged between RSV and influenza A co-circulation. The authors conclude that syndromic multiplex PCR testing reveals high etiological diversity and frequent co-detections in real-world clinical settings, and note that many apparent co-infections may reflect sequential rather than simultaneous infections due to residual nucleic acid from recent illness.
items.citation: Aygar IS. Post-pandemic resurgence and co-detectiondynamics of respiratory pathogens revealed by syndromic multiplex PCR.. BMC microbiology. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology and their applications in laboratory medicine, with particular attention to how innovations in design, fabrication, and artificial intelligence integration have expanded diagnostic capabilities. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of developments in chip fabrication methods, including three-dimensional printing and modular flexible substrates, as well as the incorporation of biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. A central theme is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the creation of smart diagnostic platforms that can self-optimize, automate analysis, and support clinical decision-making across applications ranging from cancer diagnostics and infectious disease detection to point-of-care testing and personalized medicine. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, underscoring the broad translational potential of these systems. Current challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration are discussed alongside future directions for bringing next-generation microfluidic technologies into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease
items.summary: This review paper examines the complex relationships between the human microbiome, oxidative stress, and inflammation in the context of both health and disease. The authors synthesized current evidence on how microbial metabolites and specific probiotic strains help maintain redox homeostasis through mechanisms including immune regulation, modulation of inflammatory processes, and influence over mitochondrial reactive oxygen species production and antioxidant signaling. Conversely, the review explores how oxidative stress and inflammation can reciprocally alter microbial diversity and function, creating a self-reinforcing cycle that disrupts homeostasis. The paper highlights that while the microbiome is highly dynamic and shaped by numerous factors, its interplay with oxidative and inflammatory processes is increasingly recognized as central to both normal physiology and the development of disease.
items.citation: Lourdes Herrera-Quintana, Pablo Iturbe-Sanz, Jorge Olivares-Arancibia et al.. Interplay of Microbiome, Oxidative Stress and Inflammation in Health and Disease. Antioxidants. 2026

items.title: Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies
items.summary: Severe community-acquired pneumonia remains a significant public health burden due to its complications, treatment costs, and the growing challenge of resistant pathogens. The paper reviews current approaches to empirical antibiotic selection, noting that rapid molecular diagnostic tools may help guide therapy but require further evaluation for clinical utility and cost-effectiveness. Treatment duration is trending shorter and may be refined through clinical progression monitoring and biomarkers. The review also examines the role of immunomodulatory therapies, particularly corticosteroids, for which recent large randomized trials have produced conflicting results, with potential benefit possibly limited to patients with marked inflammatory responses and excluding those with influenza. Supportive care, especially tailored respiratory support, is emphasized as a cornerstone of management regardless of other therapeutic advances.
items.citation: Pierre-François Dequin, A Torres, M. Bassetti et al.. Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies. Intensive Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Contaminating plasmid sequences and disrupted vector genomes in the liver following adeno-associated virus gene therapy
items.summary: This paper investigated the mechanisms underlying liver toxicity observed in some patients following adeno-associated virus gene therapy. The researchers performed long- and short-read metagenomic sequencing of liver tissue from a child with spinal muscular atrophy type one who developed significant hepatitis after receiving a commercial AAV gene therapy. They discovered contaminating manufacturing plasmid sequences with complex structures and evidence of recombination, as well as extensive disruption, concatemerization, and vector-human genome fusion junctions in the vector genomes. A human betaherpesvirus was also detected in the liver tissue. The authors conclude that further investigation across more patients is needed to determine whether these manufacturing plasmid contaminants, helper viruses, or the complex concatemeric DNA structures observed contribute to the liver toxicity associated with AAV gene therapy.
items.citation: Sarah Buddle, Li-An K. Brown, Sofia Morfopoulou et al.. Contaminating plasmid sequences and disrupted vector genomes in the liver following adeno-associated virus gene therapy. Nature Medicine. 2026

items.title: Neutrophil-initiated nociceptive ingrowth orchestrates inflammation resolution to potentiate bone regeneration
items.summary: This paper investigated whether pain-sensing neurons (nociceptors) play an active role in regulating immune responses and bone regeneration, rather than merely signaling discomfort. The researchers discovered that a specific neutrophil subset drives the ingrowth of nociceptive nerve fibers into injured bone, which then acts as a negative feedback mechanism to resolve inflammation by stimulating osteoblasts to produce the pro-resolving mediator lipoxin A4. The mechanistic basis involves nociceptor-derived calcitonin gene-related peptide activating osteoblastic autophagy, which redirects arachidonate 5-lipoxygenase activity to favor lipoxin A4 biosynthesis over pro-inflammatory pathways. In patients with Type II diabetes, reduced nociceptive innervation in alveolar bone corresponded with impaired autophagy, heightened inflammation, and poor bone formation, suggesting this neuroimmune axis is clinically relevant. Activating nociceptive nerves through a spicy diet or topical application of a clinically approved receptor agonist improved alveolar bone healing in diabetic mice, pointing toward novel therapeutic strategies targeting this nerve-immune-bone interaction.
items.citation: Xuanyu Qi, Guangzheng YANG, Zeqian Xu et al.. Neutrophil-initiated nociceptive ingrowth orchestrates inflammation resolution to potentiate bone regeneration. Bone Research. 2026

items.title: SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities
items.summary: This review examines wastewater-based genomic surveillance as a strategy for tracking SARS-CoV-2 and other viruses at the community level, with particular attention to how it can detect known and emerging mutations and characterize circulating viral lineages. The authors survey current approaches across the full workflow, including sample preparation, sequencing technologies, and bioinformatics methods used to interpret complex wastewater samples. A central concern is the difficulty of distinguishing between closely related lineages and identifying genuinely novel ones from mixed environmental samples, challenges that are compounded by reduced clinical surveillance and continuous viral evolution. The review notes that many recently developed analytical methods have not been rigorously tested under these shifting conditions. Overall, the authors argue that wastewater genomic surveillance holds substantial promise for reinforcing public health monitoring systems, while calling attention to the methodological gaps that must be addressed to realize that potential.
items.citation: Viorel Munteanu, Michael A. Saldana, David Dreifuss et al.. SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities. Genome biology. 2026

items.title: First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.
items.summary: This paper investigated whether a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system could be integrated with lateral-flow immunoassays to improve their sensitivity, addressing a longstanding limitation of these widely used point-of-care diagnostic tools. The researchers used a poly(ethylene glycol)-dextran system to preconcentrate protein targets into the dextran-rich phase, then introduced the enzyme dextranase to degrade dextran and reduce the high viscosity that had previously prevented this type of system from being compatible with paper-based lateral-flow assays. Enzymatic and fluid-flow studies were conducted to optimize conditions, and the combined system was tested using the protein transferrin spiked in artificial saliva. The integrated approach achieved a substantial improvement in the limit of detection compared to a conventional lateral-flow immunoassay, and a simplified version with dextranase dehydrated directly onto the paper strip produced equivalent gains in sensitivity while reducing liquid-handling steps. This work represents the first successful coupling of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system with a lateral-flow immunoassay, expanding the toolkit of preconcentration strategies available for point-of-care diagnostics.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Nicklen FD, Hadeed CH, Zhang F et al.. First implementation of a polymer-polymer aqueous two-phase system to improve lateral-flow immunoassay sensitivity via dextranase integration on paper.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.
items.summary: This paper investigated the development of a rapid diagnostic test for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, a cancer with poor prognosis largely because it lacks reliable early-detection biomarkers. The researchers designed and optimized a quantitative colorimetric lateral flow immunoassay using gold nanoparticle conjugates to detect soluble AXL, a cleaved form of a receptor tyrosine kinase that circulates at elevated levels in pancreatic cancer patients. Systematic optimization of antibody concentrations, buffer composition, and membrane type improved assay sensitivity and reproducibility, and clinical evaluation showed the assay successfully distinguished cancer patients from healthy controls. Results were validated against standard enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay measurements, confirming the diagnostic relevance of the approach. The authors claim this represents the first quantitative lateral flow immunoassay for soluble AXL detection in plasma, and propose it as a portable, cost-effective point-of-care tool with potential to improve early diagnosis of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.
items.citation: Vázquez-Bellón N, Rodríguez-Núñez M, Martínez-Bosch N et al.. Lateral flow immunoassay detection of soluble AXL for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma diagnosis.. Talanta. 2026

items.title: Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.
items.summary: This paper addressed the limited sensitivity of conventional lateral flow immunoassays, which are hampered by weak optical signals, randomly oriented antibodies, and inconsistent nanoprobe fabrication. The researchers developed a dual-enhancement approach combining gold-decorated ferritin nanocages, where engineered ferritin displaying SpyTag peptides served as a template for controlled gold nanocluster growth, with SpyTag/SpyCatcher chemistry to achieve site-directed, covalent antibody conjugation. The gold deposition produced highly uniform core-shell nanostructures with amplified optical signals, while the oriented antibody immobilization markedly improved antigen-binding capacity compared to random adsorption. Testing against human chorionic gonadotropin as a model analyte, the optimized assay achieved a substantially lower detection limit than commercial gold nanoparticle-based strips and demonstrated reliable performance in urine samples with good spike-recovery rates. The authors conclude that this synergistic strategy offers a broadly applicable route to dramatically improving the sensitivity of point-of-care diagnostic platforms.
items.venue: Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications
items.citation: Yuan X, Zhu X, Xu Y et al.. Dual-enhancement lateral flow immunoassay <i>via</i> gold-decorated ferritin nanoprobes and SpyTag/SpyCatcher site-directed conjugation.. Analytical methods : advancing methods and applications. 2026

items.title: Evaluation of a Novel In Vitro Diagnostic Immunoassay for the Rapid Qualitative Detection of KPC, NDM, OXA-48-like, IMP and VIM Carbapenemases.
items.summary: This study evaluated a commercial lateral flow immunoassay designed for the rapid detection of five major carbapenemase families in Gram-negative bacteria, a group of pathogens that pose a significant clinical threat due to limited treatment options. The assay was tested against a panel of whole-genome sequenced Gram-negative bacterial isolates with reduced carbapenem susceptibility, spanning producers of KPC, NDM, OXA-48-like, IMP, VIM, and multiple carbapenemases. The assay demonstrated perfect specificity with no false positive results, but overall sensitivity was substantially lower than expected, falling well below levels required for reliable clinical use. False negatives were particularly frequent among IMP and NDM producers, with lesser but notable deficiencies for KPC, while VIM and OXA-48-like detection was more dependable. The authors concluded that the assay's insufficient sensitivity renders it unsuitable for routine diagnostic application, and they highlight the ongoing need for rigorous, independent evaluation of newly commercialized carbapenemase detection tests.
items.citation: Dunne Z, Oueslati S, Volland H et al.. Evaluation of a Novel In Vitro Diagnostic Immunoassay for the Rapid Qualitative Detection of KPC, NDM, OXA-48-like, IMP and VIM Carbapenemases.. Diagnostics (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Simplified Point-of-Care Testing for Human Pythiosis: Development of a Whole-Blood-Based Lateral Flow Immunoassay.
items.summary: The paper studied the development and clinical validation of a rapid point-of-care diagnostic test for human pythiosis, a life-threatening infection caused by *Pythium insidiosum* that is difficult to diagnose promptly using conventional culture-based methods. The researchers created a lateral flow immunoassay using secretory proteins from *P. insidiosum* conjugated to gold nanoparticles, designed to detect anti-*P. insidiosum* antibodies in whole blood or serum within five minutes. The assay was evaluated for analytical properties including detection limit, cross-reactivity with other infections, and interference from various sample conditions, then assessed clinically against confirmed positive and negative specimens. The test demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, with no cross-reactivity against other pathogens and no interference from anticoagulants, hemolysis, lipidemia, or icterus. The authors conclude that this simple, rapid assay could meaningfully support screening and triage for pythiosis, especially in settings where laboratory infrastructure is limited.
items.citation: Szekely J, Saelai K, Youngchim S et al.. Simplified Point-of-Care Testing for Human Pythiosis: Development of a Whole-Blood-Based Lateral Flow Immunoassay.. Diagnostics (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Advances in spherical nanoparticle-labeled lateral flow immunoassays in the field of clinical detection.
items.summary: This review examines the use of spherical nanoparticle labels in lateral flow immunoassays, a key point-of-care testing technology applied to pathogen diagnosis and biomarker detection. The authors conducted a bibliometric analysis of relevant literature spanning a decade to map research trends, then systematically reviewed preparation and optimization strategies for multiple classes of spherical nanomaterials, including noble metal, fluorescent, nanozyme-active, surface-enhanced Raman scattering active, and magnetic nanoparticles. The review also addresses how multimodal signal strategies—combining colorimetric, fluorescent, Raman, magnetic, and photothermal readouts—can synergistically enhance diagnostic performance, and how surface functionalization improves sensitivity and specificity. The authors conclude that spherical nano labels offer high clinical translatability and commercial potential, and they argue that this synthesis provides both theoretical grounding and technical guidance for advancing precision diagnostics and personalized home-based health monitoring.
items.citation: Zhang Y, He H, Wang J et al.. Advances in spherical nanoparticle-labeled lateral flow immunoassays in the field of clinical detection.. Journal of materials chemistry. B. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication—including three-dimensional printing and flexible substrates—alongside advances in biosensor integration and the incorporation of artificial intelligence and machine learning. These developments have enabled real-time detection of clinically relevant biomarkers such as circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes, supporting applications in cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, and personalized medicine. The convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence has produced so-called smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support, with emerging uses extending into neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The review also identifies persistent challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be broadly translated into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential
items.summary: This review paper examines the current state of electrochemical biosensors designed to detect protein biomarkers in blood for cancer diagnosis and prognosis. The authors surveyed general strategies for electrochemical detection of serum proteins and catalogued recent advances in liquid biopsy biosensors targeting cancer-related circulating proteins, noting that many approaches have achieved impressive analytical sensitivity. The review critically identifies key obstacles preventing these technologies from reaching clinical use, including matrix effects from complex biological samples, insufficient validation with patient-derived specimens, interpatient variability, and practical challenges around manufacturing, cost, and long-term device stability. Particular emphasis is placed on the analytical and clinical validation requirements necessary for biosensors to advance toward real-world diagnostic applications. The authors conclude that while electrochemical biosensors hold significant promise as non-invasive cancer screening tools, rigorous standardization of workflows and more extensive clinical validation remain essential steps toward commercialization and routine clinical translation.
items.citation: Michael E. J. Lopez Mujica, Elena E. Ferapontova. Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Nanomaterial‐Based Inkjet Printing for Electrochemical Sensing
items.summary: This paper provides a comprehensive review of inkjet printing as a fabrication and functionalization platform for electrochemical sensors, with particular focus on nanomaterial-based ink systems. The authors examine how picoliter-scale additive deposition enables precise control over electrode properties, integrating considerations of ink formulation, jetting dynamics, and substrate interactions with key performance metrics including sensitivity, detection limits, and reproducibility. The review compares inkjet printing favorably against conventional methods such as drop-casting and screen-printing, highlighting advantages in scalability, material economy, and manufacturing consistency. Special attention is given to advanced ink chemistries including carbon nanomaterials, MXenes, and hybrid bioinks, where deposition control directly governs electrode functionality. The authors conclude by outlining a development roadmap toward automated, digitally controlled, and sustainable sensor manufacturing for applications spanning wearable devices, biomedical diagnostics, food safety, and environmental monitoring.
items.citation: David Panáček, Massimo Urban, Alessandro Silvestri et al.. Nanomaterial‐Based Inkjet Printing for Electrochemical Sensing. Small. 2026

items.title: An All‐Soft Wearable Electrochemiluminescence Chip for Sweat Metabolite Detection
items.summary: This paper developed a fully soft, skin-conformable wearable chip that uses electrochemiluminescence to detect metabolites in sweat for real-time health monitoring. The device integrates a laser-patterned closed bipolar electrode array, a microfluidic sweat collector, and deep eutectic solvent-based ionogels as ECL emitters, with a design that physically separates sensing and reporting zones to reduce interference from the complex chemical composition of sweat. The ionogels improve signal stability and mechanical flexibility, enabling prolonged on-skin use without requiring external light sources or complex circuitry. Using glucose as a model analyte, the chip achieves specific detection through an enzymatic reaction across a wide linear concentration range and was shown to accurately track postprandial glucose fluctuations in real time. The authors demonstrate that this approach is manufacturable at very low per-unit cost, suggesting strong potential for personalized health monitoring and clinical diagnostics.
items.citation: Wei Nie, Jie Jiang, Xi Wei et al.. An All‐Soft Wearable Electrochemiluminescence Chip for Sweat Metabolite Detection. Advanced Science. 2026

items.title: From Neuron‐Centric to Glia‐Centric: How Aging Glial Networks Drive Neurodegenerative Disease
items.summary: This review examines the emerging shift from a neuron-centric to a glia-centric understanding of neurodegenerative disease, placing aging glial cells—particularly astrocytes—at the center of disease pathogenesis. The authors argue that senescent astrocytes accumulate during brain aging and drive neurodegeneration through mechanisms including stable cell cycle arrest, mitochondrial dysfunction, and the senescence-associated secretory phenotype, which collectively disrupt synaptic integrity, impair proteostasis, and sustain chronic neuroinflammation. Importantly, these glial changes are proposed to precede overt neuronal loss in conditions such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease, reframing glial dysfunction as an early and active contributor rather than a secondary consequence. The review also addresses strategies for identifying glial senescence using functional and molecular biomarkers, including circulating secretory factors and extracellular vesicles as systemic indicators. Finally, it surveys emerging therapeutic approaches—senolytics, senomorphics, and CAR-T-based immunotherapies—aimed at restoring glial homeostasis as a means of delaying brain aging and slowing neurodegenerative progression.
items.citation: Lívia de Sá Hayashide, Bruna Pessoa, Gustavo Dias et al.. From Neuron‐Centric to Glia‐Centric: How Aging Glial Networks Drive Neurodegenerative Disease. Journal of Neurochemistry. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of biosensing technologies designed to detect high-priority foodborne pathogens, including *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, and *Listeria monocytogenes*, in both food safety and clinical healthcare contexts. The authors surveyed advances across electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic sensing platforms, with particular attention to how functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering strategies have improved sensor sensitivity, specificity, and performance in complex food matrices. A central theme is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into these platforms, enabling real-time analytics, intelligent pattern recognition, and high-throughput multiplexed detection that transforms conventional biosensors into smart diagnostic devices. The review critically assesses practical challenges such as matrix interference, bioreceptor instability, manufacturing scalability, and the absence of regulatory standardization, while proposing mitigation strategies including ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, explainable AI, and printable electronics. Looking ahead, the authors advocate for self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen panel hubs, blockchain-based data integrity, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward regulation-ready, next-generation food safety monitoring systems.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This study explored the feasibility and acceptability of integrating a point-of-care triage test based on interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 (IP-10), a chemokine correlated with HIV viral load, into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique. Using a qualitative design nested within a clinical evaluation at two primary healthcare facilities, the researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV on antiretroviral therapy, as well as healthcare providers and programme stakeholders at various health system levels. Participants reported that while routine viral load monitoring was widely valued, it was hampered by laboratory delays, access difficulties, and health system constraints. The IP-10 point-of-care test was broadly acceptable across groups, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and enable timely clinical decisions, though concerns about test accuracy and the continued need for confirmatory viral load testing were raised. The authors conclude that careful integration of the IP-10 triage test into existing monitoring algorithms, accompanied by staff training, workflow adaptation, and clear patient communication, could improve monitoring efficiency and support differentiated care approaches by prioritising those most in need of confirmatory testing and adherence support.
items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This qualitative study investigated the barriers and facilitators that healthcare workers experience when implementing near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV in Tanzania. Researchers conducted clinic observations and in-depth interviews with healthcare workers at multiple time points across intervention sites participating in a larger cluster-randomised trial in East Africa, using a thematic framework analysis guided by the Measurement Instrument for Determinants of Innovations. The findings showed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround time and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both healthcare workers and patients, and was supported by compatibility with existing workflows, teamwork, and management commitment. However, implementation was hampered by prolonged patient waiting times, shortages of physical space and electricity, understaffing, and delays caused by sample transport to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full benefits of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires targeted investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling procedures, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe, a population known to have historically lower rates of viral control. Participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years, all receiving a dolutegravir-based antiretroviral regimen, were enrolled in a randomized controlled and observational cohort study, with point-of-care viral load testing and urine tenofovir assays used to objectively assess adherence alongside validated mental health screening tools. The researchers found high rates of viral suppression at baseline, with detectable urine tenofovir and higher self-reported adherence being the strongest predictors of suppression, while food security, financial security, and marital status were also significantly associated. Mental health outcomes were largely positive, with most participants reporting minimal depression and anxiety, though moderate stigma was common. These baseline findings suggest that dolutegravir-based regimens combined with supportive conditions can achieve strong viral control in this vulnerable peripartum population.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion
items.summary: This review examines T cell exhaustion, a dysfunctional immune state that arises during chronic infections and within tumor microenvironments and that substantially impairs antitumor and antiviral immunity. The paper synthesizes current understanding of the mechanisms driving exhaustion, encompassing external factors such as persistent antigen exposure, immune checkpoint signaling, and cytokine environments, alongside intrinsic cellular changes including metabolic alterations, epigenetic modifications, and transcriptional reprogramming. The authors survey therapeutic strategies designed to reverse this dysfunctional state, including immune checkpoint inhibition, cytokine therapies, metabolic reprogramming, and cell-based approaches, while also considering the implications of T cell exhaustion in cancer, chronic infections, and autoimmune diseases. Despite notable progress, the review highlights persistent challenges such as individual variability in treatment response, resistance mechanisms, adverse side effects, and difficulties in accurately measuring functional recovery and evaluating long-term therapeutic outcomes. The paper concludes by identifying future research directions aimed at improving T cell function and broadening the effectiveness of immune-based therapies.
items.citation: Yiran Wu, Yuchen Wu, Zhengyu Gao et al.. Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors surveyed the history of lipid metabolism research and synthesized knowledge on lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways, with particular emphasis on how lipid metabolites regulate the proliferation, differentiation, and activation of immune cells including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. The review explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms—spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels—that govern immune cell behavior through lipid metabolism. The authors further discuss how dysregulated lipid metabolism contributes to autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review synthesizes current and emerging therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches informed by ongoing clinical trials.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Promoting Human Milk and Breastfeeding for the Very Low Birth Weight Infant: Clinical Report
items.summary: This clinical report examines the nutritional and health needs of very low birth weight infants in the neonatal intensive care unit, focusing on the role of human milk and breastfeeding support. The report synthesizes evidence on the benefits of mother's own milk, appropriately fortified, as the optimal nutrition source for these infants, with pasteurized donor human milk recommended as an alternative when mother's own milk is unavailable, insufficient, or contraindicated. Key recommendations include continuing donor milk until the infant's risk for necrotizing enterocolitis diminishes, and supporting lactation through access to breast pumps, skin-to-skin care, early and frequent milk expression, and multidisciplinary team involvement. The report also addresses social inequities in human milk provision, calling for peer lactation support, interpreter services, and standardized approaches to identifying unmet family needs at the local level.
items.citation: Margaret G. Parker, Lisa M. Stellwagen, Emily R. Miller et al.. Promoting Human Milk and Breastfeeding for the Very Low Birth Weight Infant: Clinical Report. PEDIATRICS. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review paper examined recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine and clinical diagnostics. The authors conducted a comprehensive review covering innovations in chip design and fabrication methods, including three-dimensional printing and flexible substrates, as well as the integration of advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus was the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. The review found that these combined advances have substantially improved device sensitivity, automation, and clinical applicability across areas including cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, and emerging fields such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also identified ongoing challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be widely adopted in routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: CD4+ T cells mediate CAR-T cell-associated immune-related adverse events after BCMA CAR-T cell therapy
items.citation: Matthew Ho, Luca Paruzzo, Julia Han Noll et al.. CD4+ T cells mediate CAR-T cell-associated immune-related adverse events after BCMA CAR-T cell therapy. Nature Medicine. 2026

items.title: 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.
items.summary: This study examined how public health practitioners in Ontario, Canada assess and act on HIV-related risk when deciding whether to issue enforcement orders against people living with HIV. Using assemblage theory as a conceptual framework, the researchers conducted semi-structured interviews with public health personnel and analysed the data through reflexive thematic analysis. They found that risk determination is shaped by an interplay of biomedical factors, institutional practices, legal structures, and social discourses, with viral load, care discontinuity, co-infection with other sexually transmissible infections, and noncompliance all functioning as triggers for intervention. The study also found that the Undetectable = Untransmittable principle has begun to reshape how risk is understood and governed, though its adoption is uneven across public health units, resulting in inconsistent and sometimes coercive responses. The authors argue that conceptualising HIV risk as relationally produced rather than individually determined highlights the need for equity-based, nonpunitive public health approaches.
items.citation: Daroya E, French M, Hastings C et al.. 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.. Sociology of health & illness. 2026

items.title: Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.
items.summary: This paper examined persistent gaps in HIV viral suppression and disease burden within a large Ugandan HIV care cohort and across the East African region, despite near-universal adoption of dolutegravir-based antiretroviral therapy. The researchers analyzed routine clinical data from a decade-long cohort as well as conducting a systematic review and meta-analysis of regional studies, finding that viral non-suppression remained concentrated in predictable social and clinical risk groups, and that advanced HIV disease, tuberculosis, and non-communicable diseases continued to co-occur at significant levels within HIV care settings. Based on these findings, the authors propose a pragmatic, data-enabled, risk-stratified care model that uses electronic health record signals to trigger timely viral load follow-up, deliver targeted adherence and socioeconomic support bundles to high-risk clients, and integrate tuberculosis and non-communicable disease management into HIV platforms. They argue that translating antiretroviral scale-up into durable suppression requires accountable cascade monitoring through a focused dashboard rather than relying on regimen potency alone. The model is framed as a means to reduce preventable advanced HIV disease complications and accelerate progress toward global treatment targets.
items.citation: Namaganda MM, Nabende JN, Kateete DP et al.. Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.
items.summary: This study investigated whether point-of-care hepatitis B viral load testing could be feasibly integrated into antenatal care in Uganda to improve access to antiviral prophylaxis for pregnant women and reduce mother-to-child transmission. Conducted across ten high-volume hospitals in nine regions of the country, the two-phase study first verified the diagnostic accuracy of the Xpert HBV DNA assay against a standard centralized platform, then assessed its operational feasibility, usability, and cost within routine antenatal services. The Xpert assay showed full concordance with the reference platform, the vast majority of samples were processed and results returned to providers, and a substantial proportion of mothers received results on the same day of testing. The cost per test was comparable to centralized testing while offering meaningful advantages in turnaround time and reduced patient visits, though operational challenges such as supply stockouts and competing laboratory priorities were noted. The authors conclude that the Xpert point-of-care platform represents a timely, accurate, and operationally feasible approach to improving hepatitis B diagnosis, prophylaxis access, and prevention of mother-to-child transmission in Uganda and similar low-resource settings.
items.citation: Nabitaka LK, Kasone V, Olal E et al.. Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.. PLOS global public health. 2026

items.title: Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multi-antigen rapid diagnostic test for malaria in a region of southern Ethiopia where both *Plasmodium falciparum* and *Plasmodium vivax* co-circulate, complicating accurate diagnosis. The new Bioline Malaria multi-Ag RDT, which detects HRP2 alongside dual pLDH markers for both species, was assessed against expert microscopy and quantitative PCR in febrile patients attending health facilities during peak transmission season. The multi-antigen RDT showed high specificity and strong agreement with expert microscopy, but its sensitivity declined when qPCR served as the reference standard, revealing that submicroscopic and low-density infections were frequently missed. qPCR detected the highest overall malaria prevalence and identified more mixed infections than either RDT or microscopy. The findings support the continued use of RDTs for routine clinical diagnosis while underscoring the need for more sensitive tools in malaria surveillance and control programs operating in endemic settings.
items.citation: Bogale A, Mitiku A, Tefera S et al.. Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
items.summary: This study investigated circulating arboviruses in Haiti during a period when conventional surveillance was disrupted by security concerns, using an unconventional sample source to maintain public health monitoring. Researchers extracted nucleic acids from discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes collected from febrile patients across two Haitian departments, then screened these materials using a series of real-time PCR assays targeting flaviviruses, alphaviruses, and Oropouche virus, followed by next-generation sequencing and phylogenetic analysis of positive samples. Dengue virus serotype three was identified as the sole circulating arbovirus detected, with all positive samples originating from collections made in the most recent year of the study period, and genomic analysis placed these sequences within a broader Caribbean cluster consistent with a regional outbreak occurring at that time. No alphavirus or Oropouche virus infections were detected in the subset of samples tested for those pathogens. The findings demonstrate that discarded malaria rapid diagnostic tests can serve as a practical and informative surveillance tool for arboviral disease in resource-limited or politically unstable settings where conventional sampling infrastructure is unavailable.
items.citation: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G et al.. Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.. PLoS neglected tropical diseases. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, encompassing how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms by which the microbiota modulates oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of microbial communities in simultaneously promoting and inhibiting tumor growth, as well as their role in mediating responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved treatments and ongoing clinical investigations. The authors conclude by calling for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches to oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring
items.summary: This paper presents an IoT and AI-integrated wireless health monitoring framework designed for elderly and chronically ill patients in auxiliary care environments. The system uses ZigBee protocol to enable low-power, reliable transmission of physiological data — including heart rate, body temperature, and oxygen levels — from wearable sensors to a centralized cloud-based processing unit. Machine learning algorithms, specifically random forest, support vector machine, and logistic regression, were applied to detect health anomalies in real time, with the random forest model achieving particularly high accuracy and recall. The system was evaluated in a real-world care facility setting, demonstrating very low communication latency and minimal power consumption, making it suitable for long-term deployment. The authors claim their framework addresses key shortcomings of existing telehealth systems — such as excessive delays and high false alarm rates — while offering a scalable, modular architecture that can accommodate additional sensors like blood pressure and glucose monitors.
items.citation: B. V. Ravi Kumar, Mohammad Shahnawaz Shaikh. ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring. Emerging Science Journal. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent developments in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine. The authors surveyed innovations in chip design and fabrication—including three-dimensional printing, modular platforms, and flexible substrates—alongside advances in biosensor integration that enable real-time detection of circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central theme is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has produced smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support across applications such as cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, and point-of-care testing. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, illustrating the breadth of the technology's potential. The authors additionally address persistent challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be overcome to translate these platforms into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Antimicrobial peptides: natural templates for next-generation therapeutics against antimicrobial resistance
items.summary: Antimicrobial resistance poses an escalating global health threat as conventional antibiotics increasingly fail against multidrug-resistant pathogens, driven by misuse, weak regulation, and a stagnant drug discovery pipeline. This review examines antimicrobial peptides — including natural, synthetic, and computationally designed variants — as promising next-generation alternatives, highlighting their broad-spectrum activity against bacteria, fungi, viruses, parasites, and biofilms, alongside immunomodulatory functions. The paper discusses how structural features such as alpha-helices, beta-sheets, and cyclic motifs, together with selective binding to negatively charged microbial membranes, underpin their potency and lower propensity to induce resistance. Advances in chemical modification, nanotechnology, recombinant expression, and artificial intelligence-driven design have improved peptide stability, bioavailability, and efficacy, while synthetic derivatives and combination therapies further reduce toxicity. The authors conclude that despite ongoing challenges including degradation, short half-life, production costs, and microbial defence mechanisms, continued progress in high-throughput screening, structural biology, and structure-activity relationship studies positions antimicrobial peptides as critical tools in combating antimicrobial resistance.
items.venue: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
items.citation: Ng Ngashangva, Surmani Huidrom, Indira Devi. Antimicrobial peptides: natural templates for next-generation therapeutics against antimicrobial resistance. Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Large language models in global health
items.summary: This paper examines the emerging role of large language models in global health, with particular attention to their potential and limitations in low- and middle-income countries. The authors review current progress, barriers to deployment, and illustrative case studies, including mobile-based applications, hybrid artificial intelligence systems, and open-weight models being used to improve diagnostics and clinical decision-making in resource-limited settings. Key challenges identified include inadequate digital infrastructure, scarcity of locally relevant and multilingual training data, regulatory gaps, and insufficient local expertise and capacity-building support. The paper also highlights risks such as model hallucinations, equity concerns, and environmental impacts, arguing that rigorous validation, localized fine-tuning, and global governance frameworks are essential. The authors conclude that with appropriate safeguards, codevelopment partnerships, and strategic investment, large language models could help bridge health access divides, augment overburdened health workforces, and support achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals in underserved communities.
items.citation: Jasmine Chiat Ling Ong, Yilin Ning, Rui Yang et al.. Large language models in global health. Nature Health. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.
items.summary: This prospective multicenter study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of the GeneReady MTB assay for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis in sputum samples from patients presenting with active TB symptoms at three Chinese hospitals. Participants underwent parallel testing with GeneReady, smear microscopy, mycobacterial culture, and the Xpert MTB/RIF assay, allowing direct performance comparisons. Against a microbiological reference standard, GeneReady demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, with performance stratified by bacterial load showing greater sensitivity in smear-positive than smear-negative patients. Agreement between GeneReady and Xpert MTB/RIF was strong for both positive and negative results. The authors conclude that GeneReady offers diagnostic accuracy comparable to Xpert MTB/RIF while providing advantages in cost and ease of use, supporting its potential as an initial diagnostic tool for pulmonary tuberculosis in resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Shang Y, Tang Z, Deng Y et al.. Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings
items.summary: This paper developed and evaluated a portable, field-deployable diagnostic platform designed to detect tuberculosis and identify drug-resistant strains in resource-limited healthcare settings. The system uses loop-mediated isothermal amplification targeting two Mycobacterium tuberculosis genetic markers, integrated with a compact AI-enabled device and smartphone-based readout to enable point-of-care use without specialized laboratory infrastructure. Clinical evaluation across patient samples demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, and real-world deployment in a primary healthcare setting operated by minimally trained personnel achieved sensitivity and specificity benchmarked favorably against the established GeneXpert reference method. Crucially, the same platform architecture was extended without modification to drug-resistance profiling, using a probe-free allele-specific amplification approach to detect mutations associated with rifampicin and isoniazid resistance. The authors conclude that this unified, AI-assisted molecular diagnostic platform has strong potential to democratize tuberculosis care by enabling early screening and treatment guidance in high-burden, low-resource environments.
items.citation: Nag S, Banerjee S, Banerjee S et al.. Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings. 2026

items.title: A smear test aiding with an automated slide reader acquires equivalent sensitivity to MGIT960 culture in pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether an artificial intelligence-aided automated slide reader could improve the sensitivity of smear microscopy for pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis to a level competitive with standard culture methods. Sputum samples from suspected tuberculosis patients were prepared using both Ziehl-Neelsen and auramine O staining, then scanned by the automated system across varying numbers of fields, with manual fluorescence microscopy, liquid culture, and molecular testing serving as reference standards. The automated auramine O method scanning a defined number of fields achieved a detection rate comparable to liquid culture, substantially outperforming manual microscopy, while all methods maintained very high specificity against non-mycobacterial pulmonary disease. Expanding the number of fields examined significantly increased detection rates, and auramine O staining consistently outperformed Ziehl-Neelsen staining regardless of field count. The authors conclude that AI-aided automated auramine O smear microscopy offers a rapid, same-day, and cost-effective diagnostic approach suitable for both high-throughput laboratories and resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Zhang C, Wang H, Chen Y et al.. A smear test aiding with an automated slide reader acquires equivalent sensitivity to MGIT960 culture in pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis. . Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Computer-aided detection thresholds can guide repeat rapid molecular testing in TB screening.
items.citation: Datta B, Story A, Rogers E et al.. Computer-aided detection thresholds can guide repeat rapid molecular testing in TB screening.. IJTLD open. 2026

items.title: Progress in tuberculosis diagnosis and laboratory services in the Western Pacific Region: a situational analysis of seven high tuberculosis burden countries.
items.summary: This study examined the current state of tuberculosis diagnostic test availability and laboratory services across seven high-burden countries in the Western Pacific Region. A standardized survey was administered to national tuberculosis programme representatives in Cambodia, China, Lao PDR, Mongolia, Philippines, Papua New Guinea, and Viet Nam, supplemented by follow-up interviews and an in-person workshop. The assessment found strong uptake of WHO-endorsed rapid molecular tests for TB detection, though smear microscopy remains in use in remote areas, and drug susceptibility testing employs both molecular and phenotypic approaches. While testing for resistance to first-line TB drugs is broadly available, capacity for testing against newer and repurposed drugs is limited, and comprehensive testing at peripheral health facilities remains rare. The authors conclude that although significant investment has been made in regional TB diagnostics and laboratory infrastructure, sustained efforts are needed to ensure equitable access across all settings.
items.citation: MacLean EL, Oh KH, Rahevar K et al.. Progress in tuberculosis diagnosis and laboratory services in the Western Pacific Region: a situational analysis of seven high tuberculosis burden countries.. Tropical medicine and health. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors surveyed the research history of lipid metabolism and focused specifically on how lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways regulate the behavior of immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils, in both innate and adaptive immunity. They explored how lipid metabolites influence immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation across multiple regulatory levels, encompassing genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational mechanisms. The review further addresses the contribution of lipid metabolic dysregulation to conditions such as autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, cardiovascular diseases, aging, and metabolic disorders. The authors conclude by summarizing current therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, the state of clinical trials in this area, and future directions including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel therapeutic approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research
items.summary: This review paper examines the development and application of three-dimensional organoids and organ-on-a-chip technologies as advanced alternatives to traditional two-dimensional cell cultures and animal models in biomedical research. The authors describe how organoids derived from stem cells or patient tissue can faithfully recapitulate the complex cellular architecture and function of human organs, and how combining these with micro-engineered organ-on-a-chip devices enables dynamic simulation of tissue interactions, fluid flow, and mechanical forces. The paper surveys the manufacturing principles underlying these platforms and their deployment across a range of disease models, including neurological, liver, gut, cardiac, cancer, and infectious diseases, as well as their use in high-throughput drug screening and nanotoxicology. The authors highlight how integration with CRISPR gene editing, multi-omics profiling, artificial intelligence, and biosensors is accelerating progress toward personalized medicine and reducing dependence on animal preclinical testing. While acknowledging persistent challenges around standardization, scalability, and ethical considerations, the review concludes that these technologies represent a fundamental shift in biomedical research with strong potential to more accurately model human physiology and improve drug discovery.
items.venue: Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A
items.citation: Vignesh Sakthivel, Santhanam Sanjai Dharshan, S. Karthick Raja Namasivayam et al.. Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research. Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A. 2026

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Inflammation
items.summary: This paper examines the bidirectional relationship between insulin resistance and inflammation, exploring how each condition drives and sustains the other across multiple biological levels. The authors conducted a conceptual narrative review informed by targeted literature searches, synthesizing evidence from molecular signaling, intracellular stress pathways, tissue dysfunction, and systemic inflammatory processes. The review traces how chronic metabolically induced inflammation initiates and perpetuates insulin resistance through oxidative, endoplasmic reticulum, and fibrotic stress programs, which in turn impair insulin receptor signaling and promote endothelial dysfunction and atherogenesis. The authors propose an integrative framework in which these stress-response pathways become maladaptive under conditions of persistent nutrient excess and inflammatory signaling, creating self-reinforcing feedback loops that accelerate vascular and cardiometabolic injury. The framework is intended to identify mechanistic points of convergence that could guide future translational research and hypothesis testing in cardiometabolic disease.
items.citation: Evgenii Gusev, Alexey Sarapultsev, Yulia A. Zhuravleva. Insulin Resistance and Inflammation. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication and their potential as diagnostic and therapeutic tools in human disease. The authors comprehensively survey the major classes of extracellular vesicles, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and isolation methods, as well as omics-based profiling approaches. Particular attention is paid to the roles these vesicles play in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, along with the emerging clinical applications of mesenchymal stem cell-derived vesicles in regenerative medicine. The review also highlights photobiomodulation as a means of therapeutically modulating extracellular vesicle signaling, a relatively novel angle in the field. Finally, the authors identify critical translational barriers—including standardization, scalability, and regulatory validation requirements imposed by major oversight bodies—that must be addressed before exosome-based therapeutics can be broadly implemented in precision medicine.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine
items.summary: This paper examined whether current artificial intelligence technologies, specifically convolutional neural networks for medical imaging and large language models for clinical communication, are capable of replacing physicians in the near future. The authors conducted a narrative review of scientific literature covering AI performance in medical imaging, language model competence in medical knowledge tasks, generalization limitations, the absence of physical examination capabilities, and existing regulatory frameworks. The review found that AI systems perform well on narrowly defined tasks such as image interpretation, triage support, and documentation, reducing variability and improving workflow efficiency. However, significant barriers to physician replacement remain, including poor generalization beyond training data, inability to conduct physical examinations or procedures, susceptibility to hallucinations and overconfidence in language models, and unresolved questions of legal liability and clinical accountability. The authors conclude that AI will augment rather than replace physicians, with future integration requiring robust clinical validation, clear escalation pathways, and well-defined regulatory and ethical frameworks.
items.citation: Rafał Obuchowicz, Adam Piórkowski, Karolina Nurzyńska et al.. Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine. Diagnostics. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper examines the theoretical foundations underlying anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes, which are standard tools in sequential statistical testing. The authors reframe the standard construction—mixing exponential test statistics over a prior and applying Ville's inequality—as a two-player zero-sum game in which a Learner chooses a prior and Nature adversarially produces a score process, with information serving as the currency of exchange. A central pathwise Gibbs-variational identity is derived that holds along every realized path without expectation operators, and from which existing results such as Ville's inequality, the GROW criterion, and saddlepoint formulas all follow as special cases. The paper argues that the law of the iterated logarithm emerges as the minimax detection boundary of this game rather than as an arbitrary slack bound, and that the optimal mixing prior is not a free design choice but the unique equalizer strategy—the Jeffreys prior on the scale of scales—which is selected by the Erdős–Kolmogorov integral test and yields the sharp first iterated-logarithm correction with an explicitly derived coefficient. Established constructions including the Howard–Ramdas mixture, stitching, and betting confidence sequences are shown to be instances of this single equalizer principle, and a companion empirical evaluation confirms the central identities.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of composing multiple dexterous manipulation skills so that a single robotic hand can perform sequential multi-task manipulation without one skill interfering with another. The authors developed DexCompose, a framework that assigns explicit ownership of individual fingers to different tasks, identifying through release tests which fingers are essential for maintaining an already-completed skill state and reserving the remaining fingers for a new downstream task. The approach trains two asymmetric residual modules on top of frozen pretrained policies: one stabilizes the preserved skill within its assigned fingers, and the other adapts the downstream policy only within its allocated action subspace. Evaluated across a broad set of composite dexterous manipulation tasks combining object-retention skills with downstream interactions, the framework achieves strong average composite success rates. The results suggest that structuring action ownership at the finger level with dual residual modules is a promising strategy for composing dexterous skills beyond simple sequential policy chaining.
items.citation: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.. DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance
items.summary: This paper addresses a fundamental limitation of Force-Free Electrodynamics, which breaks down as a hyperbolic theory once the magnetically dominated condition of a plasma is violated. The authors propose that when magnetic dominance is lost, FFE can be continued by a theory of null electromagnetic fields, characterized by the condition that the principal null direction is tangent to a geodesic congruence, which in flat spacetime corresponds to requiring that drift velocity integral curves follow straight lines. They develop the mathematical structure of this null continuation theory and validate it by comparing its predictions against particle-in-cell simulations of colliding planar Alfvén waves and an exact FFE solution known to undergo finite-time loss of magnetic dominance. In both tests, the combined FFE plus null continuation framework shows strong macroscopic agreement with the PIC simulations, correctly capturing the emergence and evolution of null regions as well as current sheet formation. The results suggest this continuation provides a physically meaningful and computationally viable extension of FFE beyond the breakdown of magnetic dominance.
items.citation: Morifumi Mizuno. Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigates whether the neutral hydrogen 21-cm absorption line can be detected in the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts, focusing on the complicating role of interstellar scintillation rather than detector noise alone. The researchers built a simulation model of diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen to characterize under what conditions a realistic FRB spectrum would reveal the absorption feature. They found that detectability improves when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth is sufficiently different in scale from the absorption profile width, and that stacking repeat bursts from active repeating FRBs enhances the signal, provided the bursts are separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale. Cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds suggests one source may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud, making it a candidate target. Accounting for both scintillation and detector noise, a very large number of stacked bursts is required to achieve a confident detection with current telescopes, though improvements in sensitivity could eventually allow probing hydrogen clouds in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diameter truncated operator evolution
items.summary: This paper presents a method for simulating operator dynamics in out-of-equilibrium quantum systems, where the rapid growth of operator complexity normally makes such simulations computationally intractable. The authors introduce a truncation scheme based on operator diameter—the spatial extent of the region on a lattice over which an operator has non-trivial support—as an alternative to existing approaches that truncate based on operator weight, defined as the number of non-trivial terms in a Pauli string expansion. They argue, using analysis of generic circuits, that diameter-based truncation is physically well-motivated, and they validate the method through extensive numerical simulations of the kicked Ising model and the Heisenberg XXZ model. The approach is shown to efficiently and accurately reproduce local correlation functions and transport properties for these systems of interest.
items.citation: Tom Holden-Dye, Max Marvell, Joel Mills et al.. Diameter truncated operator evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Excitation of Collective Modes in a Chiral Superfluid by Thermal Quench
items.summary: This paper investigates what happens to a chiral superfluid, specifically helium-3-A, when it is rapidly cooled through a second-order phase transition using time-dependent Ginzburg-Landau field theory. The authors performed large-scale numerical simulations of temperature quenches with Gaussian space-time white noise to study the resulting nonequilibrium dynamics. They found that the quench not only produces topological defects such as vortices and domain walls through the Kibble-Zurek mechanism, but also excites bosonic collective modes of the newly formed chiral domains.
