For thermal states, the power spectral density of these bosonic excitations onsets sharply at the frequency corresponding to the bosonic mode mass before decaying inversely with frequency, and its character depends sensitively on the Langevin damping parameter. The study further reveals that the dynamical scaling exponent governing Kibble-Zurek freeze-out transitions smoothly between two limiting values as damping increases, while the correlation length exponent remains independent of damping.
items.citation: Noble Gluscevich, J. A. Sauls. Excitation of Collective Modes in a Chiral Superfluid by Thermal Quench. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges of Syphilis in a Resource-Limited Rural Setting: A Case Report.
items.summary: This case report examined the diagnostic and clinical management challenges of syphilis in a resource-limited rural setting. A middle-aged man presenting with nonspecific systemic symptoms — including weight loss, malaise, lymphadenopathy, and a trunk rash — underwent evaluation with a rapid dual treponemal test, which returned reactive for syphilis but negative for HIV. Because confirmatory nontreponemal assays were unavailable, clinicians were unable to follow standard diagnostic algorithms that require both testing modalities, yet proceeded with a presumptive diagnosis of secondary syphilis and administered intramuscular benzathine penicillin G. The case illustrates how overlapping clinical features can mimic other serious conditions such as tuberculosis, HIV, or lymphoma, complicating diagnosis in settings where laboratory capacity is restricted. The authors highlight that incomplete diagnostic pathways, limited treatment access, and barriers to follow-up collectively increase the risk of persistent infection and ongoing transmission in underserved populations.
items.citation: Medina Sanchez JA. Diagnostic Challenges of Syphilis in a Resource-Limited Rural Setting: A Case Report.. Cureus. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals tested at these sites, using statistical tests to explore whether demographic characteristics or symptom presence were associated with positive test results. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals who tested positive were referred for treatment within one day of diagnosis. No statistically significant associations were found between test positivity and demographic or symptom variables, though the authors note this finding was constrained by a small sample size. The study concludes that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing can facilitate rapid diagnosis and same-day treatment referral in outreach contexts, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion rates.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper reports on an expert consensus meeting convened to address the limitations of syndromic management of sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings and to identify strategies for transitioning to diagnostic-led care. Syndromic management, while inexpensive, results in overtreatment, undertreatment, and poor surveillance, but advances in point-of-care testing technologies for key STI pathogens now offer a potential alternative. The meeting brought together a large group of global and national experts across multiple disciplines to assess barriers and opportunities for introducing and scaling up STI point-of-care testing, with a particular focus on sub-Saharan Africa. Recommendations were organized across four levels—individual client, health service delivery, national, and global—covering areas such as improving patient knowledge, optimizing clinical workflows, enabling testing outside formal health facilities, updating treatment guidelines, strengthening laboratory quality assurance, and harmonizing international regulatory requirements. The authors conclude that coordinated multilevel action is needed to ensure equitable access to STI point-of-care diagnostics, which they argue will improve care quality, reduce antimicrobial resistance, and strengthen STI control.
items.citation: Manguro GO, Ray J, Schocken C et al.. From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Impact of decentralised, community-led molecular STI point-of-care testing on testing practices in remote First Nations communities in Australia.
items.summary: This study examined whether introducing molecular point-of-care testing for sexually transmitted infections improved testing rates and detection in remote Australian communities with high STI burdens among First Nations peoples. Using a retrospective interrupted time-series analysis of routinely collected clinical data from remote and regional clinics participating in a community-led programme, researchers assessed changes in testing volumes, positive test rates, and concurrent syphilis testing before and after the rollout of point-of-care testing for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and trichomonas. Following the introduction of point-of-care testing, there were immediate and sustained increases in monthly tests for chlamydia/gonorrhoea and trichomonas, as well as immediate increases in detected positive cases of gonorrhoea and trichomonas. Concurrent syphilis testing also increased among those who tested positive for chlamydia or gonorrhoea, suggesting improved opportunistic screening in this high-risk subgroup. The authors conclude that decentralised molecular point-of-care testing supports earlier diagnosis and treatment, reduces onward transmission risk, and warrants broader integration into primary care in high-burden settings.
items.citation: Tangey A, Smith K, Hengel B et al.. Impact of decentralised, community-led molecular STI point-of-care testing on testing practices in remote First Nations communities in Australia.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.
items.summary: This study examined sexually transmitted infection prevalence and the feasibility of point-of-care testing among women who inject drugs, a population that faces substantial barriers to sexual healthcare. Researchers implemented a mobile clinic in Seattle offering substance use and sexual health services, providing point-of-care testing for multiple STIs at enrollment and follow-up visits alongside participant and provider surveys. STI prevalence was notably high, with trichomonas being the most common infection, and positivity was associated with having multiple sexual partners and engaging in transactional sex. Same-day treatment was achieved in the vast majority of STI-positive cases, and most participants preferred point-of-care testing over traditional laboratory methods, though providers noted logistical challenges and competing participant priorities. The findings support point-of-care STI testing at low-barrier mobile clinics as an acceptable and feasible approach to improving sexual healthcare delivery for women who inject drugs.
items.citation: Fessler AG, Corcorran MA, Violette LR et al.. Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.. Sexually transmitted diseases. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline addressing urticaria, a mast cell-driven condition characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and a structured evidence-grading framework, with input from a large multinational group of delegates representing numerous national and international societies across dozens of countries, culminating in a formal consensus conference. The guideline covers the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing between acute and chronic forms, with chronic urticaria further divided into spontaneous and inducible subtypes. It provides evidence-based and expert-guided recommendations for diagnosing and managing the various urticaria subtypes, and acknowledges the availability of novel therapeutic options for a condition known to substantially impair quality of life and daily functioning.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: An epidemiological approach to sexually transmitted diseases – with special reference to contact tracing and screening
items.summary: This thesis examined epidemiological methods for controlling sexually transmitted diseases, with a focus on identifying asymptomatic infected individuals through contact tracing and screening. The research evaluated contact tracing routines for gonorrhoea and HIV, assessed chlamydia screening in family planning clinics to identify risk factors for infection, and compared epidemiological characteristics of chlamydia-infected women identified through screening versus those attending an STD clinic. Contact tracing proved effective in controlling a gonorrhoea outbreak and in identifying previously unknown HIV-positive individuals, while chlamydia screening revealed meaningful prevalence and identified several significant risk factors including young age, short relationship duration, and non-use of condoms. Although index patients found through screening had fewer recent partners than those attending STD clinics, the differences were considered too small to justify differing approaches to partner management. Analysis of partner choice data indicated that steady partner selection was relatively restricted while casual partner choice was more random, which does not support a purely random mixing model for the spread of sexually transmitted diseases.
items.citation: Kristina Ramstedt. An epidemiological approach to sexually transmitted diseases – with special reference to contact tracing and screening. Acta Dermato Venereologica. 2026

items.title: European Code Against Cancer, 5th edition – cancer‐causing infections and related interventions
items.summary: This paper presents updated recommendations from the fifth edition of the European Code Against Cancer regarding infections that cause cancer across European Union countries. The authors examined the burden of cancer attributable to five major pathogens — *Helicobacter pylori*, human papillomavirus, hepatitis B and C viruses, and HIV — finding that these collectively account for a meaningful proportion of all EU cancers, primarily affecting the stomach, cervix uteri, and liver, with the highest burden in southern and eastern regions. Since the previous edition of the Code, substantial advances have been made in prophylactic vaccines, curative treatments, and antiviral therapies that can either eliminate or significantly reduce the carcinogenic consequences of these infections. Despite growing accessibility and affordability of these interventions, implementation across EU member states remains slow, requiring greater public awareness, political commitment, and cost-effective screening and treatment programmes. The updated Code issues two concrete recommendations: vaccination of both girls and boys against HBV and HPV at nationally recommended ages, and participation in testing and treatment programmes for HBV, HCV, HIV, and *H. pylori*.
items.citation: Catharina J. Alberts, Paul Bloem, Silvia de Sanjosé et al.. European Code Against Cancer, 5th edition – cancer‐causing infections and related interventions. Molecular Oncology. 2026

items.title: Integrative Proteomics Reveal Neuroimmune and Dopaminergic Alterations Across the Nociceptive Neuraxis in Neuropathic Pain
items.summary: This study investigated the molecular changes occurring across the full pain-processing pathway in neuropathic pain, from peripheral nerves through the spinal cord to higher brain regions involved in emotional and cognitive processing. Using a rat model of chronic constriction injury, the researchers applied high-resolution mass spectrometry-based quantitative proteomics to simultaneously profile protein alterations in the sciatic nerve, spinal cord, and orbitofrontal cortex, complemented by behavioral assessments and immunofluorescence validation. Across all three regions, proteomic analysis revealed enrichment of neuroinflammatory pathways, with S100 proteins notably elevated throughout the neuraxis alongside markers of astrocyte and microglial activation, indicating a widespread and persistent inflammatory state. Strikingly, the orbitofrontal cortex showed prominent upregulation of dopamine-regulating proteins, implicating reward-related analgesic circuits in the affective dimension of chronic pain. The authors propose that neuropathic pain is sustained by coordinated neuroimmune activation and dopaminergic remodeling spanning the entire nociceptive neuraxis, and they identify glial S100 proteins and dopamine modulators as candidate therapeutic targets.
items.citation: Shreyasi Majumdar, Santosh Kumar Prajapati, Aishwarya Dande et al.. Integrative Proteomics Reveal Neuroimmune and Dopaminergic Alterations Across the Nociceptive Neuraxis in Neuropathic Pain. Cells. 2026

items.title: The Global Epidemiology of Antimicrobial Resistance: Trends, Determinants, and Public Health Implications
items.summary: This narrative review examined the global epidemiology of antimicrobial resistance, synthesizing evidence on its trends, underlying drivers, and public health consequences across human, animal, and environmental domains. Drawing on peer-reviewed literature, international surveillance data, and policy reports spanning a decade, the authors applied a One Health framework to integrate findings across these interconnected systems. Resistance was found to emerge from a complex interplay of inappropriate antimicrobial use, inadequate infection prevention, environmental contamination, and weak governance, with the greatest burden falling on low- and middle-income countries. Despite the existence of global action plans and stewardship programs, progress has been hampered by inequitable resource distribution, limited diagnostic capacity, and fragmented policy implementation. The review concludes that reversing current resistance trends will require strengthened surveillance, responsible antimicrobial use, investment in vaccine and drug development, and coordinated political commitment guided by One Health principles.
items.citation: Saud Lafi Oudah Alenzi, Ali Hedayan Alharthi, Raad Awwadh Alnefaie et al.. The Global Epidemiology of Antimicrobial Resistance: Trends, Determinants, and Public Health Implications. Cureus. 2026

items.title: Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis)
items.summary: This review examines a range of mosquito-borne diseases transmitted by *Aedes*, *Anopheles*, and *Culex* mosquitoes, encompassing both viral arboviruses and parasitic infections. The paper synthesizes information on the clinical features, global distribution, and transmission dynamics of these diseases, highlighting how environmental factors such as temperature and humidity, alongside urbanization and climate change, shape their spread beyond traditional tropical regions. Control strategies reviewed include vector management, vaccines, chemoprophylaxis, and emerging novel technologies. The authors also address persistent challenges such as insecticide resistance and the substantial socioeconomic burden these diseases impose globally. The review concludes by emphasizing the necessity of integrated, multifaceted interventions to address these ongoing public health threats.
items.citation: Yu-Lou Wei, Zhaoyu Wu, Runle Li et al.. Review of selected mosquito-borne diseases: arboviruses (dengue, chikungunya, Zika, West Nile, Japanese encephalitis, yellow fever) and parasitic diseases (malaria, lymphatic Filariasis). Frontiers in Public Health. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper studies the theoretical foundations of sequential detection boundaries, specifically the law of the iterated logarithm (LIL), by recasting the construction of anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes as a two-player game between a Learner and an adversarial Nature. In the game-theoretic framework proposed, the Learner commits to a mixing prior over tilting scales while Nature adaptively produces a difficult score process, with their interaction governed by a pathwise Gibbs-variational identity that unifies several known results including Ville's inequality and the saddlepoint formula as special cases. The paper argues that the LIL emerges not as arbitrary slack but as the minimax boundary of this sequential detection game, and that the optimal mixing prior is not a free design choice but rather a forced equalizer strategy uniquely determined by the requirement that every boundary-crossing time be equally costly for Nature. This equalizer prior yields a sharp closed-form correction to the iterated-log boundary and corresponds exactly to the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales in the log-log chart, with the Erdős-Kolmogorov integral test serving as the selection criterion. Existing constructions such as Howard-Ramdas mixtures, stitching, and betting confidence sequences are unified as instances of this equalizer principle, and a companion empirical evaluation confirms the central identities.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of composing multiple dexterous manipulation skills so that a single robotic hand can perform multi-task manipulation without the skills interfering with one another. The proposed framework, DexCompose, reuses pretrained full-hand policies by explicitly assigning finger-level action ownership, identifying through release tests which fingers are necessary to maintain an already-completed skill state and reserving the remaining fingers for a new downstream task. Two asymmetric residual modules are then trained: one stabilizes the preserved skill within a bounded action space, while the other adapts the downstream policy only within the action subspace allocated to the new task. The framework was evaluated across a broad set of composite dexterous manipulation tasks combining object-retention skills with downstream interactions, achieving a high average composite success rate. The results suggest that explicit structural ownership of finger actions, combined with dual residual learning, is an effective approach for composing dexterous skills beyond simple sequential policy chaining.
items.citation: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.. DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance
items.summary: This paper addresses a fundamental limitation of Force-Free Electrodynamics, which governs electromagnetic fields in magnetically dominated plasmas but breaks down when magnetic dominance is lost. The authors propose that once this condition fails, the evolution can be continued using a theory of null electromagnetic fields characterized by the requirement that the principal null direction is tangent to a geodesic congruence, which in flat spacetime corresponds to the drift velocity integral curves being straight lines. They develop the mathematical structure of this null continuation theory and validate it by comparing its predictions against particle-in-cell simulations of colliding planar Alfvén waves and an exact analytic solution that undergoes finite-time loss of magnetic dominance. The combined force-free and null continuation framework reproduces key macroscopic features seen in the simulations, including the emergence and growth of the null region and the formation of a current sheet. These results suggest the null field theory provides a physically meaningful and computationally useful extension of force-free electrodynamics beyond the regime of magnetic dominance.
items.citation: Morifumi Mizuno. Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigated the detectability of the neutral hydrogen 21-cm absorption line in the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts, focusing specifically on the complicating role of diffractive scintillation alongside detector noise. The researchers built an efficient model to simulate diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen and characterized the conditions under which a realistic FRB spectrum could reveal the absorption feature. They found that detection is feasible when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs substantially in scale from the absorption profile width, and that stacking repeat bursts from active repeating FRBs improves sensitivity, provided the bursts are separated by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale to avoid correlated frequency modulations. Cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds suggested that one well-studied repeating source may have its sightline intersect a Galactic molecular cloud. Under combined scintillation and noise conditions, thousands of stacked bursts are required to achieve a robust detection with current and planned telescopes, though improvements in detector sensitivity could eventually allow probing of neutral hydrogen clouds in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe
items.summary: This paper examines how future SKA-VLBI observations can advance understanding of jetted active galactic nuclei in the early Universe, particularly at redshifts above six. The authors argue that the relativistic jets produced by these systems play a critical role in regulating star formation and the rapid growth of supermassive black holes in their host galaxies during the earliest cosmic epochs. By combining the sub-microjansky sensitivity of the SKA with milliarcsecond angular resolution from very long baseline interferometry, the proposed observations would enable direct characterization of Doppler boosting, jet duty cycles, and jet-environment coupling at extreme distances. The paper claims these observations can test models of supermassive black hole formation and evolution, including jet-assisted super-Eddington accretion phases, and can independently constrain the geometry of the Universe with high precision. The authors also emphasize that synergies with other multi-wavelength facilities will be essential to build a comprehensive picture of the first jetted AGN and their environments.
items.citation: C. Spingola, M. Mezcua, Y. Liu et al.. SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A perfectly matched layer for damping vertically propagating waves in the compressible Boussinesq equations
items.summary: This paper investigates the use of the perfectly matched layer technique as an alternative to conventional sponge layer approaches for preventing spurious wave reflections at the upper boundary in atmospheric fluid models. The authors derive perfectly matched layer formulations for both linear and nonlinear versions of the compressible Boussinesq equations, introducing a novel variant for the nonlinear case that damps perturbations relative to a hydrostatically balanced reference state. The equations are discretized using a compatible finite element method and tested in numerical experiments. Results demonstrate that the perfectly matched layer outperforms standard Rayleigh or Laplacian damping sponges in absorbing acoustic waves near the model top, even within a thin damping layer, and can suppress spurious standing wave patterns arising from the reflection of orographic gravity waves. The authors conclude that the perfectly matched layer is a promising candidate for further development as a sponge layer replacement in operational atmospheric dynamical cores.
items.citation: Timothy C. Andrews, Kenneth Duru, David Lee. A perfectly matched layer for damping vertically propagating waves in the compressible Boussinesq equations. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.
items.summary: This nationwide cross-sectional study examined the relationship between menstrual-related disorders—specifically endometriosis and dysmenorrhea—and sexually transmitted infections among young women in Japan. Using a large insurance claims database, researchers compared the prevalence of five sexually transmitted infections between women with and without these menstrual conditions, and further assessed whether hormonal therapy use modified any observed associations. Women with menstrual-related disorders showed substantially higher prevalence of all five infections compared to those without such disorders, with endometriosis showing the strongest association, particularly for genital chlamydia infection. The elevated infection prevalence was consistent across both endometriosis and dysmenorrhea subgroups, and hormonal therapy use had only a minimal effect on these patterns. The authors conclude that gynecologic care for women with these menstrual conditions should incorporate routine sexually transmitted infection screening and reproductive health education, while noting that healthcare-seeking behavior and diagnostic patterns may influence how these associations appear in claims-based data.
items.citation: Yoshihara T, Owada S, Arita H et al.. Association between menstrual-related disorders and sexually transmitted infections: A nationwide cross-sectional study in Japan.. PloS one. 2026

query: CRISPR based diagnostics infectious disease detection

items.title: CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.
items.summary: This review examined the progress of CRISPR-Cas technologies in addressing infectious diseases across three domains: diagnostics, therapeutic genome editing, and vaccine development. The authors surveyed translational advances over the past decade, with particular attention to diagnostic platforms such as DETECTR and SHERLOCK for pathogen detection, CRISPR-Cas9 strategies for targeting viral genomes and antimicrobial resistance, and the engineering of live-attenuated and personalized neoantigen vaccines. A key finding is that CRISPR-based diagnostics have advanced furthest toward clinical application, having moved from proof-of-concept to preclinical stages, while therapeutic and personalized vaccine applications remain at earlier, more exploratory phases due to delivery and safety constraints. The review uniquely integrates all three application areas within a single infectious disease framework and critically evaluates their respective levels of maturity alongside technical, regulatory, and ethical barriers. The authors conclude that although CRISPR-Cas holds transformative potential for infectious disease management, challenges including delivery efficiency, off-target effects, and ethical regulation must be resolved to achieve safe and equitable clinical translation.
items.citation: Mayuri K, Saravanan KM, Somala CS et al.. CRISPR-Cas and Infectious Diseases: A Decade of Translational Advances in Molecular Biotechnology.. Biochemical genetics. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors systematically organized their analysis around three core operational steps: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluated approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, emphasizing their suitability for simplified and equipment-free deployment. Amplification strategies reviewed include both thermal cycling methods such as PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all adapted for portable and low-power conditions. On the detection side, the review covers a spectrum from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms leveraging CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.
items.summary: This study addressed the need for rapid, accurate detection of *Escherichia coli* O157:H7, a significant foodborne pathogen, by developing a one-pot CRISPR/Cas13-based biosensor. The system simultaneously targets two key genetic markers of the pathogen by exploiting the distinct collateral cleavage preferences of two different Cas13 proteins, creating dual fluorescence channels that enable serotype-specific identification while reducing false positives. Target gene amplification, transcription, and CRISPR-mediated detection were all integrated into a single reaction tube, removing the need for complex equipment and minimizing contamination risk. The biosensor achieved high sensitivity in laboratory testing and was validated using fecal samples from a mouse infection model, with results consistent with standard PCR methods. The authors conclude that this dual-channel approach improves specificity and serotype discrimination compared to conventional CRISPR diagnostics and offers a simple, field-deployable platform suitable for food safety monitoring, clinical diagnostics, and infectious disease surveillance.
items.citation: He Y, Lin X, Zhang X et al.. Simultaneous Dual-Gene Detection of <i>Escherichia coli</i> O157:H7 Based on a CRISPR/Cas13-Mediated Biosensor.. JACS Au. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with particular attention to the growing challenge of antimicrobial resistance and the inadequacy of conventional culture-based methods. The authors conducted a structured comparative analysis of major molecular platforms, including various forms of PCR, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key performance parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capability. The review also critically assessed the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support. Additionally, the translational feasibility of deploying these technologies at the point of care was considered alongside clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude that advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics requires balancing analytical performance with clinical interpretability, and they outline remaining challenges and future directions for the field.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.
items.summary: This review examines how synthetic biology is transforming point-of-care in vitro diagnostics by enabling programmable, modular biosensing systems that overcome the limitations of conventional centralized assays. The authors organize synthetic biology-based diagnostic platforms into four functional layers—molecular recognition, signal transduction and amplification, output generation, and system integration—and discuss four core enabling technologies: cell-free protein synthesis systems, aptamer and riboswitch sensors, CRISPR-Cas diagnostic platforms, and microfluidic integration. Clinical applications spanning infectious disease detection, cancer biomarker analysis, and drug metabolism and toxicity screening from recent years are surveyed to illustrate real-world relevance. The review also addresses practical translational challenges beyond analytical sensitivity, including matrix tolerance, workflow complexity, manufacturability, quantitative capability, and regulatory readiness. Future directions identified include artificial intelligence-assisted biosensor design, multimodal readout strategies, interoperable platform architectures, and rigorous clinical validation to advance these programmable systems toward widespread deployment.
items.citation: Bao C, Zhang H, Jiang L et al.. Synthetic Biology-Enabled Biosensing Platforms for Point-of-Care In Vitro Diagnostics: Programmable Modules, Clinical Applications, and Translational Challenges.. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion
items.summary: This review paper examines T cell exhaustion, a dysfunctional immune state that arises during chronic infections and within tumor microenvironments, undermining effective antitumor and antiviral immunity. The authors synthesize current knowledge of the mechanisms driving exhaustion, encompassing both external factors such as persistent antigen exposure, immune checkpoint signaling, and cytokine milieu, as well as cell-intrinsic changes including metabolic dysfunction, epigenetic remodeling, and transcriptional reprogramming. The review surveys therapeutic strategies developed to reverse exhaustion, including immune checkpoint inhibition, cytokine therapies, metabolic reprogramming, and cell-based approaches. Despite documented progress, the authors highlight persistent challenges such as individual variability in treatment response, resistance mechanisms, adverse effects, and difficulties in reliably measuring functional T cell recovery. The paper also outlines future research directions intended to improve therapeutic efficacy and deepen understanding of T cell exhaustion across cancer, chronic infection, and autoimmune disease contexts.
items.citation: Yiran Wu, Yuchen Wu, Zhengyu Gao et al.. Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research
items.summary: This review paper examines the development and applications of three-dimensional organoids and organ-on-chip technologies as advanced alternatives to traditional two-dimensional cell cultures and animal models in biomedical research. The authors survey the underlying manufacturing principles of these systems, including the use of stem cells and patient-derived samples to construct organoids, as well as micro-engineered organ-on-chip devices that replicate tissue-tissue interactions, fluid dynamics, and mechanical forces. The paper covers a broad range of applications, including disease modeling for neurological, liver, gut, cardiac, cancer, and infectious conditions, alongside high-throughput drug screening and nanotoxicology. The authors highlight how integrating these platforms with CRISPR gene editing, multi-omics profiling, artificial intelligence, and biosensors is accelerating progress toward personalized medicine and reducing reliance on animal testing. While acknowledging ongoing challenges related to standardization, ethical considerations, and scalability, the review concludes that these technologies represent a transformative shift in biomedical research by more accurately recapitulating human physiology.
items.venue: Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A
items.citation: Vignesh Sakthivel, Santhanam Sanjai Dharshan, S. Karthick Raja Namasivayam et al.. Advances in <scp>3D</scp> Organoids and Organ‐on‐a‐Chip Systems for Biomedical Research. Journal of Biomedical Materials Research Part A. 2026

items.title: Next-Generation Strategies for Controlling Foodborne Pathogens: Precision Antimicrobials, Biofilm Disruption, and Emerging Molecular Interventions
items.summary: This review examines the growing inadequacy of conventional chemical preservatives and sanitizers against foodborne pathogens, particularly in light of increasing antimicrobial resistance and the protective role of biofilms in food processing environments. The authors surveyed a broad range of emerging biological, nanotechnological, and plant-derived strategies, including bacteriophages, phage-derived enzymes, bacteriocins, quorum-sensing inhibitors, and nano-enabled delivery systems, evaluating their capacity for selective pathogen control with reduced collateral effects on beneficial microbiota. The review also assessed advances in anti-biofilm surface engineering and the integration of these tools into coordinated multi-hurdle systems supported by real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. A notable emphasis is placed on digital frameworks such as digital twins of food-processing lines as mechanisms to connect pathogen detection, risk prediction, and targeted intervention in an adaptive manner. The authors conclude by identifying persistent knowledge gaps, regulatory hurdles, and the need for standardized validation and collaborative translation efforts to move precision food-safety technologies from laboratory promise to reliable real-world application.
items.citation: Ayman Elbehiry, Ahmed I. Alajaji. Next-Generation Strategies for Controlling Foodborne Pathogens: Precision Antimicrobials, Biofilm Disruption, and Emerging Molecular Interventions. Foods. 2026

items.title: Engineering the next generation of CAR T- cells: precision modifications, logic gates and universal strategies to overcome exhaustion and tumor resistance
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of chimeric antigen receptor T-cell therapy, focusing on the engineering innovations designed to overcome the limitations that have prevented success in solid tumors. The authors comprehensively survey advances in CAR receptor architecture, including modifications to extracellular binding domains, hinge and transmembrane regions, and intracellular signaling motifs, as well as the incorporation of alternative protein scaffolds. The review covers logic-gated control systems such as synNotch receptors, inhibitory CARs, and modular adaptor platforms that allow context-dependent T-cell activation to improve tumor specificity and reduce toxicity to healthy tissue. Strategies to combat T-cell exhaustion are also discussed, including intrinsic checkpoint rewiring, cytokine armoring, and epigenetic reprogramming, alongside the development of allogeneic and off-the-shelf cell products derived from donors, stem cells, natural killer cells, and macrophages. The authors conclude that integrating these diverse engineering approaches positions next-generation CAR T-cell therapies as programmable immunotherapeutic platforms capable of extending durable clinical responses beyond blood cancers to solid tumors.
items.citation: Juan Esteban García-Robledo, Sergio Cabrera-Salcedo, Andreas Michael Brandauer et al.. Engineering the next generation of CAR T- cells: precision modifications, logic gates and universal strategies to overcome exhaustion and tumor resistance. Frontiers in Oncology. 2026

items.title: Innovative Strategies to Overcome Stability Challenges of Single-Atom Nanozymes
items.summary: This review examines single-atom nanozymes, a class of catalytic nanomaterials in which individual metal atoms are dispersed on a carrier to maximize atom utilization and enable precise control of metal-carrier interactions relevant to biomedicine. The authors surveyed the current landscape of biomedical applications, spanning disease diagnosis through biosensors and imaging, antitumor strategies including photothermal, photodynamic, sonodynamic, and immunotherapies, as well as antimicrobial and anti-oxidative stress treatments. A central focus of the review is the identification of stability limitations that hinder clinical translation, such as metal atom clustering, loss of active sites, ligand bond breakage at elevated temperatures, poor environmental tolerance, biosecurity concerns, and diminished long-term catalytic performance. To address these challenges, the authors propose several innovative strategies encompassing synthesis process optimizations such as space-limited approaches, coordination site design, bimetallic synergistic effects, defect engineering, and atom stripping-capture methods, alongside surface modification and dynamic responsive design. Collectively, these strategies are presented as a roadmap toward developing stable and clinically viable single-atom nanozymes.
items.citation: Rong Guo, Qiuzheng Du, Yaping He et al.. Innovative Strategies to Overcome Stability Challenges of Single-Atom Nanozymes. Nano-Micro Letters. 2026

items.title: Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer
items.summary: This review examines cuproptosis, a recently characterized form of regulated cell death triggered by copper overload that disrupts mitochondrial function through aggregation of lipoylated tricarboxylic acid cycle enzymes and destabilization of iron-sulfur cluster proteins. The paper synthesizes current mechanistic understanding of how copper homeostasis, metabolic reprogramming, and key signaling pathways including p53, HIF-1α, Wnt/β-catenin, and AKT govern this cell death modality. The authors also explore how copper shapes antitumor immunity within the tumor microenvironment, implicating cuproptosis in cancer immunotherapy contexts. Therapeutic approaches centered on copper ionophores and nanomedicine delivery platforms are reviewed as strategies to selectively induce cuproptosis across multiple cancer types. The review concludes that while preclinical evidence is promising, successful clinical translation will depend on biomarker-driven patient selection and optimized drug delivery systems to realize the potential of cuproptosis in precision oncology.
items.citation: Qian Hao, Yu Gan, Xiang Zhou. Tackling cuproptosis: from metabolic rewiring to therapeutic exploitation in cancer. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper examines the theoretical foundations underlying anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes, which are standard tools in sequential statistical testing. The authors recast the standard recipe of mixing exponential test statistics over a prior as a two-player game between a Learner who commits to a mixing prior and an adversarial Nature who produces a difficult score process, with information serving as the currency of exchange. A central pathwise identity, the Gibbs variational identity, is shown to unify previously separate results including Ville's inequality, the GROW criterion, and saddlepoint approximations as special cases. The key findings are that the law of the iterated logarithm emerges as the minimax detection boundary of this game rather than as an arbitrary technical artifact, and that the optimal mixing prior is not a free design choice but is uniquely determined as the equalizer strategy—the prior that makes every boundary-crossing time equally costly—which coincides with the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales and yields a sharp closed-form correction term. The framework unifies a range of existing constructions, including Howard-Ramdas stitching and betting confidence sequences, as instances of this equalizer principle, and is supported by companion empirical verification of the central identities.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: PerceptionRubrics: Calibrating Multimodal Evaluation to Human Perception
items.summary: This paper introduces a new benchmark framework designed to better align multimodal model evaluation with human perceptual standards, addressing the problem of models achieving high scores on existing benchmarks while still failing on real-world visual tasks. The framework pairs a large set of information-dense images with thousands of instance-specific rubrics derived from high-quality captions produced through a novel peer-review consensus pipeline, organizing criteria into essential facts and fine-grained detail categories. A distinctive gated scoring mechanism penalizes failures on mandatory visual facts sharply rather than averaging performance linearly, making the evaluation more sensitive to critical perceptual errors. The evaluation reveals that models frequently verify isolated image elements correctly but fail when strict conjunctive constraints are applied, exposing brittleness in visually dense scenarios, and that a meaningful perception gap persists between open-source and proprietary models. The authors conclude that their gated metrics align more closely with human judgment than conventional benchmarks, arguing that strict perceptual fidelity must be treated as a prerequisite for reliable multimodal generation.
items.citation: Yana Wei, Hongbo Peng, Yanlin Lai et al.. PerceptionRubrics: Calibrating Multimodal Evaluation to Human Perception. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigates whether the neutral hydrogen 21-cm absorption line can be detected in the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts, focusing specifically on how interstellar scintillation affects this detectability. The authors constructed a simulation model of diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen to characterize under what conditions a realistic FRB spectrum would reveal the absorption feature. They found that detection is most favorable when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs substantially in scale from the width of the absorption profile, and that stacking repeat bursts from active repeating FRBs improves detection provided the bursts are separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale. Cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds suggests that one particular repeating source may have its sightline intersecting a Galactic molecular cloud. Accounting for both scintillation and detector noise, the study concludes that a very large number of stacked bursts is required to achieve a statistically significant detection with current telescopes, though improvements in detector sensitivity could eventually enable probing of neutral hydrogen clouds in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diameter truncated operator evolution
items.summary: This paper introduces a method for simulating operator dynamics in out-of-equilibrium quantum systems, where the rapid growth of operator complexity ordinarily makes exact simulation intractable. Rather than truncating operators based on their weight, which counts the number of non-trivial terms in a Pauli string expansion, the authors propose truncating based on operator diameter, defined as the spatial extent of the region on the lattice where an operator has non-trivial support. The authors provide theoretical motivation for this approach using existing analysis of generic quantum circuits and validate it through extensive numerical simulations on the kicked Ising model and the Heisenberg XXZ model. They demonstrate that diameter-based truncation efficiently and accurately reproduces local correlation functions and transport properties, performing comparably to or better than weight-based approaches while offering a leaner simulation protocol.
items.citation: Tom Holden-Dye, Max Marvell, Joel Mills et al.. Diameter truncated operator evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Optimal Deployment of Electric Aircraft for Canadian Domestic Flights
items.summary: The paper investigates how to optimally transition regional aviation fleets from conventional to electric aircraft, using a multi-period mixed-integer linear programming framework that simultaneously optimizes fleet acquisition, charging infrastructure deployment, and service allocation subject to emissions, service share, and budget constraints. A real-world case study based on a short-haul helicopter network in British Columbia was used to test the model's applicability. The results indicate that electrification can achieve substantial emissions reductions within a five-year horizon while remaining economically feasible. However, the primary bottleneck for transition was found to be fleet capacity and operational structure rather than charging infrastructure availability, causing unmet demand when aircraft are replaced on a one-to-one basis. The authors conclude that successful electric aviation transitions require coordinated planning across fleet sizing, scheduling, and route prioritization rather than focusing on infrastructure alone.
items.citation: Elham Soufiani, Mehrdad Pirnia. Optimal Deployment of Electric Aircraft for Canadian Domestic Flights. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Kinematic detection of dusty outflows from AGN: PAH kinematics of type 2 quasars with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy
items.summary: This study investigated the kinematics of dust in type 2 quasars to test whether dusty outflows are a common feature of energetic active galactic nuclei. Using JWST/MIRI spectroscopy and Principal Component Analysis tomography, the researchers produced velocity maps of Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon features — specifically the large neutral PAHs traced by the 11.3 μm band — in five type 2 quasars at low redshift, then compared these with ionised and molecular gas emission lines. Dusty outflows were detected in the PAH kinematics of all three objects for which velocity maps could be constructed, with molecular hydrogen rotational transitions showing consistent outflow residuals after removing disk kinematics. The 6.2 μm PAH feature tracing ionised PAHs could not be mapped, possibly due to suppression by the AGN, echoing prior findings in Seyfert galaxies and suggesting that AGN outflows preferentially contain neutral rather than ionised dust particles. The results indicate that dusty outflows become more prevalent at higher Eddington ratios and may therefore be a significant factor in AGN evolution and feedback, though the authors note the sample size remains small.
items.citation: Fergus R. Donnan, Cristina Ramos Almeida, Omaira González Martín et al.. Kinematic detection of dusty outflows from AGN: PAH kinematics of type 2 quasars with JWST/MIRI spectroscopy. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.
items.summary: This review paper examines the role of biochemical and biotechnological advances in transforming disease diagnosis and treatment across oncological, infectious, metabolic, and degenerative conditions. The authors synthesized key innovations in molecular diagnostics and biotechnology-driven therapeutic strategies, focusing on their clinical relevance to precision medicine. The review highlights improvements in early disease detection, molecular characterization, and targeted treatment approaches enabled by these technologies. The authors find that such innovations support minimally invasive monitoring, enhanced diagnostic accuracy, and personalized clinical decision-making. They conclude that continued validation, optimization, and integration of these approaches into healthcare systems will be critical for improving patient outcomes and sustaining progress in precision medicine.
items.citation: Kadwe P, Jena L, Jena D et al.. Biochemical and Biotechnological Approaches to Disease Diagnosis and Therapeutics: A Comprehensive Review.. Cureus. 2026

query: saliva based diagnostic testing infectious disease

items.title: Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.
items.summary: This study investigated whether a low-cost, noninvasive electronic nose could accurately detect SARS-CoV-2 as an alternative to conventional diagnostic methods that are often uncomfortable and slow. The device used an array of commercially available metal-oxide gas sensors to measure volatile organic compounds in both saliva and exhaled breath samples, which change in composition in the presence of the virus. Several machine learning algorithms were applied to classify infected versus non-infected samples based on the sensor data. Detection performance varied by algorithm and sample type, with the best results achieved using a nearest-neighbors classifier on exhaled breath samples. The findings support the proof-of-concept feasibility of this approach for point-of-care SARS-CoV-2 screening in real-world clinical settings.
items.venue: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Fialkovitz G, Sousa PL, Hidifira AM et al.. Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.. The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.
items.summary: This study investigated the fungal microbiome (mycobiome) in patients with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), a condition previously associated with bacterial dysbiosis but whose fungal community alterations were largely uncharacterized. Researchers performed a cross-sectional analysis of saliva, sputum, and stool samples from adults with CVID—divided into those with immune dysregulation and those with infectious-only manifestations—alongside healthy controls, using amplicon sequencing and machine learning approaches. Across all three body compartments, CVID patients showed significantly altered mycobiome composition compared to controls, characterized by reduced fungal richness and evenness, particularly in respiratory and oral niches. A consistent enrichment of opportunistic yeasts, most notably *Candida albicans*, was observed across all sample types in both CVID phenotypes, while commensal and environmental fungi were depleted, suggesting a potential pathobiont role for *C. albicans*. Fungal community profiles discriminated CVID patients from healthy controls with high accuracy, supporting their potential utility as non-invasive diagnostic biomarkers, though fungal signatures did not reliably distinguish between the two CVID clinical phenotypes.
items.citation: Cabanero-Navalon MD, Carda-Diéguez M, Mira A et al.. Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.. Frontiers in immunology. 2026

items.title: Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.
items.summary: This study investigated whether tuberculosis can be diagnosed using *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA extracted from salivary exosomes, offering a non-sputum-based alternative particularly suited to paucibacillary cases where bacterial loads are low. The researchers developed an exosomal nucleic acid (ExoNA) assay that integrates salivary exosome enrichment, nucleic acid extraction, and dual-target quantitative PCR melting curve analysis, then evaluated its diagnostic performance in a pilot clinical study of adults with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis. Salivary exosomal DNA was found to be the predominant detectable form of *M. tuberculosis* DNA in saliva, and the assay substantially reduced turnaround time compared to conventional methods while achieving a low limit of detection. The assay demonstrated reasonable sensitivity and specificity against both standard and extended microbiological reference standards, and retained meaningful diagnostic performance in the large subset of patients with negative smear microscopy results. The authors conclude that the ExoNA-based saliva assay is a promising non-invasive, rapid diagnostic tool for tuberculosis, though multicenter validation in more diverse populations is needed before clinical implementation.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Ma J, Zheng X, He Y et al.. Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies
items.summary: This review paper examines the molecular and cellular mechanisms driving inflammatory bone loss in periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease in which dysbiotic oral biofilms lead to progressive destruction of the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone. The authors focus on osteoclastogenesis and the key transcriptional regulators and signaling pathways—including MAPK, JAK/STAT, NF-κB, and PI3K/Akt—that govern osteoclast differentiation, activation, and the resulting bone resorption. The review synthesizes current understanding of how chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation orchestrate tissue destruction in periodontitis, drawing parallels to other osteolytic conditions such as osteoporosis and rheumatoid arthritis. Emerging therapeutic strategies are discussed, including small molecules, biologics, and natural compounds that target these molecular pathways, though the authors note that translating mechanistic insights into effective clinical interventions remains a significant challenge. The paper concludes that continued investigation into bone-resorptive signaling is essential for developing more effective treatments for periodontitis and related inflammatory bone diseases.
authors: Jeroen J. J. P. van den Beucken
items.citation: Rafael Scaf de Molon, Rolando Vernal, Gabriela Ezequiel Oliveira et al.. Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies. Bone Research. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the role of the gut microbiome as a causal driver of colorectal cancer, synthesizing molecular, ecological, and translational evidence across immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine systems. The authors describe how dysbiosis characterized by enrichment of specific pathobionts and depletion of protective short-chain-fatty-acid-producing bacteria promotes carcinogenesis through mechanisms including genotoxin-induced DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation. Bacterial metabolites such as colibactin and cytolethal distending toxin leave defined mutational signatures in human cancer genomes, providing a direct mechanistic link between microbial exposures and tumor development. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured ecosystem disease in which polymicrobial consortia coordinate immune evasion, barrier breakdown, and metabolic cooperation within the tumor microenvironment. The authors also highlight emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies, including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation, framing the microbiome as a tractable target for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication and their potential as diagnostic and therapeutic tools in human disease. The authors provide a comprehensive synthesis covering the biogenesis and molecular composition of major EV classes, advances in their isolation and omics-based characterization, and their roles in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology. Particular attention is given to mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs in regenerative medicine and oncology, as well as the modulation of EV signaling through photobiomodulation as an emerging therapeutic approach. The review also addresses the significant translational obstacles facing the field, including the need for standardization, scalability, harmonized quality controls, and regulatory compliance under frameworks such as those established by the FDA and EMA. The authors conclude that integrating mechanistic understanding with clinical application positions EVs as transformative next-generation platforms in precision medicine.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent developments in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors survey advances in chip design and fabrication approaches, including three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, as well as the integration of biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms that can self-optimize, automate analytical processes, and support clinical decision-making. The review finds that these combined innovations have broadened clinical applications to encompass cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also identify ongoing challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be routinely deployed in clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration
items.summary: This review examines the current state and challenges of smart wearable and implantable biosensors designed for continuous, real-time health monitoring in personalized and preventive medicine. The paper surveys advances in flexible, stretchable, and biocompatible materials that allow devices to integrate seamlessly with the body over extended periods, as well as multimodal sensing approaches capable of detecting multiple biological signals simultaneously. The role of artificial intelligence in enhancing signal processing and generating predictive health insights is also assessed. Despite notable progress, the review identifies persistent challenges including motion artifacts, energy autonomy, data privacy, and the clinical interpretability of sensor outputs. The authors synthesize findings across materials science, device architecture, and AI-assisted strategies to map the landscape of current capabilities and remaining obstacles in the field.
items.citation: Thirumalaisamy Suryaprabha, Chunghyeon Choi, Yanfang Wu et al.. Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration. npj Flexible Electronics. 2026

items.title: Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential
items.summary: This review paper examines the use of electrochemical biosensors for detecting cancer-related protein biomarkers in blood, with a focus on their potential for early cancer diagnosis and prognosis through liquid biopsy approaches. The authors critically survey current strategies for electrochemical detection of serum proteins and catalog recent advances in biosensor designs targeting circulating cancer-associated proteins, noting that many approaches have achieved impressive analytical sensitivity. The review identifies key obstacles preventing these technologies from reaching clinical practice, including interference from complex biological sample matrices, insufficient validation with real patient samples, challenges in accounting for variability between patients, and practical hurdles such as manufacturing scalability, cost, and long-term device stability. Particular emphasis is placed on the analytical and clinical validation frameworks necessary to bridge the gap between laboratory demonstration and real-world clinical or commercial deployment.
items.citation: Michael E. J. Lopez Mujica, Elena E. Ferapontova. Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential. Sensors. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper examines the theoretical foundations underlying anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes, which are widely used tools for sequential hypothesis testing and detection. The authors recast the standard recipe of mixing exponential test statistics over a prior as a two-player game between a Learner who selects a prior and an adversarial Nature who produces a score process, with a pathwise Gibbs-variational identity governing the Learner's wealth along every realized path. Through this game-theoretic lens, they demonstrate that the law of the iterated logarithm emerges naturally as the minimax detection boundary rather than as an arbitrary technical artifact, and that the optimal mixing prior is not a free design choice but rather a uniquely forced equalizer strategy that makes every boundary-crossing time equally costly for Nature. This equalizer prior yields the sharp first iterated-logarithm correction in closed form and corresponds exactly to the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales in the log-log chart, with the Erdős-Kolmogorov integral test serving as its selection criterion. The framework unifies several existing constructions—including the Howard-Ramdas mixture and stitching methods, Ville's inequality, and betting confidence sequences—as special cases of a single equalizer principle, supported by a companion empirical evaluation.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of composing multiple dexterous manipulation skills using a single robotic hand, where combining independently trained policies often causes conflicting demands on shared fingers. The authors propose DexCompose, a framework that assigns explicit ownership of fingers to different tasks by identifying which fingers are essential for maintaining an already-established manipulation state through release tests over candidate finger masks. The framework then trains two asymmetric residual modules: one that stabilizes the preserved skill within its assigned fingers, and another that adapts the downstream policy only within the finger subspace allocated to the new task. Evaluated across a broad set of composite dexterous manipulation tasks combining object-retention and downstream interaction skills, DexCompose achieves a high average composite success rate, substantially outperforming conventional policy chaining approaches. The results suggest that structuring action composition around explicit finger-level ownership is a promising direction for reusing pretrained dexterous policies in multi-task settings.
items.citation: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.. DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand. arXiv. 2026

items.title: PerceptionRubrics: Calibrating Multimodal Evaluation to Human Perception
items.summary: This paper introduces PerceptionRubrics, a new evaluation framework designed to better align multimodal model assessments with genuine human perceptual standards rather than saturated benchmark scores that mask real-world brittleness. The framework pairs a large set of information-dense images with thousands of instance-specific rubrics derived from high-quality captions produced through a Circular Peer-Review consensus pipeline, organizing criteria into essential facts and fine-grained detail streams. A key innovation is the Gated Scoring mechanism, which applies sharp binary penalties when models fail on mandatory visual facts rather than averaging performance linearly across criteria. Evaluation using this framework reveals that models frequently verify fragmented elements correctly while failing strict conjunctive constraints, exposing hidden brittleness in dense visual domains, and that a meaningful perception gap persists between open-source and proprietary models. The authors conclude that their gated metrics align substantially better with human judgment than conventional benchmarks, supporting the view that strict perceptual fidelity is a prerequisite for reliable multimodal generation.
items.citation: Yana Wei, Hongbo Peng, Yanlin Lai et al.. PerceptionRubrics: Calibrating Multimodal Evaluation to Human Perception. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance
items.summary: This paper addresses a fundamental limitation of Force-Free Electrodynamics, which governs electromagnetic fields in magnetically dominated plasmas but breaks down mathematically when magnetic dominance is lost. The authors propose that once this condition fails, the evolution can be continued using a theory of null electromagnetic fields characterized by the requirement that the principal null direction is tangent to a geodesic congruence, which in flat spacetime corresponds to drift velocity streamlines following straight-line trajectories. They develop the mathematical structure of this null continuation theory and validate it by comparing its predictions against particle-in-cell simulations for two test cases: colliding planar Alfvén waves and an exact analytical solution that exhibits finite-time loss of magnetic dominance. In both cases, the combined force-free and null continuation framework shows strong macroscopic agreement with the simulations, correctly capturing the emergence and growth of the null region as well as the formation of a current sheet. The results suggest this null field theory provides a physically meaningful and mathematically consistent extension of Force-Free Electrodynamics beyond the point where magnetic dominance is violated.
items.citation: Morifumi Mizuno. Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigated whether neutral hydrogen absorption features could be detected in the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts, focusing specifically on how diffractive scintillation affects the detectability of the 21-cm absorption line. The researchers constructed a model to simulate diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen and analyzed under what conditions a realistic FRB spectrum could reveal the absorption signal. They found that detection is favored when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs substantially in scale from the width of the absorption profile, and that stacking repeat bursts from active repeaters improves detectability, provided the bursts are separated in time beyond the diffractive scintillation timescale to avoid correlated frequency modulations. Cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds suggested that the sightline to one known repeating source may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud. With current and planned telescopes, the combined effects of scintillation and detector noise require a very large number of stacked bursts to achieve confident detection, though improvements in sensitivity could eventually enable probing of neutral hydrogen clouds in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diameter truncated operator evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses the computational challenge of simulating operator dynamics in out-of-equilibrium quantum systems, where complexity grows rapidly and exact methods quickly become intractable. The authors introduce a truncation scheme based on operator diameter—the spatial extent of the region on a lattice where an operator has non-trivial support—rather than the more commonly used weight, which counts the number of non-trivial terms in a Pauli string expansion. They provide theoretical motivation for this diameter-based truncation by drawing on existing analysis of generic quantum circuits, arguing that operators with large spatial extent contribute negligibly to many quantities of interest. Numerical simulations on the kicked Ising model and the Heisenberg XXZ model demonstrate that the method accurately and efficiently captures local correlation functions and transport properties. The results suggest that diameter truncation offers a leaner and effective alternative to weight-based approaches for studying quantum operator dynamics.
items.citation: Tom Holden-Dye, Max Marvell, Joel Mills et al.. Diameter truncated operator evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Why Andes hantavirus is not the next SARS-CoV-2: contrasting viral shedding, transmissibility and genomic patterns.
items.venue: Euro surveillance : bulletin Europeen sur les maladies transmissibles = European communicable disease bulletin
items.citation: Bal A, Sauvage V, Bouscambert-Duchamp M et al.. Why Andes hantavirus is not the next SARS-CoV-2: contrasting viral shedding, transmissibility and genomic patterns.. Euro surveillance : bulletin Europeen sur les maladies transmissibles = European communicable disease bulletin. 2026

items.title: The Salivary Microbiota–Host Nexus: Unraveling Opportunities for Non-Invasive Monitoring of Health and Productivity in Farm Animals
items.venue: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
items.citation: The Salivary Microbiota–Host Nexus: Unraveling Opportunities for Non-Invasive Monitoring of Health and Productivity in Farm Animals. Animals : an open access journal from MDPI. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid, and accessible testing. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. For extraction, they evaluated approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods, emphasizing their suitability for simplified and equipment-free deployment. On the amplification side, they reviewed how established techniques such as PCR, NASBA, RPA, and LAMP have been adapted for portable, low-power, and rapid use in field settings. For detection, they catalogued platforms ranging from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensors incorporating CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, highlighting the high sensitivity and programmability these newer approaches offer for nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

query: group A streptococcus molecular diagnostic test
