items.title: Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.
items.summary: The paper evaluated whether the Xpert Xpress Strep A, a rapid quantitative PCR assay originally designed for throat swabs, could accurately detect *Streptococcus pyogenes* in pediatric pleural fluid samples, where culture-based diagnosis is often limited by prior antibiotic use. Using a convenience sample of pleural fluid specimens from pediatric patients, the researchers compared the performance of the Xpert assay against both standard bacterial culture and a laboratory-developed PCR targeting a different gene, with the latter two forming a composite reference standard. The Xpert assay detected all positive specimens, achieving perfect agreement with the composite reference standard, while culture alone identified only a minority of the same positive cases. These findings demonstrate that the Xpert assay is substantially more sensitive than culture for identifying Group A streptococcal infection from pleural fluid, and its faster turnaround time supports more timely clinical management and public health decision-making regarding contact chemoprophylaxis.
items.citation: Amaral G, Gadkar VJ, Almodahka A et al.. Performance of the Xpert Xpress Strep A for detection of <i>Streptococcus pyogenes</i> from pediatric pleural fluids.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives
items.summary: Silver nanoparticles have attracted considerable interest as antibacterial agents, and this review examines the scientific literature on their mechanisms of action, practical applications, and safety considerations. The antibacterial activity of these nanoparticles is driven primarily by the release of silver ions, which disrupt bacterial cell membranes, generate reactive oxygen species, inhibit protein synthesis, and interfere with DNA replication. Physical properties such as particle shape, size, and surface characteristics are recognized as key determinants of their efficacy and specificity against a broad range of pathogens, including antibiotic- and drug-resistant strains. The review covers applications spanning agriculture, biomedical settings, and environmental contexts, while also highlighting that inappropriate dosing or suboptimal formulation can lead to significant toxicity, representing a major challenge for safe deployment. The paper concludes by outlining future perspectives for optimizing silver nanoparticle use in a way that balances antimicrobial effectiveness with acceptable safety profiles.
items.citation: Hasan Karataş, Furkan Eker, Emir Akdaşçi et al.. Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the complex and multifaceted relationship between the microbiota and cancer, tracing how this field has evolved from early observations of microbial presence in tumors to mechanistic discoveries linking specific microorganisms to carcinogenesis. The authors synthesize current understanding of the molecular mechanisms by which the microbiota influences tumor progression, including modulation of oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual role of microbiota as both promoters and suppressors of cancer, and explores how microbial communities shape responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by calling for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches to oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical translation in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases
items.summary: This review examines organ cross-talk, the network of biochemical and physiological communication between organs that sustains homeostasis and enables responses to environmental challenges. The authors synthesized current research on inter-organ interactions, centering on the brain, heart, and gut as primary nodes, and mapping their pairwise connections—including brain-gut, brain-liver, brain-heart, heart-kidney, heart-lung, heart-liver, gut-liver, gut-kidney, and gut-lung axes—alongside additional organ relationships catalogued in supplementary tables. By integrating findings on molecular mechanisms and signaling pathways, the review elucidates how these interactions operate in both healthy and diseased states. The authors also survey experimental methods and advanced technologies used to study organ communication and propose therapeutic strategies derived from cross-talk research. Looking forward, the review advocates network-driven models, novel diagnostics, and personalized treatment approaches grounded in systems biology to better address complex, multi-organ diseases.
items.citation: Huiting Che, Yidan Gao, Yonghu Xu et al.. Organ cross-talk: molecular mechanisms, biological functions, and therapeutic interventions for diseases. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) and inflammatory bowel disease, which encompasses Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis. The authors synthesize current understanding of how SCFAs—primarily acetate, propionate, and butyrate—produced through microbial fermentation of dietary fiber influence gut homeostasis, with particular attention to their roles in maintaining epithelial barrier integrity, modulating immune responses, and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine expression. Butyrate is highlighted as especially important, serving as an energy source for colonocytes while reinforcing mucosal defenses. The review critically evaluates clinical trials investigating SCFA administration as a therapeutic approach, noting that results have been inconsistent and variable, attributable to differences in study design and patient characteristics. The authors conclude by assessing the broader therapeutic potential of all three major SCFAs in IBD management, situating their effects within the complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and immunological factors underlying the disease.
items.citation: Roberta Ottria, Susan Mirmajidi, Pierangela Ciuffreda. Gut Microbiota-Derived Short-Chain Fatty Acids in Inflammatory Bowel Disease: Mechanistic Insights into Gut Inflammation, Barrier Function, and Therapeutic Potential. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research
items.summary: This review paper examines graphene oxide, a two-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, as a versatile and sustainable platform for addressing challenges across energy, environmental, and healthcare domains. The authors surveyed the synthesis chemistry and property modification strategies of graphene oxide, focusing on how its layered architecture enables surface functionalization and structural configurations spanning zero-dimensional to three-dimensional nanostructures, along with tunable optical, electronic, and mechanical properties. The review covers a broad range of applications, including adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of pollutants such as microplastics, pathogens, and volatile organic compounds, as well as energy applications such as carbon dioxide photoreduction, hydrogen production, and battery technologies. The authors also address graphene oxide's role in advanced sensing platforms, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering for ultrasensitive molecular detection and environmental gas sensing, as well as its use in healthcare contexts. While highlighting graphene oxide's transformative potential for real-world and industrial applications, the review acknowledges persistent challenges related to scalability, long-term stability, and reproducibility, and proposes possible solutions to support its sustainable future deployment.
items.citation: S.N. Thakur, Ayush Badoni, Rupam Sharma et al.. Graphene oxide as smart sustainable nanomaterial: a versatile multifunctional material with transformative potential in advanced materials science research. npj Materials Sustainability. 2026

items.title: Dietary polyphenols maintain human health through modulation of gut microbiota
items.summary: This review paper examined the bidirectional relationship between dietary polyphenols and gut microbiota and how this interplay influences human health. The authors synthesized existing research on how polyphenols, which are abundant in plant-derived foods and possess antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer properties, interact with the complex microbial communities of the gastrointestinal tract. The review describes how polyphenols modulate the composition and function of gut microbiota, helping to counteract dysbiosis that has been linked to conditions such as inflammatory bowel diseases, metabolic syndromes, and neurological disorders. Reciprocally, the gut microbiota metabolizes polyphenols into bioactive signaling molecules that enhance their bioavailability and exert systemic physiological effects. The authors conclude that fully characterizing this mutual interaction is essential for understanding how polyphenol-based dietary interventions might be leveraged to prevent or manage disease.
items.citation: Mithun Rudrapal, André M. de Oliveira, Ravi Pratap Singh. Dietary polyphenols maintain human health through modulation of gut microbiota. Frontiers in Pharmacology. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper reframes the standard recipe for constructing anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes — averaging exponential test statistics over a prior via Ville's inequality — as a two-player sequential game in which a Learner chooses a mixing prior and Nature adaptively produces a mean-zero score process. The key technical contribution is a single pathwise Gibbs-variational identity that holds along every realized path without expectation operators, from which Ville's inequality, the GROW characterization, and saddlepoint formulas all emerge as special cases. The paper argues that the law of the iterated logarithm is not an arbitrary combinatorial bound but rather the minimax detection boundary of this game, and that the optimal prior is not a free design choice but a forced equalizer strategy — the unique distribution making every boundary-crossing time equally costly for Nature. This equalizer prior coincides with the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales in the log-log chart, and it yields a sharp closed-form first iterated-log correction with a coefficient whose two components have explicit probabilistic interpretations. Existing constructions including the Howard-Ramdas stitching method and betting confidence sequences are unified as instances of this equalizer principle, and a companion empirical evaluation confirms the central identities.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of composing multiple dexterous manipulation skills within a single robotic hand, where simultaneous tasks create conflicting demands on shared fingers. The authors developed DexCompose, a framework that assigns explicit finger-level ownership by first identifying which fingers are essential for maintaining an already-completed skill state, then training two asymmetric residual modules—one to stabilize the preserved task and one to adapt a frozen downstream policy within the fingers allocated to the new task. This role-aware decomposition allows pretrained full-hand policies to be reused without destructive interference between tasks. The framework was evaluated across a broad set of composite manipulation tasks combining object-retention skills with downstream interactions, achieving a high average composite success rate. The results suggest that explicit structural action ownership with dual residual adaptation is a promising approach for composing dexterous manipulation skills beyond simple sequential policy chaining.
items.citation: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.. DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand. arXiv. 2026

items.title: WARP-RM: A Warp-Augmented Relative Progress Reward Model for Data Curation
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of training robot imitation learning policies from mixed-quality human teleoperation data, which often contains hesitations and inefficient motions. The authors developed WARP, a self-supervised method that learns a dense progress reward signal by training a model to predict normalized elapsed time between frames using time-warp augmentations, including variable playback speeds and reversals, without requiring human annotations or subtask labels. These per-frame progress estimates are aggregated into chunk-level advantages and used to upweight high-quality action chunks during behavior cloning, yielding the WARP-BC training procedure. The approach was evaluated on a physical bimanual robot performing long-horizon T-shirt folding from random crumpled configurations, with datasets of varying quality constructed using episode length as a proxy for suboptimality. When training data quality was degraded by including more inefficient demonstrations, WARP-BC maintained near-perfect success rates while standard behavior cloning largely failed, demonstrating substantial improvements in policy robustness and task throughput.
items.citation: Justin Yu, Andrew Goldberg, Kavish Kondap et al.. WARP-RM: A Warp-Augmented Relative Progress Reward Model for Data Curation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Drift Behavior in a Bounded-Confidence Opinion Model with Media Influence
items.summary: This paper examines how media sources shape collective opinion formation by extending a classical bounded-confidence model of opinion dynamics. The authors modify the Deffuant–Weisbuch model to incorporate two opposing media sources representing a polarized media landscape, one with a positive opinion value and one with a negative opinion value. Using both numerical simulations and analytical methods, they demonstrate that the extended model produces a drifting phenomenon in which a large cluster of agents' opinions gradually shifts toward one of the media sources. They characterize how the trajectory and speed of this drift depend on the model's parameters and identify conditions that either promote or suppress the drift. These findings offer insight into how competing media influences can steer the collective opinions of populations in social systems.
items.citation: Oliver Zheng, Mason A. Porter. Drift Behavior in a Bounded-Confidence Opinion Model with Media Influence. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance
items.summary: The paper addresses a fundamental limitation of Force-Free Electrodynamics, which breaks down when the magnetic dominance condition is violated, leaving the theory non-hyperbolic and unable to evolve the electromagnetic field further. To resolve this, the authors propose replacing Force-Free Electrodynamics in magnetically non-dominated regions with a theory of null electromagnetic fields, characterized by a principal null direction tangent to a geodesic congruence, which in flat spacetime corresponds to requiring that drift velocity integral curves form straight lines. They develop the mathematical structure of this null continuation theory and test it by comparing its predictions against particle-in-cell simulations of colliding planar Alfvén waves and an exact type-changing solution that exhibits finite-time loss of magnetic dominance. In both cases, the combined force-free and null continuation framework shows strong macroscopic agreement with particle-in-cell results, correctly capturing the emergence and growth of null regions as well as current sheet formation. The work thus offers a physically motivated and computationally tractable extension of Force-Free Electrodynamics beyond its usual domain of validity.
items.citation: Morifumi Mizuno. Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Pairwise Reflection Symmetry in Generalized Latin Rectangles
items.summary: This paper investigates a structural property called pairwise reflection-symmetry in generalized Latin rectangles, which are rectangular arrays where symbols are distributed with controlled frequencies across rows and columns. The property requires that for every pair of columns, each ordered symbol pair appears as often as its reversal, ensuring that head-to-head comparisons between symbols are unbiased regardless of column position—a fairness condition relevant to tournament scheduling and comparative trials. The authors establish that existence of such designs for odd values of the repetition parameter is considerably more subtle than for even values, and they prove that the minimal case of column multiplicity equal to one is achievable if and only if the number of symbols is a power of two. They also develop a direct product construction, derive further structural observations, and formulate a quadratically constrained integer program to computationally search for these objects. Computational experiments suggest that many of these designs harbor an underlying group-theoretic structure, which the authors conjecture may be unavoidable in certain parameter regimes.
items.citation: Enrico Iurlano, Günther R. Raidl. Pairwise Reflection Symmetry in Generalized Latin Rectangles. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.
items.summary: The paper investigated whether synthetic peptide epitopes derived from Group B Streptococcus (GBS) proteins could serve as the basis for an ELISA-based immunodiagnostic test to detect GBS carriage in pregnant women. Three epitopes were selected from the bacterial proteins GroEL, enolase, and EF-Tu and tested against serum samples from GBS-positive and GBS-negative individuals using IgG, IgM, and IgA antibody detection. All three synthetic peptides, whether used individually or in combination, significantly distinguished between GBS-positive and GBS-negative sera, with the enolase- and EF-Tu-derived epitopes performing best as single candidates due to their lower required concentrations and higher absorbance values. Combining the epitopes in various mixtures further improved immunoreactivity beyond what any single epitope achieved alone. The authors claim this prototype represents a pioneering alternative to the current gold-standard culture-based method, which is time-consuming and can yield ambiguous results.
items.citation: Brzychczy-Włoch M, Dobrut A, Drożdż K et al.. Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.. Applied microbiology and biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a syndromic molecular panel for central nervous system infections by comparing it against conventional cerebrospinal fluid culture in a real-world clinical setting over three years. Using a large dataset of paired molecular and culture results, the researchers calculated measures of diagnostic concordance, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values for the panel both across all pathogens and when restricted to only those bacterial targets the panel was designed to detect. The syndromic panel demonstrated high specificity and a strong negative predictive value when analysis was limited to its included targets, but overall sensitivity was considerably lower, reflecting the panel's inability to detect organisms outside its fixed target list. Conventional culture identified clinically relevant pathogens absent from the panel, including common and rare bacteria, underscoring that the closed architecture of syndromic panels creates meaningful diagnostic gaps. The authors conclude that syndromic molecular testing and conventional culture must be used together rather than interchangeably, and advocate for next-generation multiplex platforms with broader target coverage guided by real-world epidemiological data.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Sberna G, D'Arezzo S, Valli MB et al.. Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LIAISON PLEX Gram-Positive Blood Culture Assay, a multiplexed molecular test designed to rapidly identify gram-positive bacterial pathogens and antibiotic resistance markers directly from positive blood culture bottles. The assay targets thirteen gram-positive bacterial species and four antibiotic resistance markers, and its performance was assessed across prospective, pre-selected, and contrived blood culture samples in a multisite design. The assay demonstrated very high overall sensitivity and specificity for all targets, with compatibility confirmed across blood culture bottles from multiple manufacturers and robustness in the presence of potential interfering substances and polymicrobial infections. Nearly all tested bacterial strains were detected in the inclusivity analysis, and minimal cross-reactivity was observed among off-panel species. The authors conclude that the assay offers fast, accurate, and reliable detection of gram-positive organisms and their resistance genes, with potential to improve antibiotic optimization and patient management in bloodstream infection settings.
items.citation: Chow S-K, Stevig C, Granato P et al.. Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study examined the diagnostic performance and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels used to detect pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with suspected meningitis or encephalitis over a five-year period at a tertiary care hospital. Researchers retrospectively reviewed test results alongside clinical data to determine how often positive findings led to documented changes in diagnosis or treatment. Viral pathogens dominated the detections, with human herpesvirus-6, enterovirus, and herpes simplex virus type-1 most frequently identified, while Streptococcus pneumoniae was the most common bacterial finding, and co-detections occurred in a notable proportion of positive cases. Despite the panels' capacity for rapid, broad pathogen identification, fewer than half of positive results were associated with a clinically relevant action, repeat testing after an initial negative result added little diagnostic value, and some tests were ordered for conditions the panels do not cover. The authors conclude that while multiplex PCR panels are a useful tool for diagnosing central nervous system infections, their clinical impact is undermined by inappropriate utilisation and poor clinician familiarity with panel content, highlighting the need for clearer testing criteria and better education.
items.venue: The Indian journal of medical research
items.citation: Gürler M, Karaoğlu Ç, Yurtcu EE et al.. Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.. The Indian journal of medical research. 2026

items.title: Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.
items.summary: This study evaluated rapid molecular point-of-care tests for detecting Group B Streptococcus during labour, addressing the limitation that antenatal screening does not reliably reflect colonisation status at the time of delivery. Six molecular assay platforms and one antigen-based test were prospectively assessed under real-world clinical conditions using vaginal-rectal swabs from women at term, with conventional intrapartum culture serving as the reference standard. Diagnostic performance varied considerably across platforms, with positive percent agreement spanning a wide range while negative percent agreement was generally more consistent. Some culture-positive samples tested negative across all molecular platforms, and one sample returned consistently positive molecular results despite negative culture, suggesting potential discordances in both directions. The authors conclude that intrapartum molecular point-of-care testing shows substantial inter-platform variability under routine conditions, potentially influenced by differences in specimen lysis procedures, and that local validation and careful clinical interpretation are necessary before widespread implementation.
items.citation: Serrari M, Bianchi L, Tré-Hardy M et al.. Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.. Microorganisms. 2026

query: point-of-care nucleic acid amplification test infectious disease

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly referred to as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments in three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A particular focus is placed on the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the creation of smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. The review finds that these combined advances have broadened clinical applications to include cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also identify persistent challenges in manufacturing consistency, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic technologies can be widely adopted in routine clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review examines the current landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the need for decentralized, rapid testing accessible outside conventional laboratory settings. The paper organizes its analysis around three core operational steps: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection, covering platforms such as lateral flow assays, biochips, and biosensors. For extraction, it evaluates approaches including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic methods with an emphasis on simplicity and equipment-free operation. Amplification strategies reviewed include both thermal cycling methods like PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all adapted for portable and low-power use. On the detection side, the review spans colorimetric and fluorescent assays through to advanced biosensors exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles, highlighting their potential for sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition at the point of care.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed a novel electrochemical diagnostic platform that combines isothermal nucleic acid amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for identifying viral pathogens. The method works by monitoring proton release generated during amplification using a pH-sensing redox compound, offering an alternative to optical detection approaches that can complicate traditional LAMP-based tests. The platform was evaluated against established PCR-based methods across multiple respiratory viruses including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B, RSV A, and equine herpesvirus, using both purified RNA from clinical samples and an extraction-free workflow with equine nasal swabs. Results showed high concordance with RT-qPCR across tested conditions and a low detection limit for influenza A virus. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is feasible across several sample types and pathogens, though they acknowledge that broader point-of-care validation in additional sample matrices and prospective patient cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR method integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three bacterial pathogens responsible for cerebrospinal fluid infections. The researchers assessed the assay's sensitivity using recombinant plasmids and its specificity against common CSF pathogens, then tested it on both simulated and clinical CSF samples, comparing performance against conventional real-time PCR with manual nucleic acid extraction. The integrated point-of-care system achieved very low detection limits for all three target pathogens with no cross-reactivity, while reducing total testing time to approximately two hours compared to four hours for the manual approach. When applied to clinical samples, the point-of-care method identified additional positives that conventional real-time PCR had missed, demonstrating superior sensitivity. The authors conclude that this culture-free, automated approach offers a rapid and highly sensitive diagnostic workflow suited for point-of-care detection of these bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examines isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based molecular diagnostics. The authors surveyed the principles, detection strategies, and applications of the most widely used isothermal techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Because these methods operate at a constant temperature and do not require thermal cycling equipment, they offer simpler, faster, and more cost-effective nucleic acid detection suited to resource-limited settings. The review consolidates information on reaction mechanisms, product detection approaches, and disease diagnostic applications that had previously been scattered across the literature. The authors also discuss the advantages of each technique and offer perspectives on the future development of isothermal amplification methods in clinical and research contexts.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, motivated by the limitations of conventional culture-based methods and the growing threat of antimicrobial resistance. The authors conduct a structured comparative analysis of major platforms including PCR and its variants, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key performance parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, limit of detection, turnaround time, and multiplexing capacity. The review also critically assesses how artificial intelligence and machine learning are being integrated into molecular diagnostic workflows to support antimicrobial resistance prediction and clinical decision-making. Additionally, the translational feasibility of these technologies for point-of-care deployment is discussed, accounting for clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude by outlining an integrated perspective on current culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics and identifying key challenges and future directions for the field.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This paper developed a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a portable, fully automated point-of-care testing platform for detecting three key meningitis pathogens — *Haemophilus influenzae*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and *Cryptococcus neoformans* — directly from cerebrospinal fluid. The closed-system nested amplification design was intended to reduce contamination risk and operational complexity compared to conventional approaches. Analytical testing demonstrated very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a broad panel of non-target organisms, and maintained sensitivity in simulated clinical samples. Evaluation against clinical cerebrospinal fluid specimens showed near-perfect agreement with conventional quantitative PCR, with the new assay additionally detecting cases of *Cryptococcus neoformans* that were missed, suggesting superior sensitivity. The complete assay delivers results in under two hours, supporting its potential utility as a rapid, sensitive, and operationally practical tool for point-of-care meningitis diagnosis, pending validation in larger patient cohorts.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey
items.summary: This survey examines the intersection of foundation models and computational healthcare through a data-centric lens, focusing on how data quality, characterization, and scale shape AI performance in clinical settings. The authors reviewed a broad range of data-centric approaches spanning the full pipeline from model pre-training through inference, addressing longstanding healthcare challenges such as data quantity, annotation burden, patient privacy, and ethical considerations. The survey also addresses AI security, model assessment, and alignment with human values as critical dimensions for responsible deployment. The authors conclude with an outlook suggesting that foundation model-based analytics hold promise for improving patient outcomes and streamlining clinical workflows as the healthcare AI landscape continues to evolve.
items.citation: Yunkun Zhang, Jin Gao, Zheling Tan et al.. Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey. ACM Computing Surveys. 2026

items.title: Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions
items.citation: Lorenzo Galluzzi, Oliver Kepp, Laurence Zitvogel et al.. Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions. Cancer Cell. 2025

items.title: Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
items.summary: This review paper synthesizes current knowledge on acute pancreatitis, an inflammatory condition whose global incidence is rising with notable regional variation in both frequency and underlying causes, including gallstone disease, hypertriglyceridemia, and alcohol abuse. The authors provide a structured overview of how the condition is defined and classified, and examine recent advances in clinical scoring systems, biomarkers, and artificial intelligence-based predictive models relevant to early risk stratification. The review also details the multilevel pathophysiological mechanisms driving the disease, encompassing aberrant enzymatic activation, calcium overload, impaired autophagy, inflammatory cascades, and multiple forms of pancreatic acinar cell death. From a therapeutic standpoint, the paper covers management strategies across disease stages, adjunctive approaches from traditional Chinese medicine, and individualized treatment considerations for special populations. Emerging interventions such as nanotechnology and extracellular vesicle-based therapies are additionally highlighted as promising directions for future research and clinical innovation.
items.citation: Qian Hu, Yue Hu, Chunlu Tan et al.. Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as sources of diagnostic information, focusing on the biosensing technologies developed to detect and analyze them for clinical applications. The authors surveyed the landscape of sensing modalities—including surface plasmon resonance, electrochemical, fluorescence, and magnetic detection systems—evaluating how each has advanced sensitivity and specificity for EV-based measurements. The review also addresses standardization efforts, analytical validation frameworks, and the integration of multiplexed multiomics approaches at the single-vesicle level, as well as the application of machine learning to improve diagnostic interpretation. Clinical relevance is explored across oncology, neurology, and cardiometabolic and infectious diseases, tracing translational progress toward regulatory-compliant and scalable platforms. The authors identify persistent challenges in manufacturing scale-up, quality control, and point-of-care deployment, and propose a unified framework intended to accelerate EV biosensing toward routine use in precision diagnostics and personalized medicine.
items.citation: Seong-Jun Choi, Jaewon Choi, Jin Kim et al.. Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation. Molecules. 2026

items.title: Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies
items.summary: This paper reviews the current state of knowledge and ongoing controversies surrounding severe community-acquired pneumonia, a condition that remains a significant public health burden due to its complications and treatment costs. The authors examine challenges in empirical antibiotic selection, noting that while rapid molecular diagnostic tools hold promise for guiding treatment, their clinical role and cost-effectiveness require further evaluation. The review addresses the trend toward shorter antibiotic courses, potentially guided by clinical progress and biomarkers, and explores immunomodulatory approaches given that the condition involves dysregulated systemic inflammation. Recent high-powered randomized trials of corticosteroids have produced conflicting results, leaving unresolved which patient subgroups—possibly those with marked inflammatory responses but excluding influenza cases—might benefit from such therapy. Supportive and respiratory care are highlighted as essential pillars of management that must be individualized to disease severity.
items.citation: Pierre-François Dequin, A Torres, M. Bassetti et al.. Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies. Intensive Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries
items.summary: This paper examines the theoretical foundations underlying anytime-valid confidence sequences and e-processes, which are standard tools for sequential statistical inference. The authors recast the standard recipe of averaging exponential test statistics over a prior as a two-player game between a Learner who selects a prior and Nature who produces a mean-zero score process, with information serving as the currency of exchange. A pathwise Gibbs-variational identity is derived that holds along every realized path without requiring expectation operators, unifying several existing results including Ville's inequality, the GROW characterization, and saddlepoint formulas as special cases. The central finding is that the law of the iterated logarithm emerges not as an arbitrary slack bound but as the minimax detection boundary of this sequential game, and the optimal prior is uniquely determined as the equalizer strategy—the Jeffreys prior on the scale-of-scales in the log-log chart—rather than a free design choice, yielding a sharp closed-form correction with a coefficient whose two components have explicit interpretations. Many existing constructions in the literature, including Howard-Ramdas stitching and betting confidence sequences, are shown to be instances of this unifying equalizer principle.
items.citation: Akshay Balsubramani. The multiply iterated law of the iterated logarithm: game-theoretic foundations of sequential detection boundaries. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of composing multiple dexterous manipulation skills so that a single robotic hand can perform multi-task manipulation without different skills interfering with one another. The authors developed DexCompose, a framework that assigns explicit finger-level ownership to each task, identifying through release tests which fingers are essential for maintaining an already-completed skill state and reserving the remaining fingers for a new downstream task. The approach trains two asymmetric residual modules—one to stabilize the preserved skill and one to adapt the downstream policy within its assigned finger subspace—layered on top of frozen pretrained full-hand policies. Evaluated across a broad set of composite manipulation tasks combining object-retention skills with downstream interactions, the framework achieves a high average composite success rate. The results suggest that structuring action ownership at the finger level, combined with dual residual adaptation, is an effective strategy for reusing and composing dexterous manipulation policies without destructive interference.
items.citation: Dihong Huang, Zhenyu Wei, Zhuxiu Xu et al.. DexCompose: Reusing Dexterous Policies for Multi-Task Manipulation with a Single Hand. arXiv. 2026

items.title: StructSplat: Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting from Uncalibrated Sparse Views
items.summary: StructSplat is a framework for reconstructing 3D Gaussian representations from sparse, uncalibrated images without requiring known camera parameters. The approach introduces a structured separation of geometry, semantics, and texture within the reconstruction pipeline, using pixel-aligned feature injection for accurate texture modeling, semantic-aware priors for global consistency, and a camera alignment strategy to prevent information leakage. Unlike existing methods that either optimize per scene or assume known poses, StructSplat operates in a feed-forward, generalizable manner across diverse scenes. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate substantial improvements in reconstruction quality over prior approaches, with notable gains in peak signal-to-noise ratio on both in-distribution and cross-dataset evaluations.
items.citation: Jia-Chen Zhao, Beiqi Chen, Xinyang Chen et al.. StructSplat: Generalizable 3D Gaussian Splatting from Uncalibrated Sparse Views. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance
items.summary: The paper addresses a fundamental limitation of Force-Free Electrodynamics, which governs electromagnetic fields in magnetically dominated plasmas but breaks down mathematically when magnetic dominance is lost. To handle this failure, the authors propose replacing the force-free description with a theory of null electromagnetic fields characterized by the condition that the principal null direction is tangent to a geodesic congruence, providing a natural continuation beyond the breakdown. They develop the mathematical structure of this null-field theory and show that in flat spacetime it reduces to requiring zero field invariants combined with the constraint that drift velocity streamlines are straight. The theory is validated against particle-in-cell simulations of colliding planar Alfvén waves, where the combined force-free and null continuation reproduces macroscopic features including the emergence and growth of null regions and current sheet formation. An additional test against an exact analytical solution that exhibits finite-time loss of magnetic dominance further confirms that the null continuation agrees well with particle-in-cell simulations at the macroscopic level.
items.citation: Morifumi Mizuno. Continuation of Force-Free Electrodynamics upon the loss of magnetic dominance. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra
items.summary: This paper investigates whether neutral hydrogen's 21-cm absorption line can be detected in the spectra of Fast Radio Bursts, focusing specifically on how interstellar scintillation affects that detectability. The authors constructed an efficient model to simulate diffractive scintillation from a thin scattering screen and explored the conditions under which the absorption feature remains recoverable. They found that the absorption profile is most detectable when the scintillation decorrelation bandwidth differs substantially in scale from the absorption line width, and that stacking repeat bursts from active repeating FRBs improves detectability—provided the bursts are separated in time by more than the diffractive scintillation timescale so that frequency-dependent flux modulations are uncorrelated. By cross-referencing known repeating FRB positions with a catalog of Milky Way molecular clouds, they identified a candidate sightline that may intersect a Galactic molecular cloud. Accounting for both scintillation and detector noise, they conclude that a very large number of stacked bursts is required to achieve a statistically significant detection with current telescopes, though improvements in detector sensitivity could eventually enable probes of neutral hydrogen in host galaxies, intervening galaxies, or high-redshift minihalos.
items.citation: Om Gupta, Pawan Kumar, Paz Beniamini et al.. The Role of Scintillation in Detecting HI Absorption in FRB Spectra. arXiv. 2026

items.title: SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe
items.summary: This paper examines how jetted Active Galactic Nuclei in the early Universe can serve as powerful probes of black hole formation, galaxy evolution, and cosmology, with particular focus on sources at redshifts above six. The authors present a White Paper outlining the scientific case for combining the Square Kilometre Array with very long baseline interferometry, which would deliver sub-microjansky sensitivity alongside milliarcsecond angular resolution across a broad frequency range. Such capabilities would enable direct imaging and characterisation of relativistic jets in the first billion years of cosmic history, allowing constraints on Doppler boosting, jet duty cycles, and how jets couple to their surrounding environments. These observations are argued to test competing models of supermassive black hole formation and growth, including scenarios involving jet-assisted super-Eddington accretion phases, while also providing geometric probes of the cosmological framework. The paper further highlights the importance of synergies with other multi-wavelength facilities to build a comprehensive, panchromatic understanding of the earliest jetted AGN and their host galaxies.
items.citation: C. Spingola, M. Mezcua, Y. Liu et al.. SKA$-$VLBI view of AGN jets in the early Universe. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a novel lateral flow immunoassay-based antigen rapid diagnostic test designed to simultaneously detect SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus from nasopharyngeal swab specimens. Archived clinical specimens were tested in parallel with the antigen test and real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction, which served as the reference standard, and cross-reactivity was assessed against a panel of respiratory pathogens. The antigen test demonstrated high sensitivity for influenza A and SARS-CoV-2, with somewhat lower sensitivity for influenza B and RSV, while achieving perfect specificity for all four targets and showing no cross-reactivity with any tested microorganism. Sensitivity was consistently high in specimens with low cycle threshold values, indicating strong performance when viral loads are elevated, but declined in specimens with lower viral loads. The authors conclude that the test is a reliable rapid screening and initial differentiation tool for major respiratory viruses but should complement rather than replace molecular testing.
items.citation: Kim JA, Kang M, Yu HJ et al.. Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: This study investigated the disease burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients at a Japanese clinic by testing nasopharyngeal swabs collected from adults presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests were used to simultaneously screen for SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B, and RSV. While numerous patients tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, and one tested positive for influenza B, no RSV-positive cases were detected. Comparison with prior studies employing PCR-based testing revealed a statistically significant difference in RSV detection rates, suggesting that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing has limited diagnostic utility for identifying RSV infections in adult patients in Japan.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.
items.citation: Davidson H, Han L, McConeghy K et al.. Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.. Antimicrobial stewardship & healthcare epidemiology : ASHE. 2026

items.title: Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.
items.summary: This prospective study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multiplex rapid antigen test capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus in patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. The researchers enrolled over a thousand symptomatic patients, including children, comparing results from nasal swab antigen testing performed by emergency department staff against PCR conducted on separately collected nasopharyngeal samples at a central laboratory. Sensitivity for each pathogen was assessed at a defined PCR cycle threshold cutoff to reflect clinically relevant viral loads, and a subset of test device media were also subjected to PCR to examine viral RNA absorption differences between swab types. The test demonstrated high sensitivity across all four viruses and age groups at the chosen cycle threshold reference level, with perfect specificity for all agents, while nasal swabs appeared to absorb less viral RNA than nasopharyngeal swabs in adults for influenza A. The authors conclude that the test's performance supports its use as a marker of infectiousness in acute clinical care settings.
items.venue: Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Berger K, Vang SH, Bekkeseth RHO et al.. Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.. Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the comparative analytical performance of over-the-counter lateral flow tests designed to detect influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2, addressing a gap in consumer-facing performance data. Researchers conducted a controlled benchtop assessment of multiple tests commercially available in the United States, using contrived specimen panels prepared from propagated viral stocks diluted in clinical matrix and applied according to each manufacturer's instructions, with results read by blinded evaluators. All eight tests performed similarly, with detection limits falling within a fourfold dilution range across all four viral targets tested. A notable finding was that at low viral concentrations, several tests initially appeared negative at the start of the interpretation window but converted to positive results by the end of it. The authors therefore recommend that users wait for the full manufacturer-specified incubation period before concluding that a test result is truly negative.
items.citation: Lohsen S, Hoy-Schulz Y, Taing A et al.. Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: The study investigated the disease burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients in Japan, where such infections have been receiving increasing attention. Nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms at a single clinic were tested using rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B, and RSV simultaneously. While the tests identified numerous positive cases for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, not a single RSV-positive case was detected across all patients tested. Comparing these results against previous studies that used polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a statistically significant difference in RSV detection rates, with PCR identifying a meaningful proportion of positive cases that the rapid antigen test missed. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing is insufficiently sensitive for diagnosing RSV infections in adults in Japan.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor: structure, signaling, physiology and pathology
items.summary: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor is a ligand-dependent transcription factor that senses both environmental and endogenous signals, and this review comprehensively examines its structure, signaling mechanisms, and roles in health and disease. The authors trace six decades of research on the receptor, covering its canonical function as a xenobiotic sensor alongside its broader physiological roles in development, immune regulation, barrier tissue integrity, and host-microbe interactions. Recent structural advances illuminating ligand-binding and transcriptional control are detailed, as is the diverse range of AHR ligands, including environmental toxins and microbial or dietary tryptophan metabolites that activate canonical and noncanonical signaling pathways. The review also addresses AHR involvement in pathologies such as cancer and autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, positioning it as a promising therapeutic target. The authors conclude by highlighting ongoing efforts in drug design to develop effective AHR modulators and situate the receptor within the broader exposome framework as a master integrator of environmental and endogenous cues.
items.citation: Xavier Coumoul, Robert Barouki, Charlotte Esser et al.. The aryl hydrocarbon receptor: structure, signaling, physiology and pathology. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven skin disease characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and the GRADE framework, incorporating consensus from a large, multinational body of delegates representing national and international societies across numerous countries. The updated guideline addresses the definition, classification, diagnosis, and management of urticaria, covering both acute and chronic forms, with the latter further categorized as chronic spontaneous or chronic inducible urticaria. The authors highlight that chronic urticaria significantly impairs quality of life and productivity, while noting that novel therapeutic options are now available. The resulting recommendations reflect expert-guided and evidence-based approaches to the full spectrum of urticaria subtypes.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review examines the role of neutrophils in chronic inflammation, challenging the traditional view that these innate immune cells function only in acute, short-term defense responses. The authors synthesize current knowledge on how neutrophils, through mechanisms such as neutrophil extracellular trap formation, reactive oxygen species production, degranulation, and phagocytosis, can adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype that drives sustained tissue damage rather than resolving infection. The review also explores how neutrophils actively communicate with other immune and non-immune cell types—including endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes—thereby orchestrating broader inflammatory and tissue remodeling processes. The paper applies this mechanistic framework to the pathophysiology of several immune-driven diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders. The authors conclude that deeper understanding of neutrophil behavior in chronic inflammatory contexts could reveal novel therapeutic targets for these conditions.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of the field from early observations of microorganisms in tumors to mechanistic discoveries linking specific microbes to cancer, and they synthesize current understanding of how the microbiota modulates oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a promoter and suppressor of tumor growth, and explores how microbial communities shape responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota — including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation — are discussed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by calling for personalized approaches to leverage the anticancer potential of the microbiota and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical translation in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis
items.summary: This paper investigated how tumours in peripheral organs communicate with the brain and how this bidirectional signalling influences the immune response against cancer. Using genetically engineered mouse models combined with neural tracing, tissue imaging, and single-cell transcriptomics, the researchers studied lung adenocarcinoma and its interactions with the nervous system. They found that lung tumours activate vagal sensory neurons expressing a specific receptor, which transmit signals to brainstem nuclei, subsequently elevating sympathetic nervous system activity within the tumour microenvironment. This sympathetic output then suppresses anti-tumour immunity by acting on alveolar macrophages through adrenergic signalling, thereby promoting tumour growth. Disrupting this sensory-to-sympathetic circuit through genetic, pharmacological, or chemogenetic means significantly inhibited lung tumour growth by unleashing immune responses, suggesting that targeting this tumour–brain axis could represent a novel therapeutic strategy for visceral cancers.
items.citation: Haohan Karen Wei, Chuyue Yu, Bo Hu et al.. Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis. Nature. 2026

items.title: CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-engineered cell therapies, tracing their evolution from oncology-focused applications toward a broad range of non-malignant conditions. The authors survey the structural and signaling features of multiple CAR platforms—including CAR-T, CAR-NK, CAR-macrophage, and CAR-NKT cells—as well as next-generation design innovations such as logic-gated CARs and off-the-shelf allogeneic products. Applications covered span hematologic and solid tumors, autoimmune disorders such as lupus and rheumatoid arthritis, HIV, organ transplantation tolerance, tissue fibrosis, and ageing-related conditions. The review highlights that CAR therapies in autoimmune settings appear to carry a lower risk of cytokine release syndrome compared to oncology use, and that certain CAR-T approaches targeting CD19 and BCMA have achieved long-term remission without ongoing immunosuppression. Persistent challenges including manufacturing complexity, high costs, antigen escape, and the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment are identified as key barriers to broader clinical translation.
items.citation: Mobina Bayat, Javid Sadri Nahand. CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives. Molecular Biomedicine. 2026

items.title: AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.
items.summary: This scoping review systematically mapped the landscape of artificial intelligence-enhanced point-of-care and rapid diagnostic technologies for infectious diseases in resource-limited settings, examining research published over roughly a decade. Following established scoping review methodology, the authors searched five major databases, screened over a thousand records, and ultimately included more than two hundred studies, extracting data on disease focus, diagnostic modalities, AI methods, validation approaches, and geographic distribution. The field was found to be growing exponentially, yet heavily concentrated around a narrow set of diseases—COVID-19, malaria, and tuberculosis—while neglected tropical diseases and regions such as West Africa and Latin America remained substantially underrepresented. Convolutional neural networks were the dominant AI approach, and microscopy was the most common diagnostic modality, but the vast majority of studies lacked prospective field validation, with only a small fraction reporting this rigorous standard. The authors conclude that despite rapid growth, the field faces critical deficiencies in validation rigor, disease equity, and geographic inclusivity, and they call for future work to prioritize real-world field testing and greater involvement of end-user communities from the earliest stages of research design.
items.venue: Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH
items.citation: Farquhar H. AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.. Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH. 2026

items.title: Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.
items.summary: This paper examines the role of infectious disease diagnostic competencies in medical education as a foundation for building resilient physicians capable of responding to future infectious disease challenges. The authors argue that teaching in medical microbiology, virology, and hygiene must develop clinical infection medicine skills through structured, hands-on practical training, which cannot be replaced by self-directed learning formats. They emphasize that proficiency in rapid and appropriate infection diagnostics is essential for rational antimicrobial therapy, thereby helping to prevent the misuse of antibiotics that drives multidrug-resistant pathogen development. The paper further highlights that competencies such as correct sample collection, appropriate test selection, and critical interpretation of microbiological findings are indispensable for outbreak control and interrupting transmission chains. Framing these skills within a One-Health perspective, the authors contend that fostering infection-related competencies in medical students is the only sustainable pathway to long-term professional resilience against infectious disease threats.
items.citation: Brandt C, Becker K, Ciesek S et al.. Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.. GMS infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.
items.summary: This review examines the current state of bovine viral diarrhoea virus management in Australia and evaluates how modern diagnostic technologies could transform the national response to this endemic disease. The authors analyse the limitations of Australia's decentralised, non-compulsory biosecurity framework, which places responsibility on individual producers and results in fragmented, inconsistent disease control across beef and dairy systems. The review proposes that point-of-care diagnostic tools — including lateral flow assays, nucleic acid amplification tests, and biosensors — could serve as a pivotal catalyst for a coordinated national eradication program by enabling rapid, field-based identification and culling of persistently infected animals, which are the primary reservoir of transmission. The authors argue that when these diagnostics are integrated with strengthened biosecurity protocols and optimised vaccination strategies, they provide a scientifically credible and economically viable pathway to systematic eradication rather than mere control of the disease.
items.citation: Ogada S, Naseem MN, Ranjbar S et al.. From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.. Viruses. 2026

items.title: Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.
items.summary: This paper summarizes the key scientific discussions and emerging themes from an international conference focused on respiratory infections and their prevention and management. The authors reviewed plenary sessions, symposia, and panel discussions, organizing insights across domains including therapeutics, vaccines, host response, diagnostics, and surveillance. A central finding was the field's shift away from purely pathogen-centred approaches toward frameworks that also account for host inflammatory responses as major contributors to disease severity. The conference highlighted advances in long-acting and broadly active antivirals, combination therapy strategies, vaccines targeting conserved viral components, and integrated surveillance approaches incorporating genomic sequencing and metagenomics within a One Health framework. The authors conclude that connecting clinical care, translational research, and public health infrastructure more closely will be essential for translating scientific progress into practice and preparing for future respiratory disease epidemics and pandemics.
items.citation: Morvil N, Goh WGW, Zheng C et al.. Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.. Influenza and other respiratory viruses. 2026

items.title: Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.
items.summary: This paper describes the establishment of an advanced infectious disease diagnostic facility in Poltava, Ukraine, a region under significant strain from Russia's ongoing invasion and the influx of internally displaced persons. The authors, through a German-Ukrainian hospital partnership, implemented EU-compliant serological and automated PCR testing alongside bacterial identification systems with antibiotic susceptibility testing at a regional infectious diseases hospital. Key features of the initiative included participatory equipment selection, hands-on training of Ukrainian staff conducted in Germany, jointly developed standard operating procedures, and adaptive reagent supply chains to account for wartime disruptions. Over the course of the project, the laboratories detected previously unrecognized cases of HIV, Hepatitis B and C, Lyme disease, Toxoplasmosis, and antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections that would likely have been missed using the low-sensitivity rapid tests previously in use. The authors conclude that high-quality diagnostic infrastructure can be successfully built under active conflict conditions when partnership design emphasizes pragmatic solutions, shared decision-making, and contingency planning, and they propose this model as a template for expansion to other regions of Ukraine.
items.citation: Zelinskyy G, Koval T, Schrammel U et al.. Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.
items.summary: This study examined the prospective acceptability of a novel point-of-care screening tool called the genital inflammation test (GIFT), designed to detect genital inflammation as a proxy for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in asymptomatic women. The research was conducted in South Africa and Zimbabwe through in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with women, healthcare professionals, and decision-makers. Women expressed strong interest in tools supporting sexual health and viewed diagnostic testing positively, but their acceptance decreased when GIFT was framed as a preliminary screening step requiring follow-up confirmatory testing, given the cost and limited availability of such tests. Healthcare professionals and decision-makers similarly questioned the tool's practical utility within resource-constrained health systems, while acknowledging its potential to catalyse the development of cheaper, infrastructure-light diagnostics. The findings highlight that acceptability of biomedical technologies is deeply shaped by health system context, and that successful implementation would require affordable confirmatory diagnostics, clear communication strategies, and co-designed care pathways.
items.citation: Karumazondo J, Bernays S, Naidoo J et al.. Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.. Global public health. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven condition characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methods and a GRADE framework, incorporating consensus input from a large number of delegates representing national and international societies across numerous countries, with a formal consensus conference held in late 2024. The document covers the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing between acute urticaria and chronic forms—namely chronic spontaneous and chronic inducible urticaria—and outlines evidence-based diagnostic and therapeutic approaches for each subtype. The guideline highlights that chronic urticaria is a disabling condition with significant impacts on quality of life and daily functioning, while noting that novel therapies are now available to address it.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion
items.summary: This review examines T cell exhaustion, a dysfunctional immune state that arises during chronic infections and within tumor microenvironments, where it impairs effective antitumor and antiviral responses. The authors synthesize current understanding of the mechanisms driving exhaustion, distinguishing between external factors such as persistent antigen exposure, immune checkpoint signaling, and cytokine environments, and intrinsic cellular changes including metabolic dysregulation, epigenetic remodeling, and transcriptional reprogramming. The review traces the historical milestones in the field and surveys the relevance of T cell exhaustion across cancer, chronic infections, and autoimmune diseases. Emerging therapeutic strategies discussed include immune checkpoint inhibition, cytokine-based approaches, metabolic reprogramming, and cell-based therapies, all aimed at restoring functional immunity. The authors highlight persistent challenges in the field, including inter-individual variability, therapeutic resistance, adverse effects, and the difficulty of reliably measuring functional recovery and evaluating the long-term consequences of these interventions.
