items.citation: Yiran Wu, Yuchen Wu, Zhengyu Gao et al.. Revitalizing T cells: breakthroughs and challenges in overcoming T cell exhaustion. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its disruption contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the historical development of lipid metabolism research and emphasize its particular importance in immune cell biology, covering macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils across both innate and adaptive immune responses. The review details how lipid metabolites regulate immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation through diverse transport and signaling pathways, as well as through multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational control. It further explores how dysregulated lipid metabolism underlies conditions including autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review surveys current and emerging therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, the status of relevant clinical trials, and the potential of lipid-derived biomarkers for future clinical application.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: EULAR recommendations for the management of rheumatoid arthritis with synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs: 2025 update
items.summary: These updated recommendations address the management of rheumatoid arthritis using synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs, incorporating the most recent clinical evidence. An international task force conducted systematic literature reviews on the safety and efficacy of these agents, then used a structured voting process to reach consensus on overarching principles and specific recommendations. The resulting guidance consolidates previous recommendations into a smaller set, covering conventional synthetic agents such as methotrexate as initial preferred therapy ideally combined with short-term glucocorticoids, with escalation to a biologic agent upon inadequate response. Janus kinase inhibitors remain an option after careful individual risk assessment for cardiovascular events, malignancies, and thromboembolic complications. The recommendations also address treat-to-target strategies, sequencing of therapies upon treatment failure, and cautious tapering in sustained remission, noting that complete cessation frequently leads to disease flare.
items.citation: Josef S. Smolen, Christopher J. Edwards, Victoria Konzett et al.. EULAR recommendations for the management of rheumatoid arthritis with synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs: 2025 update. Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases. 2026

items.title: Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies
items.summary: This narrative review examined the application of digital twin technology in oncology, exploring how virtual patient replicas can transform cancer care delivery. The authors conducted a comprehensive literature search across major scientific databases, synthesising evidence on digital twin development, validation, and clinical application across cancer types. Digital twins were found to have demonstrated utility across the full cancer care continuum, including precision treatment selection, radiotherapy optimisation, drug development, immuno-oncology modelling, surgical planning, and survivorship care. Integration of multi-omics data, imaging biomarkers, and artificial intelligence enables dynamic simulation of tumour behaviour and treatment response prediction. Despite this promise, the review identified persistent challenges in data integration, model validation, computational scalability, and ethical governance, concluding that interdisciplinary collaboration and regulatory framework development are essential prerequisites for widespread clinical implementation.
items.citation: David B. Olawade, Emmanuel O. Oisakede, Oluwakemi Jumoke Bello et al.. Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies. Critical Reviews in Oncology/Hematology. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the gut microbiome's causal role in colorectal cancer development and treatment response, synthesizing molecular, ecological, and translational evidence across immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes. The authors analyze how dysbiotic microbial communities, characterized by enrichment of specific pathobionts and depletion of protective short-chain-fatty-acid-producing bacteria, drive carcinogenesis through mechanisms including genotoxin-induced DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation. Bacterial-derived genotoxins such as colibactin and cytolethal distending toxin produce defined mutational signatures now traceable in human cancer genomes, providing direct mechanistic links between microbial exposures and tumor initiation. The review reconceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which coordinated microbial consortia sustain immune evasion, metabolic reprogramming, and tumor growth. It concludes by highlighting emerging microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies, including bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation, repositioning the microbiome as an actionable determinant of both cancer pathogenesis and treatment outcomes.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.
items.summary: This study investigated whether a low-cost, noninvasive electronic nose could reliably detect SARS-CoV-2 infection as an alternative to conventional diagnostic methods that are often uncomfortable, slow, or inaccurate in early infection. The researchers developed a device using an array of commercially available metal-oxide gas sensors to analyze volatile organic compounds present in both saliva and exhaled breath, which shift in composition in response to viral infection. Several machine learning algorithms were applied to classify samples as infected or non-infected, and their discriminatory performance was compared across both sample types. The best-performing algorithm achieved area-under-the-curve values approaching approximately 0.9 for exhaled breath and somewhat lower for saliva, demonstrating meaningful discriminatory power in a real-world hospital cohort. The authors conclude that this approach represents a feasible proof-of-concept for rapid, point-of-care SARS-CoV-2 screening using inexpensive, noninvasive hardware.
items.venue: The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Fialkovitz G, Sousa PL, Hidifira AM et al.. Noninvasive SARS-CoV-2 detection using a low-cost electronic nose.. The Brazilian journal of infectious diseases : an official publication of the Brazilian Society of Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.
items.summary: This study investigated the fungal microbiome (mycobiome) in patients with common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), a condition known to involve bacterial dysbiosis but whose fungal component had not been well characterized. Researchers conducted a cross-sectional study comparing adults with CVID—subdivided into those with immune dysregulation and those with only infectious manifestations—against healthy controls, analyzing saliva, sputum, and stool samples using amplicon sequencing and machine learning approaches. Mycobiome composition differed significantly between CVID patients and controls across all three body compartments, with reduced fungal richness and evenness particularly in respiratory and oral samples. A consistent "Candida-skewed" pattern was identified in both CVID subtypes, characterized by enrichment of opportunistic yeasts such as Candida albicans and depletion of commensal or environmental fungi. Fungal signatures showed strong discriminatory power in distinguishing CVID from healthy controls, supporting their potential as non-invasive biomarkers and implicating C. albicans as a possible pathobiont in this immunodeficiency.
items.citation: Cabanero-Navalon MD, Carda-Diéguez M, Mira A et al.. Multiniche mycobiome profiling identifies distinctive fungal dysbiosis in common variable immunodeficiency.. Frontiers in immunology. 2026

items.title: Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.
items.summary: This study investigated whether tuberculosis could be diagnosed non-invasively using salivary exosomes as a source of *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* DNA, with a particular focus on paucibacillary cases where conventional sputum smear microscopy fails. The researchers developed a method called ExoNA-based saliva tuberculosis diagnostic, which integrates salivary exosome enrichment, nucleic acid extraction, and dual-target quantitative PCR melting curve analysis into a streamlined workflow. In a pilot clinical study enrolling adults with suspected pulmonary tuberculosis—the majority of whom had negative smear microscopy results—the assay demonstrated moderate-to-good sensitivity and specificity against both standard and extended microbiological reference standards, while maintaining diagnostic performance in the smear-negative subgroup. The method also substantially reduced turnaround time compared to conventional approaches and achieved a low limit of detection. The authors conclude that this non-invasive, rapid salivary assay shows promise for tuberculosis diagnosis, though multicenter validation in more diverse populations is needed before clinical implementation.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Ma J, Zheng X, He Y et al.. Salivary exosomal <i>Mycobacterium tuberculosis</i> DNA enables sensitive detection of paucibacillary tuberculosis: a molecular diagnosis study.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies
items.summary: This review examines the molecular and cellular mechanisms underlying inflammatory bone loss in periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease in which dysbiotic oral biofilms drive progressive destruction of the periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone. The authors focus on osteoclastogenesis and the signaling pathways that govern osteoclast differentiation and activation, including the MAPK, JAK/STAT, NF-κB, and PI3K/Akt pathways, as well as key transcriptional regulators such as NFATc1, c-Fos, and c-Src. By mapping how these pathways orchestrate the inflammatory response and bone resorption, the review situates periodontitis within the broader context of osteolytic conditions such as osteoporosis and rheumatoid arthritis. The authors survey current and emerging therapeutic strategies—spanning small molecules, biologics, and natural compounds—that target these molecular pathways to limit tissue destruction. They conclude that while mechanistic insights have revealed promising therapeutic targets, translating this understanding into effective clinical interventions remains an ongoing challenge requiring continued research.
authors: Jeroen J. J. P. van den Beucken
items.citation: Rafael Scaf de Molon, Rolando Vernal, Gabriela Ezequiel Oliveira et al.. Inflammatory bone loss and signaling pathways in periodontitis: mechanistic insights and emerging therapeutic strategies. Bone Research. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the gut microbiome's causal role in colorectal cancer development, progression, and treatment response, moving beyond traditional genetic and environmental explanations. The authors synthesize molecular, ecological, and translational evidence showing how dysbiotic microbial communities reprogram immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine systems within the tumor microenvironment. Key pathobionts, including certain strains of Fusobacterium nucleatum, toxin-producing Escherichia coli, and enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis, drive carcinogenesis through DNA damage via genotoxins such as colibactin, epithelial barrier disruption, metabolic rewiring, and chronic inflammation, while the concurrent loss of short-chain fatty acid–producing commensals removes protective counter-signals. The review frames colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which coordinated microbial consortia sustain immune evasion and tumor growth, with defined mutational signatures in human cancer genomes now directly linking specific bacterial metabolites to oncogenesis. Building on this framework, the authors highlight emerging therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered live biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—as avenues for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as mediators of intercellular communication, covering their biogenesis, molecular composition, and therapeutic potential across human disease. The authors survey the major EV classes, including exosomes, microvesicles, apoptotic bodies, and oncosomes, and discuss advances in their isolation, characterization, and omics-based profiling. Particular attention is given to EV roles in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, as well as the clinical promise of mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs in regenerative medicine and the modulation of EV signaling through photobiomodulation. The review also addresses critical translational obstacles including standardization, scalability, and the regulatory frameworks imposed by bodies such as the FDA and EMA that govern exosome-based therapeutics. The authors argue that integrating mechanistic understanding with clinical application positions EVs as transformative tools for next-generation diagnostics and precision therapeutics.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review paper examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their applications in laboratory medicine. The authors conducted a comprehensive review covering innovations in chip design and fabrication techniques, including three-dimensional printing and modular flexible substrates, as well as the integration of advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. The review finds that these combined advances have broadened clinical applicability across cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also address ongoing challenges related to manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration, and outline future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration
items.summary: This review examines smart wearable and implantable biosensors designed for continuous, real-time monitoring of biophysical and biochemical signals in the context of personalized and preventive healthcare. The paper surveys advances in flexible, stretchable, and biocompatible materials that enable long-term comfort and seamless integration with the body, as well as multimodal sensing approaches that improve measurement robustness. The integration of artificial intelligence is discussed as a means of enhancing signal processing and generating predictive health insights from the collected data. The review identifies persistent challenges including motion artifacts, energy autonomy, data privacy, and the clinical interpretability of sensor outputs. Overall, it synthesizes current knowledge across materials science, device architecture, and AI-assisted strategies to outline the state and trajectory of the field.
items.citation: Thirumalaisamy Suryaprabha, Chunghyeon Choi, Yanfang Wu et al.. Smart wearable and implantable biosensors for continuous health monitoring: materials, biocompatibility, and AI integration. npj Flexible Electronics. 2026

items.title: Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential
items.summary: This review examines the current state of electrochemical biosensors designed to detect protein biomarkers in blood for cancer diagnosis and prognosis, with a particular focus on liquid biopsy approaches that offer non-invasive alternatives to traditional screening. The authors critically survey general electrochemical detection strategies for serum proteins alongside recent advances in biosensors targeting cancer-related circulating proteins, noting that many approaches have achieved impressive analytical sensitivity. The review identifies significant barriers preventing these technologies from reaching clinical use, including interference from complex biological sample matrices, insufficient validation with real patient samples, challenges in accounting for variability between patients, and practical hurdles such as manufacturing scalability, cost, and sensor stability. Particular emphasis is placed on the analytical and clinical validation steps necessary to bridge the gap between laboratory demonstrations and deployable diagnostic tools. The authors frame these discussions within the broader public health need for accessible, accurate early cancer detection methods.
items.citation: Michael E. J. Lopez Mujica, Elena E. Ferapontova. Electrochemical Biosensors for Cancer Diagnosis and Prognosis Using Protein Biomarkers: Current Trends, Advances, and Clinical Translation Potential. Sensors. 2026

items.title: Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards
items.summary: This paper investigates whether a unified large multimodal model capable of both visual understanding and image generation can improve itself autonomously without relying on human annotations, preference labels, or external reward models. The proposed self-evolving framework assigns three internal roles to the model: a Proposer that generates visual questions, a Solver that answers and evaluates them, and a Generator that synthesizes images, with training driven entirely by self-derived consistency signals. To stabilize learning, the authors introduce a token-level prediction uncertainty measure called Solver Token Entropy as a continuous difficulty signal, and they design a multi-scale internal evaluation scheme combining question-answer fidelity with cycle-consistent captioning for image generation assessment. The framework is architecture-agnostic, having been applied to diffusion-based, rectified-flow, and autoregressive backbone models using only each model's native interface. Across multiple understanding benchmarks the method consistently outperforms the corresponding base models, demonstrating that unified multimodal models can self-improve both visual understanding and image generation through purely internal consistency rewards.
items.citation: Ritesh Thawkar, Shravan Venkatraman, Omkar Thawakar et al.. Ask, Solve, Generate: Self-Evolving Unified Multimodal Understanding and Generation via Self-Consistency Rewards. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models
items.summary: This paper investigates a failure mode in self-evolving large multimodal models called visual under-conditioning, where models rely on language priors rather than image content during generation due to reward schemes that optimize answer agreement without ensuring proper attention to visual tokens. To address this, the authors propose VISE, a purely unsupervised self-evolving framework that introduces two complementary invariance-based rewards: a geometric invariance reward enforcing spatial consistency under known transformations, and a semantic invariance reward that penalizes generation agnostic to visual evidence by requiring the model to recognize when predicted regions are perturbed. VISE operates within a single model without specialist roles, external reward models, or labeled data, training instead on raw unlabeled images. Evaluated across a broad set of benchmarks, the framework demonstrates substantial improvements in image captioning and visual question answering performance, while also reducing object hallucination, and generalizes across multiple model families and scales.
items.citation: Shravan Venkatraman, Ritesh Thawkar, Omkar Thawakar et al.. Paying More Attention to Visual Tokens in Self-Evolving Large Multimodal Models. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks
items.summary: This paper investigates a limitation of standard softmax-based multihead attention in vision transformers, where noisy attention patterns can dilute relevant features and reduce model performance. The authors propose Denoising Attention (DnA), a method that uses positive queries to identify relevant image features and negative queries to capture closely related but irrelevant features, then projects these interactions into two distinct subspaces with larger principal angles to improve discriminability. By enforcing greater separation between the relevant and irrelevant feature subspaces, DnA suppresses attention noise and sharpens the model's focus on task-relevant information. The approach is evaluated across image classification, video understanding, and video language model tasks, demonstrating consistent performance improvements over baselines using a standard vision transformer backbone. Empirical analyses are provided to justify the dual-subspace design and validate the denoising effect of the proposed method.
items.citation: Ron Campos, Subhajit Maity, Xin Li et al.. DnA: Denoising Attention for Visual Tasks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem
items.summary: This paper investigates the behavior of massless Dirac fermions coupled to non-Hermitian gauge fields arising from spatially modulated anti-Hermitian mass-like terms in a Lorentz-symmetric non-Hermitian Dirac theory. When such non-Hermitian gauge potentials are configured to produce non-Hermitian magnetic fields threading a finite flux through a planar system, the authors find that spatially localized zero-energy eigenmodes emerge in the spectrum. Crucially, these zero modes are exclusively either right- or left-handed, representing a non-Hermitian generalization of the Atiyah-Singer index theorem. The predictions are numerically confirmed through microscopic tight-binding realizations on graphene's honeycomb lattice, with potential experimental platforms proposed. The authors argue that the resulting non-Hermitian flat bands of chiral zero modes open pathways toward realizing non-Hermitian magnetic catalysis, fractional topological phases, and chiral anomaly phenomena.
items.citation: Christopher A. Leong, Bitan Roy. Dirac fermions in non-Hermitian magnetic fields: Zero modes and index theorem. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs
items.summary: This paper investigates whether large language models can be improved through reinforcement learning on optimization tasks that lack ground-truth solutions, a setting where standard reward-based training approaches break down. The authors introduce RiVER, a framework that uses deterministic execution feedback as continuous-valued rewards and applies group-relative reinforcement learning to score-based heuristic contest tasks. They identify two failure modes in naïve application of this approach—scale dominance from uncalibrated score magnitudes and frequency dominance from overrepresentation of suboptimal solutions—and address both through instance-wise reward calibration that emphasizes top-ranked solutions while preserving bounded feedback for other valid outputs. Training on heuristic programming contest tasks without any ground-truth solutions, RiVER substantially improves model performance not only on score-based optimization benchmarks but also on exact-solution coding benchmarks, whereas baselines using raw execution scores fail to generalize beyond the training task type. These findings suggest that properly calibrated score-based optimization environments can serve as effective and broadly transferable training grounds for general coding ability in language models.
items.citation: Yingyu Lin, Qiyue Gao, Nikki Lijing Kuang et al.. Reinforcement Learning without Ground-Truth Solutions can Improve LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Hidden-ordered Dirac fermions
items.summary: This paper proposes a theoretical extension of Lorentz-symmetric Dirac theory by adding a second masslike anticommuting Dirac operator to the standard Hamiltonian, creating a Hermitian framework that preserves the linear energy-momentum relationship in arbitrary spatial dimensions. The resulting quasiparticle excitations, termed hidden-ordered Dirac fermions, are symmetry protected and exhibit responses analogous to conventional Dirac systems but with a renormalized Fermi velocity arising from the hidden ordering. A key finding is that this hidden ordering generally shifts quantum phase transitions into insulating states toward stronger coupling in dimensions greater than one, though whether the hidden order persists or vanishes near an itinerant quantum critical point depends on the internal algebra between the insulating order parameter and the masslike Dirac operator. The author constructs explicit lattice models realizing these hidden-ordered massless Dirac fermions and identifies potential numerical and experimental platforms for testing the theoretical predictions.
items.citation: Bitan Roy. Hidden-ordered Dirac fermions. arXiv. 2026

items.venue: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing that can operate outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. They surveyed extraction strategies including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic approaches, emphasizing designs that are simplified and equipment-free, and reviewed amplification methods such as PCR, NASBA, RPA, and LAMP as adapted for portable and low-power conditions. For detection, the review spans established colorimetric and fluorescent assays used in lateral flow platforms through to advanced biosensors leveraging CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles. The authors conclude that current point-of-care nucleic acid platforms offer increasingly sensitive, selective, and economically viable solutions for infectious disease diagnosis beyond centralized facilities.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026
