items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine and clinical diagnostics. The authors conducted a comprehensive survey of innovations in chip design and fabrication—including three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates—alongside progress in integrating advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the development of smart diagnostic platforms offering self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. These combined technologies have expanded applications across cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The review also identifies ongoing challenges in manufacturing scalability, clinical validation, and system integration that must be addressed before next-generation microfluidic platforms can be broadly translated into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies
items.summary: Severe community-acquired pneumonia remains a significant public health burden due to its frequency, complications, and treatment costs. The paper reviews current understanding and ongoing controversies in the management of this condition, including the challenge of selecting appropriate empirical antibiotic therapy in the context of resistant pathogens and the emerging but incompletely evaluated role of rapid molecular diagnostic techniques. Treatment duration is trending shorter and may be guided by clinical markers or biomarkers, while the potential for immunomodulatory therapy has gained attention given that the condition involves dysregulated systemic inflammation. Recent large randomized trials of corticosteroids have produced conflicting results, leaving unresolved which patient subgroups — potentially those with marked inflammatory responses but not those with influenza — might benefit, and some macrolides are also being explored for immunomodulatory properties. The review concludes that supportive care, especially tailored respiratory support, remains a cornerstone of management and must be individualized to disease severity.
items.citation: Pierre-François Dequin, A Torres, M. Bassetti et al.. Severe community-acquired pneumonia: current concepts and controversies. Intensive Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed and evaluated a novel electrochemical diagnostic platform that combines isothermal nucleic acid amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for identifying viral pathogens. The system detects amplification-associated proton release using redox probing of a pH-sensing compound, offering an alternative to optical detection methods that can impede conventional isothermal approaches like LAMP. The platform was tested against multiple respiratory viruses including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV A using RNA purified from clinical samples, demonstrating high concordance with RT-qPCR as the reference standard. An extraction-free proof-of-concept workflow was also validated using equine nasal swab samples for a veterinary pathogen, with correct classification of all tested samples. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is feasible across diverse sample types and pathogens, though they acknowledge that broader point-of-care validation in additional matrices and prospective cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. They surveyed extraction strategies including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic approaches, emphasizing designs that remain simple, portable, and equipment-free. Amplification methods reviewed include both thermal cycling approaches such as PCR and isothermal alternatives such as LAMP, RPA, and NASBA, all of which have been adapted for low-power and portable conditions. On the detection side, the review covers a spectrum from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensor platforms exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three bacterial pathogens — *Streptococcus pneumoniae*, *Streptococcus agalactiae*, and *Streptococcus pyogenes* — directly from cerebrospinal fluid. The approach was designed to automate the entire workflow from sample input to result output, eliminating the need for bacterial culture. The assay demonstrated very low detection limits and no cross-reactivity with other common pathogens, and it reduced total testing time compared to manual extraction paired with conventional real-time PCR. When evaluated on clinical cerebrospinal fluid and non-cerebrospinal fluid samples, the point-of-care method identified additional positive cases that conventional real-time PCR had missed. The authors conclude that this integrated system offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally streamlined approach to diagnosing bacterial meningitis-related pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examined isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostics. The authors surveyed the principles, detection methods, and applications of the most widely used techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Unlike PCR, these methods operate at a constant temperature and do not require thermal cycling equipment, making them particularly suited for resource-limited settings that demand rapid, affordable, and simple nucleic acid detection. The review addresses a gap in the existing literature by comprehensively consolidating reaction mechanisms, product detection strategies, and clinical diagnostic applications in a single work. The authors conclude that isothermal amplification techniques offer significant promise for broadening access to molecular diagnostics across research and clinical practice, and they discuss future perspectives for the field.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, motivated by the inadequacies of conventional culture-based methods and the growing threat of antimicrobial resistance. The authors conduct a structured comparative analysis of major platforms, including PCR and its variants, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capability. The review also critically assesses how artificial intelligence and machine learning are being integrated into molecular diagnostic workflows to support antimicrobial resistance prediction and clinical decision-making. Additionally, the translational feasibility of these technologies for point-of-care deployment is considered, accounting for clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude by outlining an integrated perspective on current molecular strategies and identifying key challenges and future directions for advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This study developed a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a portable, fully automated point-of-care testing platform for detecting three key meningitis pathogens in cerebrospinal fluid. The assay performs nested amplification within a closed system using conventional primers and probes, reducing contamination risk and operational complexity, and was evaluated both analytically and clinically using simulated and real cerebrospinal fluid samples. The method demonstrated very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a broad panel of non-target microorganisms, and delivered results in under two hours. Clinical testing against conventional quantitative PCR revealed near-perfect agreement, with the new assay additionally detecting more cases of one fungal pathogen, suggesting enhanced sensitivity under real-world conditions. The authors conclude that the platform offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally straightforward approach for infectious meningitis diagnostics, though they note that validation in larger patient cohorts is needed.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey
items.summary: This survey examines the intersection of foundation models and computational healthcare through a data-centric lens, focusing on how data quality, scale, and characterization drive advances in clinical AI. The authors review a broad range of data-centric approaches spanning model pre-training through inference, addressing longstanding challenges in healthcare such as data scarcity, annotation burden, patient privacy, and ethical considerations. The survey also explores AI security, model assessment, and alignment with human values as critical dimensions of deploying foundation models in clinical settings. The authors conclude with an outlook on how foundation model-based analytics can improve patient outcomes and clinical workflows as healthcare AI continues to evolve.
items.citation: Yunkun Zhang, Jin Gao, Zheling Tan et al.. Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey. ACM Computing Surveys. 2026

items.title: Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions
items.citation: Lorenzo Galluzzi, Oliver Kepp, Laurence Zitvogel et al.. Cancer cell death: Cell-autonomous and immunogenic dimensions. Cancer Cell. 2025

items.title: Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
items.summary: This review paper examines acute pancreatitis, a severe inflammatory condition whose global incidence is rising with notable regional variation in both frequency and etiology, including gallstone disease, hypertriglyceridemia, and alcohol abuse. The authors synthesize current knowledge on disease definition and classification, as well as advances in clinical scoring systems, biomarkers, and artificial intelligence-based predictive models intended to support early risk stratification and clinical decision-making. The review also details the multilevel pathophysiological mechanisms underlying the condition, encompassing aberrant enzymatic activation, calcium overload, impaired autophagy, inflammatory cascades, and various modes of pancreatic acinar cell death. From a therapeutic standpoint, the paper covers early- and late-stage management strategies, adjunctive approaches drawn from traditional Chinese medicine, and individualized treatment considerations for special patient populations. Emerging interventions such as nanotechnology and extracellular vesicle-based therapies are additionally highlighted as promising future directions for research and clinical innovation.
items.citation: Qian Hu, Yue Hu, Chunlu Tan et al.. Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation
items.summary: This review examines the current landscape of biosensing technologies designed to detect and analyze extracellular vesicles, which carry molecular cargo that reflects the biological state of their parent cells and thus hold strong potential as noninvasive diagnostic biomarkers. The authors survey a range of detection modalities—including surface plasmon resonance, electrochemical, fluorescence, and magnetic systems—evaluating how each has advanced sensitivity and specificity for EV analysis. The review also addresses efforts to standardize EV research through established guidelines and analytical validation frameworks, and explores how multiplexed profiling at the single-vesicle level combined with machine learning can improve diagnostic accuracy and account for biological heterogeneity. Clinical applications across cancer, neurological, cardiometabolic, and infectious diseases are discussed to illustrate translational progress. The authors conclude by identifying outstanding challenges in manufacturing, quality control, and point-of-care implementation, and propose a unified framework intended to integrate EV biosensing into precision diagnostics and personalized medicine.
items.citation: Seong-Jun Choi, Jaewon Choi, Jin Kim et al.. Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation. Molecules. 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This qualitative study explored perceptions of integrating a point-of-care triage test measuring interferon-gamma-induced protein 10 (IP-10), a chemokine correlated with HIV viral load, into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique. Researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV undergoing IP-10 testing, healthcare providers, and HIV programme stakeholders across different health system levels, nested within a clinical evaluation at two primary healthcare facilities. Participants widely valued viral load monitoring but described significant barriers to access, including laboratory delays and health system constraints. The IP-10 triage test was broadly acceptable, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and enable timely clinical decision-making by identifying those needing confirmatory viral load testing. However, concerns about test accuracy and the continued necessity of confirmatory testing were raised, with successful implementation deemed dependent on adequate training, workflow integration, quality assurance, and clear patient communication.
items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This qualitative study investigated the implementation bottlenecks of near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV in Tanzania, from the perspective of healthcare workers. The researchers conducted clinic observations and in-depth interviews with healthcare workers at multiple time points across Tanzanian intervention sites participating in a larger cluster-randomised controlled trial, using a thematic framework analysis guided by the Measurement Instrument for Determinants of Innovations. The findings revealed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround time and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both healthcare workers and patients, and was further supported by compatibility with existing clinical practices, teamwork, and management commitment. However, significant barriers were identified, including patients waiting extended periods for results, scarcity of physical resources such as rooms and electricity, staff shortages, and delays caused by sample transport to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full potential of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires targeted investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling procedures, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe. Participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years who were in the peripartum period and on tenofovir-based antiretroviral therapy were enrolled into a randomized controlled and observational cohort study, with point-of-care viral load testing and urine tenofovir detection used alongside validated mental health screening tools. The researchers found high rates of viral suppression at baseline, with the large majority of participants achieving undetectable viral loads, and suppression was strongly associated with detectable tenofovir in urine and higher self-reported adherence. Socioeconomic factors including food security, financial security, and marital status were also significantly associated with viral suppression. Mental health outcomes were generally positive, with most participants showing minimal depression and anxiety, though moderate HIV-related stigma was common among over half the cohort.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Feasibility and reliability of decentralized HIV-1 viral load monitoring on self-sampled blood.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether home-based blood self-sampling using a capillary collection device could reliably support HIV-1 viral load monitoring as an alternative to clinic-based testing. Participants recruited at an outpatient clinic collected their own blood samples at home, which were then tested for HIV RNA and compared against conventional plasma samples from the same individuals, with additional stored low-level viremic samples also assessed. While overall correlation between self-sampled and conventionally sampled results was acceptable, a substantial proportion of self-collected samples had insufficient plasma volume for analysis, and low-level viremia was missed in several cases where conventional testing detected detectable virus. User-friendliness was rated positively by only around half of participants, and overall return rates were modest. The authors conclude that meaningful technical and procedural improvements are needed before this approach can be considered reliable for routine clinical use.
items.citation: Mendes de Leon KF, Jurriaans S, Klungers RD et al.. Feasibility and reliability of decentralized HIV-1 viral load monitoring on self-sampled blood.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Herpes Simplex Virus Type 2 Screening in Persons with and Without HIV: Evidence, Challenges, and Future Directions
items.summary: This review examined the current evidence and guidelines surrounding serologic screening for herpes simplex virus type 2, particularly in the context of HIV prevention and care. The authors synthesized existing guidance, test performance data, available therapeutics, and psychosocial considerations to evaluate the role of screening across different populations. International guidelines recommend against routine population-level serologic screening in asymptomatic individuals, citing poor test specificity, lack of curative or highly potent antiviral options, absence of demonstrated population-level benefit, and potential psychosocial harms from diagnosis. Instead, evidence supports targeted screening in defined clinical contexts, such as among people living with HIV, sex partners of infected individuals, certain pregnant women, and those with recurrent or atypical symptoms, where results can directly guide management. The authors conclude that advances in diagnostics, novel antivirals, and therapeutic or curative vaccines may eventually shift the risk-benefit balance to justify broader routine screening, but such a shift requires first demonstrating measurable reductions in disease burden or transmission at the population level.
items.citation: Laith J. Abu-Raddad, Hiam Chemaitelly, Anna Wald et al.. Herpes Simplex Virus Type 2 Screening in Persons with and Without HIV: Evidence, Challenges, and Future Directions. Current HIV/AIDS Reports. 2026

items.title: Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.
items.summary: This study examined the prevalence of sexually transmitted infections among women who inject drugs and tested whether point-of-care STI testing could be feasibly and acceptably implemented at a mobile clinic serving this population in Seattle. Researchers enrolled women with a history of injecting drugs and offered point-of-care testing for multiple STIs at baseline and follow-up visits, alongside surveys assessing participant and provider experiences. They found notably high rates of trichomonas positivity, with STI positivity overall associated with having multiple sexual partners and engaging in transactional sex. Same-day treatment was successfully delivered in the vast majority of positive cases, and most participants preferred point-of-care testing over traditional methods, though providers reported logistical challenges and difficulties navigating competing participant priorities. The authors conclude that low-barrier, mobile point-of-care STI testing is both acceptable and feasible for this population and facilitates timely treatment.
items.citation: Fessler AG, Corcorran MA, Violette LR et al.. Correlates of Sexually Transmitted Infection (STI) Positivity and Implementation of Point-of-Care STI Testing at a Mobile Clinic for Women Who Inject Drugs in Seattle, WA.. Sexually transmitted diseases. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals tested at these sites, using statistical tests to explore associations between positive results and demographic characteristics or symptom presence. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals who tested positive for either infection were referred for treatment within one day of diagnosis. No statistically significant associations were found between test positivity and demographic factors or symptoms, though the small sample size limited these analyses. The authors conclude that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing can facilitate rapid diagnosis and timely treatment referral in outreach settings, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion rates.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper examined the challenges and opportunities involved in transitioning from syndromic management to diagnostic-led management of sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings, with a focus on sub-Saharan Africa. An expert meeting brought together specialists in diagnostics, implementation research, policy, regulatory affairs, and clinical practice to identify priorities and strategies for introducing and scaling up point-of-care STI testing. The group analyzed barriers and enablers at multiple levels, ranging from individual patient uptake to health service delivery, national policy, and global regulatory frameworks. Recommendations spanned improving patient knowledge about STIs, optimizing clinical workflows, enabling testing outside traditional facilities, updating treatment guidelines, strengthening laboratory quality assurance, harmonizing regulatory requirements across regions, and expanding manufacturing capacity. The authors conclude that coordinated action across all these levels is necessary to achieve equitable access to quality STI diagnostics, which they argue will reduce overtreatment, undertreatment, antimicrobial resistance, and improve overall STI control.
items.citation: Manguro GO, Ray J, Schocken C et al.. From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.
items.summary: This study examined the prospective acceptability of the genital inflammation test (GIFT), a point-of-care screening tool designed to detect genital inflammation associated with sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in asymptomatic women. Researchers conducted in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with women, healthcare professionals, and decision-makers across South Africa and Zimbabwe, using a theoretical framework of acceptability to guide thematic analysis. Women expressed strong enthusiasm for sexual health diagnostics generally, but their acceptance of GIFT specifically declined once they understood it functions as a preliminary screen requiring follow-up confirmatory testing, given the cost and scarcity of such tests in these settings. Healthcare professionals and decision-makers similarly questioned the practical utility of GIFT within resource-constrained health systems, while also acknowledging its potential to catalyze the development of cheaper, infrastructure-light diagnostics. The findings highlight that acceptability of novel biomedical tools is fundamentally shaped by health system context, pointing to the need for affordable follow-on diagnostics, clear communication strategies, and co-designed care pathways.
items.citation: Karumazondo J, Bernays S, Naidoo J et al.. Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.. Global public health. 2026

items.title: Implementing STI point-of-care testing using a whole-system programme model: the ProSPiRO implementation research protocol.
items.summary: This protocol paper describes a planned implementation research study examining how to integrate sexually transmitted infection point-of-care testing into diverse health system settings. The researchers developed a whole-system programme model delivered through stakeholder-led workshops that can be locally adapted across multiple countries, with participating sites spanning sexual and reproductive health services, antenatal care, and general practice. Sites will implement point-of-care tests for selected pathogens including chlamydia, gonorrhoea, trichomonas, HIV, and syphilis over one year, with evaluation using a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative clinical outcomes, economic analysis, and qualitative data from workshops, focus groups, and interviews. The study aims to assess acceptability, feasibility, and suitability of the programme model, with primary outcomes focused on same-day diagnosis and treatment, service capacity, and cost consequences relative to usual care. The protocol is designed to identify which components of the model work, for whom, and under what circumstances, with the goal of generating policy-relevant evidence to support equitable and sustainable scale-up of point-of-care testing.
items.citation: Fuller SS, Padovese V, Sadiq ST et al.. Implementing STI point-of-care testing using a whole-system programme model: the ProSPiRO implementation research protocol.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026
